Now, millennia later, the temple stands proud, but silent. The walls and windows remain steadfast against the onslaught of time. A faint trace of priestly magic keeps the more monstrous elements from creeping inside. It is a sanctuary against the wild frontiers of Ashenfall, and therefore the home base of the current expedition team.

It is said that the windmill, northwest of the temple, is haunted. The timber creeks ominously even though the sails no longer turn. At night, sinister creatures patrol its exterior, waiting for any opportunity to strike.
keysToEntries.BramblemeadValley:
Bramblemead valley is home to the village of its namesake, a once tranquil location bustling with life. It was founded in the first age by the god Guthix and his followers, and converted to the worship of Saradomin sometime in the third age.

The Keep of Blue Flames, was built in the popular style of the Saradominist faithful. The ordered shape a symbol of the god of order’s will. Powdered metals were cast into the braziers, so that the flames would burn in the hue favoured by Saradomin. Here the lord of the region would watch over the village and surrounding areas, under the careful guidance of the high priest of the church.

To the northwest stands a symbol of mankind’s thirst for knowledge, a great guild dedicated to uncovering the secrets of the runes that had been first discovered on Ashenfall by Guthix himself. Here, powerful wizards learned spells and enchantments, pushing the boundaries of mankind’s knowledge.

Ashenfall is rife with danger and the need for heroes has always been strong. A guild was created, to the east of Brynmoor, where heroes and champions would gather together to seek out the deadliest monsters and slay them where they stood.

Once this valley was the hub of human activity, but now it sits silent, and overrun with monsters. Goblins spread across the region claiming it in the name of their new god. Rats swarm through the ruins with sharp and deadly fangs. Soil that was once fertile farmland, is now soaked to the roots in blood.
keysToEntries.Swamp:
When war broke out between humans and garou, it was the swamps that formed a buffer between the two factions. The muddy water was difficult to fight in and neither side enjoyed the idea of drowning on the battlefield.

Now, the swap retains the memories of that conflict, swallowing down the remnants of that battle into its murky depths.

Yet, among all the ruin and the decay, the swamp is rife with ingredients for crafting. Herbs grow between the weeds and the caves that run along the swamp’s edges are filled with ore.

Still, one must be ever careful. Goblins roam these regions, hunters keeping an eye out for northern invaders. The garou watch from Ghornfell, ready for any foolish trespasser that might enter their domain.

And in the darkness of night, an ancient horror walks the region, offering only death’s embrace to any it finds.
keysToEntries.GhornfellPlains:
The anima of Ghornfell is less table than that of Brynmoor, and the anima twist’s effects are more pronounced. Plants take on the hues of dragonfire, making the entire region dance like an inferno when the summer wind blows.

Towering in the distance is a great tower, shattered now yet still imbued with ancient dragonkin magic. A terrible roaring can be heard from this structure and a shadow passes over it when General Velgar returns. The dragon makes his lair in the remains of a dragonkin’s hubris, a monument to an architect long since dead.

Garou make their homes across this region, sending out hunting parties to slaughter anything that crosses their path.

Atop the mesa, something glistens in the moonlight and a mournful sound carries on the wind. A sorrow that resonates in shimmering crystal.
keysToEntries.GhornfellHighlands:
Bones of ancient wyrms litter this region. Gargantuan dragons that are now mercifully dead. Forged by the mad science of the dragonkin Lacrussa, these titans could well have ended the god wars themselves. Fortunately, none still live in this region.

A great storm rages across the region, the result of desperate sorcery in a long drawn-out war. The thunder here is hateful and spiteful, striking any that walk the land, as if driven by the vengeful fury of its long-dead caster.

Watching over the Highlands stands a great monument to a fallen garou monarch. A king who challenged impossible foes. A lasting memory of the garou people, hewn out of stone.

The garou here are well trained and merciless, seeking nothing but the death of all who dare step foot in these sacred lands.
keysToEntries.ToxicSwamp:
Once these lands were fertile plains. Great fields of wheat and barely were sewn by the garou that made it their home. It was a resting place for a people that had been hunted for too long, a sanctuary at last.

But General Velgar put a stop to that dream. He was injured in battle with an arrogant god, flesh scarred in conflict, with wounds weeping. His blood, warped by the magic gifted to him by his queen, became a terrible toxin that cursed the region forever.

Now, poison mists float out of the soil. All the water is corrupted by this toxic blood. The air alone is dangerous, tinted green by the vengeful cruelty of Ghornfell’s master.

The garou here have grown resistant to the poison, though their minds are addled by constant exposure. They are more dangerous, more violent and more filled with hatred, with an entire settlement that seethes with rage.

Monstrous creatures roam this region. Ancient nightmares from another work prowl in the shadows, hunting any that might foolishly enter unprepared.
keysToEntries.Vaults:
Hidden across Ashenfall are the Vaults, the ruined remains of Dragonkin research. Each contains lost knowledge or riches, sealed away so that none but their jealous masters might possess them.

Paranoid and wary of intrusion by the “lesser races”, the Dragonkin of Ashenfall filled each of the vaults with an array of deadly traps and they created servitor guardians to watch over them.

Although time has whittled away at most of the mysteries, the cruel claws of entropy rending secrets to shreds, there still remain pieces of powerful knowledge that bold adventurers might be able to salvage.
keysToEntries.PoisonDragonfireShield:
Strange magics have transformed the head of a tyrant, into a mystical adornment to this powerful shield. It is still strong, still durable and resists the fury of dragons, but now the shield seethes with Velgar’s wrath.

The magic of the shield is twisted into a bitter poison. One that yearns to strike and rage against any in its path. In battle, it’s possible to turn this poison on one’s enemies, afflicting them with a dragon’s hate.

Sometimes, in the dark, when the night is silent, it’s as if the shield whispers angry curses into the darkness. As if the last vestiges of Velgar’s fury are carried on the wind as hateful poison.
keysToEntries.Adhesive: Tacky like your great aunt’s wardrobe, this sticky substance has a wide range of uses when it comes to crafting.
keysToEntries.Charcoal:
What’s better than a dead tree? A dead tree that has been burned to the point of carbonisation. No longer do you see unsightly green leaves, but now the wonderful blackness of charcoal. Highly praised by smiths. Way better than stupid old logs of wood.  

This journal entry was sponsored by the tree haters of Lumbridge, if you’re interested in joining their focus group, please speak to Tim near Bob’s Axes.
keysToEntries.UnfiredClayVessel: A sticky clay container in need of a kiln.
keysToEntries.UnfiredClayDecoration: This clay nicknack will look really nice on a shelf, once you’ve cooked it in the kiln. Otherwise it’ll just leave red stains everywhere.
keysToEntries.UnfiredClayMold: Once cooked, this will serve as a useful template for further crafting projects.
keysToEntries.ClayVessel: This vessel can be sealed to allow for the storage of potions.
keysToEntries.ClayDecoration: Much prettier now that it’s not leaving nasty red stains everywhere.
keysToEntries.ClayMold: This mould can be used in crafting.
keysToEntries.UnfiredClaySlab: A thick slab of clay, not very useful without being baked in a kiln.
keysToEntries.ClaySlab: A thick slab of clay. Used in construction projects.
keysToEntries.IronPickaxe:
Some metals tower over others, laughing at the weakness of their lesser cousins. Iron is sturdy, strong and the foundation of many a civilisation. The metal makes strong bridges to cross dangerous waters. It can be smithed into tools, and weapons, stronger than any before.

An iron pickaxe is one such tool. The blade of the axe is hard and precise, delivering massive force at the point of impact. Few ores and stones can resist this power, which is why dwarves have long since favoured the iron pickaxe.
keysToEntries.IronAxe:
While it might be cheaper to use bronze, there are some times when sheer power is necessary. For those times, a lumberjack need look no further than the glittering might of an iron axe’s blade. The mightiest of Ghornfell trees falls before its onslaught, and none in that region may stand against its might.

Iron was so powerful, that it created a problem in Brynmoor. The population craved the metal so desperately that they mined the region almost dry. This let to infighting, small civil skirmishes and the white knights of the Blue Flame Keep, needed to be brought in to keep the peace.

It is said that where iron goes, blood soon follows.
keysToEntries.ChefsHat: A hat worn by masters of the culinary arts. There are no special enchantments, no mysterious contraptions, indeed nothing that should make this hat stand out. Yet, it remains one of the most well regarded and respected pieces of clothing across the whole of Gielinor.
keysToEntries.Windstep:
The air can be wrapped as a cloak around you, making heights much less scary to face. Once, long ago, young druids would use windstep to hurl themselves from atop the highest mountains, descending to the ground below. Higher and higher they would climb, each trying to outdo the other. Until the inevitable finally intervened. 

Since that tragic day, it has been well known that there is a limit to the height at which Windstep might protect someone. They call it the splat-limit, because druids are very bad with names.
keysToEntries.InternalAlchemy:
The druid Gastritix often struggled with Bramblemead dishes. Something about the rustic cooking troubled her stomach and she would find herself in constant distress. Even bread, simple as it was, would leave her doubled over in agony and there were many times where she regretted that day's meal. So it was that she created a spell, which would speed up her digestion to near instant speed, granting her the energy needed to survive, but somehow preventing her normal stomach issues. 

Thereafter, with this spell in her repertoire, she became a noted local chef, finally able to enjoy a good meal.
keysToEntries.DetectOre:
It was a dwarf who asked Guthix for this spell. Ashenfall promised ores and metals that the dwarven smiths could only dream of, but they were often hidden away in the bowels of the earth. So it was that Guthix created this spell and taught it to the dwarven blacksmiths that had joined him on Ashenfall. 

The result was the creation of wonderous new weapons, armour and tools that pushed Bramblemead into a new era of prosperity.
keysToEntries.MagicalMending:
Skilled craftspeople are worth their weight in gold. The ability to repair broken equipment, and weaponry, was a core part of any craftsperson's trade. But, in the wilds of Ashenfall, such trades lacked the excitement and adventure of more whimsical professions. Hunters, warriors and druids, were the roles that everyone wanted, and so it was that fewer and fewer apprentices took up the hammer. As such, smiths would focus on creating new equipment, and tools, leaving repairs on the backburner for sometimes weeks at a time. 

Frustrated, the druids created a solution to this problem. Using a variation on the ritual that preserved wood, timber, and stone, the druids could turn back the clock on a piece of equipment, returning it to a healthier state. 

Over time, more people began to favour less deadly professions, and the spell would be used in only the most dire situations.
keysToEntries.SummonShelter:
Transforming dirt, stone and nearby twigs and leaves into canvas, this spell has saved many a Wild Knight from a cold night in the rain. A favourite among those engaged in long hunts in the Highlands, it has helped fight in the constant battle against fatigue. 

One Wild Knight, a loner called Ansel, would use this spell to grant him shelter as he wandered round the lands. He would journey to the highest peaks or the deepest crevices, using this spell to grant him a pinch of comfort in the least comfortable of regions. Sadly, it would offer no protection against poison, so when he rested in the Blightblood Swamp, he never awoke again.
keysToEntries.FireSpirit:
The aviansie believe in five types of spirit, those of the water, earth, light, storm and fire. The spirits of fire governed inspiration and creativity and were known among the aviansie people as the Gal'Son – the passionate. While perhaps not replicating the dancing spirits of legend, the fire spirit that this spell calls is no less passionate about being helpful. It loves little more than lighting your way and shielding you, as best it can, from the cold. 

In truth, this spell does not create a fire spirit, rather the runes attract nearby motes of elemental fire, bringing them together into a single entity. Each spirit is more like a colony of tiny lights, ready to banish the shadows in the darkest places.
keysToEntries.AxtralProjection:
There is nothing trees fear more than this spell. Crafted by their ancient enemy, the lumberjack, this spell has wiped out whole legions of arboreal sentinels. Guthix taught this spell to the people of Brynmoor, hoping that it would help them expand their settlement. It worked perhaps too well, and soon entire forests would find themselves reduced to stumps. Guthix was forced to implement restrictions to the spell, preventing any but the most senior druids from using it. 

Since his death this restriction has been lifted, and the trees tremble in the terror that is to come.
keysToEntries.Rocksplosion:
Kethren was a wizard who could rarely sit still. He found his thoughts flittering around the place like bees on too much sugar. As an apprentice, he was tasked with shadowing the Brynmoor miners, to see if there was anyway that magic could assist them in their tasks. He found it interminably dull. 

The slow clang of metal against metal. The painfully tedious focus on one piece of ore at a time. It was too much for him, and his solution was simple – what if the stone went boom? Imbued with chaotic instability, his spell would turn an ore cluster into an explosion waiting to happen. The result was that ore was gathered far more quickly, and Kethren was banned from a second cup of coffee a day.
keysToEntries.BonesToPeaches:
The essence of magic is change, this the druids believed. Elemental magics were assumed to balance one element with another. Mending spells transformed the present into past. When one druid found himself trapped in a wolf's den, he was forced to rely on this concept.

The wolf had him cornered, trapped behind a crack in the wall, too small for the wolf to attack him, but leaving him no room for escape. Around the druid, were the bones of the wolf's victims, which had been hurled through the crack like some burial ritual. Starving and thirsty, the druid worked out a spell, using the runes in his pocket to turn the bones into the sweet, sticky fruit that would keep him alive. Fed, on this strange discovery, the druid outlasted the wolf's hunger for his flesh and when the wolf went to seek his own food, the druid took his chance to escape. 

Eating the transmuted bones of others is considered a little odd in civilised society, yet when one is in the wilderness, one takes what they can get.
keysToEntries.FerociusFurnace:
Some wizards study the stars. Some wizards investigate the ways in which an enchantment can be used to make beautiful art. Jillian Varden was only interested in fire. She would study it relentlessly, she created spells to warm the hearth and light candles from a distance.  But in her heart she wanted only one thing, to create a flame hotter than anyone else. 

The furnace was her answer and she spent weeks studying how one might push the flames harder and higher, so that metal might melt faster beneath their rage. She created a spell that would revolutionize smithing, yet she dreamed still of hotter flames. 

Soot was all that remained of her lab. Her body was never recovered.
keysToEntries.Choppertunity:
Edgar was a lazy lumberjack, who was good friends with some of the wizards at the guild. He would skive off work, to visit the taverns with his blue-robed buddies, where they would laugh and discuss the fundamentals of magic, whilst their world blurred out of focus. Yet, Edgar always listened to his friends and he picked up on concepts that his more academically minded companions would miss. Magic, whilst it could be wielded like a scalpel, was also perfectly capable of being used like a club. 

With some runes in his pocket and laziness as his shield, Edgar created a spell that would make his job go much faster – shattering tree trunks into useful logs, in a matter of moments. 

He was later fired, when he tried the spell when drunk – demolishing the beams of his master's cabin.
keysToEntries.EyeOfOculus:
Elias Oculus was an avid birdwatcher. He delighted in nothing more than seeing sparrows, falcons and corvids nest. In the spring, he would wake just before the sunrise, so that he could watch the flocks of birds emerge from their nests and take to the skies in a cacophony of song. How he longed to join them, to fly beside them and witness their world in the skies. But, alas, as a human, he was chained to the floor by the unbreakable bonds of gravity.

When he started studying to be a wizard, he realised a way to live his dream. A spell that would move his vision outside the confines of his mortal flesh, and allow him to soar among the heavens. In addition to creating a spell found invaluable by architects, Elias also published the most comprehensive studies of bird flight patterns.

When he passed on to the next life, a statue was created in his honour.  It isn't found on any ruin, nor on grand display, but resting in the branches of a great tree, so that his beloved birds could remember him fondly.
keysToEntries.Treequipment:
The Wild Knights often spent weeks at a time alone in the wilderness, surviving on nothing but what they can forage and hunt. But tools and weapons, are subject to the curse of entropy and more than a few times a Wild Knight would find themselves alone and miles from civilisation, with a broken axe-shaft in hand. Death would often follow swiftly after. 

The druids among them, created this spell to save their brethren. It would transform a tree into supplies enough to let the knight return home, where they could repair their equipment properly. 

Although not as flashy as a fire-blast, this spell has saved more lives than most and it's creator is still toasted by the Wild Knights on their nights of celebration.
keysToEntries.EnchantCampfire:
The first campfires changed the path of human destiny. Shivering cave dwellers lasted longer against the winter, and in time they learned to use this flame to dominate the land. Ever since, campfires have warmed a primal part of our spirit, and many yearn for the simple pleasure of sitting around one with their companions, on a summer night. So it comes as no surprise that the first druids found a way to make such fires even more appealing. 

The runes drew out some essential nature of the campfire, letting it burn hotter, brighter and better than before. Food cooks faster, cold quickly burns away and even the campfire ghost stories feel better against a backdrop of this flame.
keysToEntries.PersonalChest:
A good bag is surprisingly difficult to make in Ashenfall. First, leather must be obtained by hunting down deer and the deer of Brynmoor are skittish creatures. They flee at the first sign of danger and chasing them can be more trouble than it's worth. Wood, on the other hand, stays pleasantly still and so it was that the druids invented this very useful little spell. 

Now, they no longer need to traipse through the forest in search of deer, when they can simply summon a chest to wherever they are. Much faster. Much easier and you don't have to lug around all those heavy resources all the time. 

This spell made druids very popular among locals, less so among hunters.
keysToEntries.GoblinWarBanner:
To most goblins, war is like breathing. Every action a precursor to conflict. Every morning they wake with a focus on picking a fight. They rarely kill one another, it would be counterproductive, but they enjoy finding someone different enough that they can justify a spat.

This is what Bandos engineered them for. When the god arrived on Yu’Biusk, he urged the population into constant conflict. Nations became states, that were ground down into tribes. An entire civilisations reduced to platoons and squadrons.

This cape bears the symbol of Bandos, a weapon known as the kyzaj – a brutal blade designed to eviscerate an opponent.

Once this was a banner, praising the war god, but now it lies forgotten and the other banners defiled to worship a new Big High War God. Velgar, who defeated a god in single combat.
keysToEntries.GarouCloak:
Highborn garou are better than their lowborn kin, or so it is claimed. It is true that the highborn are stronger and capable of wielding magic denied to the lowborn, yet what makes them great is their will to rule.

Garou claim power not through birthright, but by proving their strength over all rivals. A garou leader must be ready to face all challenges, because to refuse even one is a sign of weakness.

When a challenge is made the two garou battle to the death. Mercy is a concept alien to them, once fangs are bared then blood must be spilled.

Thanes are garou wanting to be kings. Each day they push themselves further, to be ready for the challenge that will see them burn moon-bright as king, or fade away as a forgotten challenge. All or nothing, the garou way.

Even after turning from the moon-goddess Lougrim, becoming servants of the High Queen Kuldra, the garou continue their ancient rituals. Might, always proving right.
keysToEntries.TatteredCape: A cape made from tattered strips of cloth.
keysToEntries.AdventurersCape:
Capes are stylish and protect against the weather. They can be elaborate symbols of stature, or simple cloth to act as a ward against the cold. This cape is the latter.

Simple, elegant, and functional. Worn by adventurers and explorers across Gielinor’s mainland and beyond.

After the dragon attack, there are few of these capes worn on Ashenfall. The rest sink beneath the ocean waves, are torn to pieces on the cliff rocks, or have long since burned to ash beneath draconic hate.
keysToEntries.SaradominCape: A cloak embroidered with the symbol of Saradomin, the human god of order. You'll be the envy of priests everywhere.
keysToEntries.CapeOfVelgar: A cape depicting a powerful dragon.
keysToEntries.AbyssalDemonCape: This cloak is infused with the dark power of the Abyss. It's probably fine, definitely not cursed at all. Honest.
keysToEntries.GraniteMaulHead: A chunk of granite imbued with powerful magic. On its own, this hunk of stone isn’t useful, but when combined with other components it becomes part of a much stronger weapon.
keysToEntries.GraniteMaulHandle: The handle for a powerful weapon. Wrapped in sturdy leather and imbued with ritual magic. A dwarf capable of crafting such a piece is acknowledged to have completed one of the trials required to be a master smith.
keysToEntries.GraniteMaulWrap: These wrappings are made from the leather of a powerful and dangerous beast. The ambient anima of Ashenfall infuses it with a sturdiness that most leather wrapping can’t equal.
keysToEntries.AstralRune: Astral runes borrow resonance from the stars, that silent hum when looking up at the night sky. They embody the power of distance and displacement, action from inaction, movement in the dark. Astral runes are used primarily in spells designed to summon an object to the caster’s location, making it a very popular rune with the lazier magicians.
keysToEntries.EarthRune: Imbued with the harmony of soil and dirt, the earth rune stands firm in a world of mercurial change. Predominantly used to manipulate or control natural elements associated with, soil and rock, it can also be used to reference the permanence of things.
keysToEntries.WaterRune: Water is a strange thing, required by all living things, yet capable of ending civilisations. Rain is vital to the survival of a kingdom, yet its citizens lament the grey clouds forming overhead. Mages understand that water is a special sort of power, capable of being shaped in a multitude of ways. Water runes embody this fluidity of concept, finding use in many different types of spell.
keysToEntries.LawRune: The law rune is an odd sort of power, it embodies not some elemental force, but instead it represents the rigidity of reality. The rules in which all of creation operates, are conceptually encapsulated within this small stone. Gravity, movement, location, all these things are bound within, and when the rune is used in magic, these rules may be altered for a time. Teleportation relies heavily on these runes, overwriting the concept of distance for the casting.
keysToEntries.NatureRune: Contrary to popular belief, the nature rune does not solely govern plants, roots and the natural world. That is only part of its design. Instead, nature is about the connection of things, threading together the way things could be, rewriting them to a state more desirable. Favoured by wizard’s for their ability to adjust the flow of time to an entity, dragging it back to a point when it was in a more preferable state.
keysToEntries.WeakWarmingPotion: This potion never chills, always tasting warm on the tongue. It's sweet, like hot chocolate round a warming fire and that feeling spreads throughout the drinker's body, shielding them from the chill of night.
keysToEntries.WeakCoolingPotion: The liquid in this potion is always cold to the touch. It tastes like an iced glass of lemonade on a hot summer's day, or a chilled glass of water when you've been working out in the sun. Drinking this potion makes the body cooler than before, shielding one against fire's fury.
keysToEntries.MeatPizza:
A delicious pizza, covered in juicy tender pieces of meat, and seasoned with a delicious sauce. You might ask yourself where the cheese comes from, but it’s best not to think too hard about it. Ashenfall has many secrets and those who survive here must do many strange things. No one judges you for this “cheese”, it’s fine.

Just enjoy the delicious flavours and try not to have an existential crisis.
keysToEntries.Kebab: The Drunken Dwarf delights in the humble kebab, offering it to any that might grab his attention. The delicious combination of spices and subtle flavourings, makes it a marvellous meal. For those afflicted, or self-inflicted, with particular poisonings, the kebab is a time-honoured remedy.
keysToEntries.MeatStew: A classic meal across multiple realities. The aviansie enjoy a version of this using insects called Tobi’it. Goblins make their meat stews out of anything they can find… results vary.
keysToEntries.Peach:
Try not to think too hard about the nature of transmutation as you consume this fruit. It's sweet, juicy and the pseudo-necromantic origins only make it a little disturbing. Oh, sure, it tastes nice and it goes surprisingly well in a crumble, but it's a little disturbing when you get that unexpected crunch. 

Best not to think about it. Ignorance is bliss.
keysToEntries.DivineCabbage:
Few gods offer their bounty as freely as Brassica Prime, lord of nutrition and god of deliciousness. In exchange for this bounty of crisp and delightful vegetables, he asks only for the runes that make the spell possible. No prayers are invoked, nor altar laden with sacrifices, just a few runes and a gesture of fingers, to provide a most healthy meal. 

The cabbages here are perfect. Their leaves are crisp. Just the right amount of moisture is contained within each bite. Nutrition abundant in every morsel. A true gift from the gods.
keysToEntries.DireWolfLeather:
The tanned hide of a powerful dire wolf. It's sturdy, thick, ready for enchantment and more resilient than normal leather. 

A legend speaks of a warrior that wore the tanned hide of a dire wolf. He wore it proudly as he walked the streets and used it to strike fear into the hearts of bandits and criminals alike. After all, a warrior that could best such a beast, was no one to be trifled with. 

The story goes that one day he met another warrior, proud, skillful and handsome, and the two of them became the best of friends. They would fight together, spar together and rescued helpless villagers as an unstoppable pair. Until the night of the full moon, where the truth was revealed. Where once his new friend had stood, there was now a dread wolf of midnight-black fur and eyes of burning coal. They fought again, but gone was the jovial sparring of friends. Now they fought as man and beast, tooth and claw versus blade and shield, until all that was left of the two of them was this story.
keysToEntries.GroundStone: Much harder to stub your foot on in powdered form. Stone ground down and down until all that remains is a fine powder useful in ways that big lumps of stone are not. Like cake frosting for your enemies.
keysToEntries.BirdsNest: Once the home to a chirping little critter, this nest now best serves as a means of gathering materials. Don’t feel bad about destroying a little bird’s home, they were saying horrible things about you anyway. I mean really horrible, like so, so bad.
keysToEntries.GarouPack: These leathery packs are carried around by garou warriors. They store rations, herbs and the spoils of battle inside. Crafted by elderly garou in the villages, or parents sending their child off to battle.
keysToEntries.GoblinPack: This foul smelling container is made from animal hide and filled with whatever trinkets its owner collected. Occasionally a goblin will keep a pet beetle, worm or spider in these, so be careful when opening.
keysToEntries.SummoningHorn:
Cathan’s challenge horn design is a collection of materials hastily thrown together. A dragon’s tooth, carved with dragonkin sigils as well as metal wires used to connect them together –  like a spiderweb lattice, glistening with traces of magical power.

When placed in the dragonkin altar, the horn acts as a focus point for the magical power stored within the tower, sending an invisible beacon off into the air, in a perfect draconic pitch.

Velgar has been summoned many times before. By heroes, kings, warriors and gods, and yet still the tower stands.

Be ready, when you send out the call.
keysToEntries.BandosianAmulet1:
A seemingly simple amulet carrying the symbol of Bandos. It feels strangely warm to the touch, and a faint sensation like the memory of a heartbeat thrums through your fingertips.

Zanik would be very interested in this.
keysToEntries.BandosianAmulet2: A simple amulet bearing Bandos’ symbol. Every once in a while you could swear you hear strange whispers from it, like a song you can’t quite remember. It’s cold to the touch and makes the hairs, on the back of your neck, shiver constantly.
keysToEntries.KingsToken:
These tokens are a unique creation, crafted hundreds of years ago by high priests of Lougrim. They are passed down from monarch to monarch, a sign of their overwhelming power and strength. The power of a king has waned over the centuries, no longer sovereigns of nations, but now masters of the clan. Servants of the High Queen, Kuldra, the god-eater.

Inside each token rests a single pebble taken from Antumnos, the Garou homeworld.
keysToEntries.PurifiedKingsToken:
These tokens are a unique creation, crafted hundreds of years ago by high priests of Lougrim. They are passed down from monarch to monarch, a sign of their overwhelming power and strength. This token has been blessed as part of an ancient Garou ritual, symbolically wiping away Gielinor's "corruption". Although it looks no different to before it was purified, it feels strangely lighter and is a little cold to the touch. 

Inside each token rests a single pebble taken from Antumnos, the Garou homeworld, and if you focus your senses you're sure you can feel the faintest pulse from within.
keysToEntries.VelgarsHead:
General Velgar was one of Queen Kuldra’s most feared disciples. Infused with the power of the earth itself, his poison corrupted entire regions and slaughtered countless foes.

The fall of Blue Flame Keep, Bramblemead village, and the Saradominist presence on Ashenfall, was orchestrated by Velgar’s hand. His army incinerated villagers, priests and soldiers alike. His forces tore down castles and houses with equal relish.

For thousands of years, Velgar has been a thorn in everyone’s side, until one hero took him down.

Bring this to the Wise OId Man, rumours are that he can make something useful from it.
keysToEntries.CrystalSeed:
The crystal tree is a magical thing, a native to the elven world Tarddiad and brought to Gielinor when the elves migrated. Unlike most seeds it is nourished not by soil, nor sunlight, but by music. Elf song reaches into the crystal, creating a harmonious resonance that causes the crystal to thrive.

As well as growing trees, this seed can also be sung into specific shapes and forms, becoming a tool or weapon of great power.

This seed is a little different to those found in Tirannwn, infused both with the magic of Ashenfall, but also the essence of cherished memories.
keysToEntries.CrystalOrb: A crystal sphere containing a shimmering fog. It’s part of a magical ritual needed to craft a powerful staff, one that is infused with celestial power. The Icyene, of Hallow, once gifted these orbs to the followers of Saradomin, so that they might better serve the god of Order.
keysToEntries.RingOfPursuit:
Hunters have always needed an edge, and this ring is just that. It enhances a hunter’s senses and quietens their movements. Their prey becomes less wary of them, and more vulnerable to attack.

The garou were particularly fond of these rings, and during the few years of peace between the humans of Bramblemead and the garou of Ghornfell, villagers could barter these trinkets for far more than they were worth to other villagers.
keysToEntries.WoodenClub: Sticks and stones may break your bones and this club is proof of the first bit. A large stuck that can be swung with impressive heft, capable of crushing bone with surprising ease. Popular by every warrior ever, when they're abandoned in the woods and need to defend themselves.
keysToEntries.AshLongbow:
Many a villager longed to be a master archer. The youngsters would spend hours shooting cabbages, potatoes and whatever fruit or vegetable they could get their hands on, all to prove they had what it took to be a master of the bow. 

The ash longbow was their first weapon in this journey. Many wouldn't be able to muster the strength needed to pull the string taught. More still would struggle to fire the arrow straight, missing their targets by embarrassingly large distances. But those who could wield the bow, who could strike the cabbages from as far as possible, they would become the most popular of the Bramblemead youngsters.
keysToEntries.AshSapling: A young ash tree, easily torn down by just your bare hands. Doesn't provide much wood, but it's a good start.
keysToEntries.AshTree: A mature ash tree, strong and sturdy and a great source of timber.
keysToEntries.OakTree: Oak trees look down on the trees around them, convinced of their own superiority. Their bark is harder, stronger and more difficult to fell than their ash cousins.
keysToEntries.BlightwoodRoot: These roots have absorbed more of the anima than other roots around them. It has made them stronger and more durable than the rest of the flora around them.
keysToEntries.StoneBoulder: Large boulders can be found just about anywhere. With even the most basic pickaxe they can be reduced to little more than pebbles, which are much more useful for crafting. It is perhaps humbling to see theses centuries old stones reduced to nothing.
keysToEntries.ClayDeposit: Clay can be found in deposits around Ashenfall and can be recovered with just a few pickaxe swings. It is said that the Elder God created the universe out of clay, albeit of a more interesting vintage, and the unwanted remains hurled into the abyss. The truth of this claim is hard to establish as the Elder Gods are rarely prone to a friendly chat. Not least because, were you to hear them speak, your head would explode. This makes conversation tricky.
keysToEntries.CopperRock: On its own copper is useful but not great for weapons or armour and must be combined with tin to create the sturdier bronze.
keysToEntries.TinRock: Tin and copper are the classic double act, combining their essence to make the superior bronze.
keysToEntries.GoldRock: Few metals are as beloved as gold. Its glistening nature has attracted countless pursuers, and those who find a viable vein need never work again. Sadly, on Ashenfall, the economy is not quite so well established and gold is merely another crafting component. Still, remember where you found it, because once all this dragon malarky is dealt with you might make a tidy sum selling the location to prospectors.
keysToEntries.SilverRock: They say that silver is touched by the moon, explaining why the lycanthropes are so affected by it. Silver makes for an exceptionally good magical conductor, making it highly prized by enchanters everywhere.
keysToEntries.GraniteDeposit: These great lumps of rock are sturdier than normal stone and are excellent materials for construction.
keysToEntries.IronRock: Do you know the riddle of iron? No, me neither, asking for a friend. Iron is a durable metal, capable of creating some of the strongest arms and armour available.
keysToEntries.RuneEssenceGeyser:
This strange material absorbs magical energy, causing it to defy gravity, among other things. It is best known for the creation of runes, powerful little stones that can be used to cast spells. A pickaxe is needed to harvest the essence. 

The floating essence rocks can be knocked out of orbit, with a little effort, making them easier to mine.
keysToEntries.SandstoneDeposit: Large sandstone deposits can be mined for this useful crafting material.
keysToEntries.DragonAltar:
This strange altar is a creation of dragonkin alchemy, more than it is a place of religious observance. It hums with a faint power, imbued as it is with the energies of Ashenfall. Atop the altar sits a resting place for a challenge horn, a device used to summon a dragon and force them into battle. 

This altar is attuned to General Velgar, a warrior dragon infused with the power of the earth.
keysToEntries.BloodblightCape:
The Bloodblight swamp was once a pleasant place. Golden fields of wheat, rustling in the breeze. Life here was simple yet fulfilling.

Until Velgar took it all away.

He fought the mighty god of war, Bandos, and he should have lost. Dragons are powerful, and Velgar is stronger than most, but even in his most arrogant days he was no match for a god.

Yet, arrogance is not a mortal taint and the war god reeked of it. Bandos cast aside his wards. He refused to don his armour. The god pressed into the lair of the great dragon, and tried to wrestle him to the grave.

He nearly succeeded. Bandos wrapped his arms around the dragon’s serpentine neck and dug his fingernails beneath the scales. Velgar, in his writhing fury, called down a poison-filled rock from the heavens and cast it at Bandos, striking him in the temple. As the god reeled, he released Velgar, allowing the dragon to breathe a jet of anima-infused poison, at the unsteady god. Still wavering from the blow, Bandos breathed in the toxins and felt weakness for the first time in centuries.

A smart tactician, Bandos retreated. Better to face the shame of retreat, than risk the oblivion of death.

In his jubilant rage, Velgar spilled his blood on the once fertile plains, corrupting them with his hate-filled poison. The fields wilted. The crops blackened and then collapsed into venom.

This cloak, is reminder of that blighted day.
keysToEntries.BramblemeadCape:
The heart of Bramblemead village was never really the castle, which came much later to the party. It wasn’t the town hall. It wasn’t the druid huts. It was the windmill. Grain heaped by the village was brought here and milled down into flour. The flour became bread, pastry and batter, turning village meals into something that helped people to live, rather than simply survive.

Food, becomes a banner around which everyone can gather. To break bread with one’s enemy was a way to settle old scores, and forge new connections. More good has been done over the dinner table, than on any throne. Flour, turned Bramblemead from a settlement, into a thriving village.

Now, the windmill lies dormant and still. The foundations warped by the centuries, despite the magic holding the rot at bay in the ancient timber, and the stone cracking from time. When humanity fell, it was rats and goblins that claimed the heart of the village, though they had no use for the swirling sails.

To the goblins it was a place where food could be hidden. Supplies sealed up from rival tribes. For the rats, it was a restaurant full of goblin supplies that were never well sealed.

The sail no longer turns. But, perhaps, in the fullness of time, this village heart might beat again.
keysToEntries.FracturedCape:
Brakka’s hubris was a marvel to behold, perhaps more impressive than any monument of his construction. When Rasthin shared with him the elemental power she had wrung from Ashenfall’s mysterious vents, Brakka became a master of the earth itself.

With the power to shape stone and soil, by will alone, Brakka could have created great villages. He could have constructed aqueducts and watermills that could brought Ashenfall into a new, golden age. Instead, he made a tower.

A single tower that rose as far as the eye could see. Forged through his own power, and the blood, sweat and tears of garou slaves. It is, perhaps, fitting, that this is where he died. Kuldra, the godeater, crushed him beneath her great claw and feasted on his anima-rich soul. Stealing Brakka’s power for herself.

She gifted Velgar, her most trusted general, with the elemental mastery of the fallen Nodon architect, and, to mock the dragonkin further, Velgar took Brakka’s monument as his new lair.

Now the tower stands for all to see, as a reminder of dragonkin hubris, and rightfully deserves its new moniker; Brakka’s folly.
keysToEntries.StormtouchedCape:
Centuries ago, a spell was cast over the Highlands that changed it forever. Once temperate skies became pendulous clouds filled with lightning. Electric hate that strikes at anything and everything that dares step beneath their gaze.

War changes everything, even the sky.

The land itself is strewn with the dead. Ancient skeletons of colossal wyrms, the monstrous creations of dragonkin science, lie across the terrain, dead eyes watching over everything. Lacrussa considered these monstrosities her finest work. Yet she shed no tears at their destruction. She could always make more, or so she believed.

It was a lone girl, a necromancer of great power, who turned the sky into a curse. A last act of vengeance for a fallen friend. She dug into forbidden magic, drawing power from the countless dead slain in constant conflict. The garou, lords of this region, were not prepared for the power of grief, for it was a concept alien to their primordial nature.

These stories weave into the threads of this cape, barely remembered, yet never quite forgotten.
keysToEntries.WhisperingCape:
It is said these swamps are haunted. Those who wander it at night, tell stories of whispering that bubbles up from the fetid water.

There is a coldness to these swamps, that is more than just temperature. It reaches beneath the warmest clothing and presses itself into bone and blood. Heartbeats slow and bravery breaks beneath its touch.

The trees here are old and spiteful, their roots wrapped around the bones of adventurers, villagers, and warriors alike. They care not for goblin, human, or garou, they will strangle the remains of any within their reach.

Occasionally tiny stones will bubble from the surface, black as midnight and cold as the darkest winter night. A villager once tried to craft jewelry from these stones, thinking them beautiful. It took twelve village guard to put an end to her murder spree.

This cape is woven from the fibres of swamp plants, and shields its wearer from the swamp’s whispers. Or so the old wives tales claim.
keysToEntries.Swingslash:
Contrary to popular belief, a goblin is as smart as any other species. It was Bandos that instilled within them the notion that intelligence was a sin, and demonstrating your education was something to be discouraged. As a result, goblins often go out of their way to make themselves seem less intelligent than they really are.

Hence “swingslash”, the name given to this axe because of how it swings through the air, slashing anything it hits. It’s so reductive and silly, it can only be an overcorrection, a desperate attempt by the creator to seem dumber than they are.

You can ignore, therefore the curve of the blade and the sharpness of the edge, created by carefully thought-out techniques that make the weapon deadlier than those before them. The name, chosen by the blacksmith, so none might accuse them of the crime of being smart.
keysToEntries.AdventurersLongbow:
The expedition to Ashenfall included skilled adventurers. If fate had been kinder, they could have gone down in legend as mighty heroes, rather than a slow, quiet, death where their names might be forgotten.

This bow was a design brought to Ashenfall by the hunter, Finn, a simple longbow crafted from easily obtained materials. Finn was a scout, and a ranger, who enjoyed getting into all the nooks and crannies when he would wander White Wolf mountain in search of treasure. He joined the expedition in hope of discovering new wonders, and new stories to share with the people back home.

Instead he found a dragon, and the end to his story.
keysToEntries.WoodenSword:
In the village of Bramblemead, children grew up knowing battle. The God Wars hadn’t reached them yet, but the priests always knew that the danger was coming. So children were taught combat in the form of a game.

Carpenters were encouraged to make little wooden swords, so that the children could emulate the noble paladins that would wander the village. These soldiers were held up as the peak aspiration, young men and women daydreamed through school, about taking up those glittering swords and running off into adventure.

These weapons clicked and clacked against one another, harmless it seemed to the adults that watched. But wood in Bramblemead was hardened and preserved by druidic magic, and it soon became clear the damage these swords could deal. Injuries were common, bruises and broken bones were the rite of passage of every child.

It was fortunate, perhaps, that these weapons were so painful – because the children of Bramblemead grew up with a better understanding that war was no game.
keysToEntries.Skullsplitter:
Garou children would often play pretend at great, epic, battles. They would create their own stories, true, but they enjoyed reenacting the battles of legend, and no character was more popular than King Raluh, the mighty.

Pups would wander into the forests in search of the sturdiest stick they could find and then announce to the other children that they were the king, and this stick that most legendary of weapons – Skullsplitter.

As the legends go, King Raluh had been granted a weapon by the moon goddess, Lougrim. A weapon capable of destroying any foe. Armour was as paper to Skullsplitter and the fables had him crush the bones of villain and monster alike.

It is, therefore, no surprise that as the garou pups mature, they create weapons to match the legends, and garou warriors wield mighty maces carrying that mythical name. Though the true Skullsplitter may be just a story, these powerful imitations are no less deadly.
keysToEntries.ChieftainsBlade:
Mouldfinger never wanted to be chief, unlike the other goblins she aspired to be just a background character – happy and forgotten while she worked on her own things. She enjoyed working with wood, bone and, on those special occasions, metal.

She crafted a sword, a simple blade that was sturdy and strong, and the other goblins went wild because of it. Seeing its sharpness, they became convinced it was magical, or imbued with the power of their draconic overlord. It wasn’t, it was just very well made.

She was made chieftain after the others challenged her, and her sharpened blade cut easily through their defenses. A fate she had never wanted, and a fate she could never escape.
keysToEntries.WildScoutsShortbow:
There was magic once, in this bow, though it has mostly faded now. Runes were ground down and mixed with plant oils, to create a special resin that allowed the Wild Knights to work their magic through the bow, as if they were carrying runes. That magic has faded away, and the recipe for the mixture lost to the ages, but these bows are still formidable in their own right.

Wild Knights told tales of creatures that lived within the wilds of Ashenfall, that could not be felled by normal bows. Deer with hides that glistened with starlight, sheep who’s silver fleeces repelled all arrows. They claimed that these magically infused bows were the only ones capable of harming the creatures, though none ever returned with trophies to share.
keysToEntries.GrievingMoonStaff:
Lougrim was a moon deity, born to a cold world of soft silvers and silence. Her people lived lives of quiet contemplation, each moment spent wondering about the next. They were passive, soft spoken and sedentary, which drove Lougrim wild.

When a doorway to another world appeared and a grieving god wandered through it, Lougrim took the opportunity to run. To flee to another place, and live a life that she could endure.

History skips the next bit. Her closest allies have never learned what elevated her from her previous form to that of the goddess of the hunt, but whatever it is was profound enough to change her completely. She no longer resembled her silent kindred. She was quiet, yes, but in the way a tiger is moments before the kill. She is contemplative, true, but it was the stillness of a spider waiting for its prey.

When she found the garou, she knew she had found her real kindred. They were violent and deadly, and they yearned for the hunt. When she took her place as their goddess, she focused and refined them, creating hunters that were capable of felling almost any prey.

Yet still she kept the moon as her symbol, a reminder of her home and how far she has come since leaving it.

Now her priests bear the symbol, atop their staves and as the focus on their altars. They accept and revere Kuldra as their High Queen, yet they grieve the moon goddess that made them who they are today.
keysToEntries.DragonboneDagger:
Viika was never fond of creating dragons, they thought the process was cruel and unbecoming. When an egg was laid, Viika saw in that moment both the egg and the dragon it would become. They saw past and presence at the same time, and they grieved the creature before it had even hatched.

They pleaded with their sibling, and with the other Dragonkin, to change their treatment of the beasts. But Viika’s curse, like so many Seers, was to be ignored by their kindred.

A rare egg hatched once, containing a dragon with white scales like pearls. The dragon was infused with magic and possessing a power capable of travelling to other worlds, where she might have lived a happier life. But Viika wept, for they knew she would manage no such ending.

Viika kept the hatchling company. Treated them like their dearest friend, and held them softly when Lacrussa’s need for knowledge, took the dragon’s last breath.

The Seer kept only a single keepsake – a blade carved from the tooth of their white-scaled friend. A dagger capable of terrible damage, but as fragile as the friendship that it reminded them of.
keysToEntries.ThanesAuthority:
Might makes right. This is the truth of the Garou philosophy. Strength and cunning dominates all. The Garou thanes demonstrate this might in the weapons they wield. Though heavier and more tiring, this axe swings with deadly force, capable of cleaving through almost any armour.

The design is passed down to the garou from templates granted to the first garou blacksmiths by divine revelation. Lougrim taught them about the curve of the blade, and the heft of the hilt. Designed for the hunt, it could fell most quarry in a single blow.

Under Kuldra’s leadership, the chosen quarry was human. The dragon queen had a deep hatred for the humans, she found them weak, petty and flawed. The Thanes carried out her orders, using the axe to wield their authority, in her name.
keysToEntries.HunterStagbow:
Gahl was a garou druid who lost everyone he knew. In the wars inflicted on his people by the other races of Ashenfall, he grew jaded with the cruelty he saw all around him. This reached its peak when he found a garou village in ruins and goblins dancing and laughing as they threatened a newly born pup, in her crib.

The village is gone now, dragged beneath the soil. Gahl’s vengeance unleashed upon the goblins, erasing them from existence. He took the child as his own and raised her in a way alien to his people – he raised her with love.

She had no talent for magic, and could not learn his druid spells, so he taught her how to hunt with a bow, and she was a natural. Dahnu, as he named her, made a bow out of the first deer she and her father had hunted and that bow became a symbol to all she encountered.

In time she would claim a king’s seat. Defeating the prior monarch with reason and wit. Charming him out of his power and offering her people another way.  

Her people thrived, making her father proud.

Until Velgar brought her reign to an end.
keysToEntries.DraconicStaff:
Rasthin was a powerful sorceress, even among the dragonkin. She had mastered all manner of spells, and was even schooled in the techniques from the previous Revision.

Still, magic as an art was largely looked down upon by dragonkin society. It was considered vulgar, and the obsession of children, and alchemy and science was the real way forward. In part it’s because of the connection between magic and the hated Elder Gods, and the dragonkin feared rousing, further, their anger.

But Rasthin knew the value of magic, as did the other members of her group. She created a staff, forged from glittering crystal and anima-infused iron, which allowed her to channel her magic into powerful spells.

Yet, even with a weapon as powerful as this, she discarded her magic when she uncovered the power of Ashenfall – preferring, instead, to enjoy her newfound connection with elemental flame.

Still, the remnants of the staff bristle with potential, and while they may never reach the might of their ancestor, they remain powerful magic tools just waiting to be crafted.
keysToEntries.LeggingsOfLightness:
A cousin to the better-known Boots of Lightness, popular on the Gielinor mainland. These leggings were created by Shana, a Bramblemead villager who hated the cold. While the boots were functional, and useful, making it easier to carry things round the village, she always felt the winds chill no matter what she wore.

Her solution was simple. Since enchantment created an excess of energy, at least when she did it – she was never going to become a wizard – she laced the leggings with the lightness charm.

The effect was twofold. Firstly, she could carry things around the town as easily as if she were wearing the boots. Secondly, the enchantment kept her legs nice and warm.

Fortunately, the designs for these leggings improved over the years, removing the overheating problem that Shana had been so fond of. Although she was doubtless less fond of it after her house caught fire.

Progress demands sacrifice.
keysToEntries.PaladinHelm:
The knights of Saradomin had many names over the years. White Knights. Temple Knights. In Ashenfall they were called Paladins. Brave, and faithful, members of noble families that took up the sword.

They were the second, third and fourth children of Brynmoor’s “nobility” – a title given to those families that were most loyal to the priesthood, and the most obedient were raised the highest.

This helm was a symbol of pride to the respectable families of Brynmoor. A sign that the bearer had been chosen to stand as the guardian of Saradominist faith for all of Brynmoor to see. It earned them favour, it earned the status, it earned them the ire of those who followed a different faith.
keysToEntries.PaladinBody:
The knights of Saradomin had many names over the years. White Knights. Temple Knights. In Ashenfall they were called Paladins. Brave, and faithful, members of noble families that took up the sword.

They were the second, third and fourth children of Brynmoor’s “nobility” – a title given to those families that were most loyal to the priesthood, and the most obedient were raised the highest.

The chest piece was especially well made. Blacksmiths would take extra special care to make sure that the process went smoothly, as nothing but perfection would withstand inspection. It made forgery practically impossible, or at the very least, far too expensive to work.

It is said that the paladins were capable of working miracles in battle. Rumour held that the power of Saradomin flowed through them, and helped them to smite all who stood against the church.
keysToEntries.PaladinLegs:
The knights of Saradomin had many names over the years. White Knights. Temple Knights. In Ashenfall they were called Paladins. Brave, and faithful, members of noble families that took up the sword.

They were the second, third and fourth children of Brynmoor’s “nobility” – a title given to those families that were most loyal to the priesthood, and the most obedient were raised the highest.

These platelegs were exceptionally well made. Sturdier and stronger than the armour worn by Brynmoor guards, and other members of Ashenfall’s human soldiers. It was certainly stronger than the helms of the Guthixian aligned knights, or the druids they guarded.

Paladins were a righteous force, noble and true to the priest’s teachings. They brought order from chaos, and discipline to sloth. Remaking Bramblemead according to the church’s designs.
keysToEntries.WildArchersHelm:
The Wild Knights were known for their ability to scout the wild areas of Ashenfall. They could walk where no one else dared to tread, and they were masters of the hunt, putting even the garou to shame.

Their archers wore sturdy leathers, using carefully laid folds to shield them against harm. They would break easily, but the archers made it part of their ritual to maintain them regularly. Each day they would wake and tend to the leather, making their armour seem brand new with each morning.

The helm is designed to focus the wearer’s vision, so that their target receives all their attention. Distractions are cut out of the picture. Everything exterior to their quarry is removed from view. The archer’s sight is trained on their victim and, so the rumours claimed, they never missed their marks.
keysToEntries.WildArchersBody:
The Wild Knights were known for their ability to scout the wild areas of Ashenfall. They could walk where no one else dared to tread, and they were masters of the hunt, putting even the garou to shame.

Their archers wore sturdy leathers, using carefully laid folds to shield them against harm. They would break easily, but the archers made it part of their ritual to maintain them regularly. Each day they would wake and tend to the leather, making their armour seem brand new with each morning.

Archers of the Wild Knights were quick, silent and deadly. A joke their more melee focused companions used to raise on those rare occasions that the knights would gather. Drink and company lead to banter and poor jokes, which created much missed laughter that the Wild Knights always enjoyed.

Archer, warrior or mage, the Wild Knights would hurl jibes at once another, without a moment’s thought. Yet, when needed, there was no stronger camaraderie and they would all die for one another.
keysToEntries.DragonkinDarkMageHood:
The dragonkin of Ashenfall all reveled in power, gaining elemental mastery from the strange anima of the land. Although their kind tended to ignore magic, in favour of alchemy and science, the dragonkin of Ashenfall enjoyed their time as sorcerer kings.

They were robes that empowered their magic, using alchemical techniques to create cloth that would resist their elemental powers. Fire would not burn so easily, cold would not damage the threads and lightning could be turned aside with a flick of a cloak. Though these techniques are lost to time, the echoes of their powerful resistance remains.

The hood was designed for two purposes. The shadow it cast upon the dragonkin features, made their visage scarier and intimidation easier. Goblins, humans and garou would cower from the reptilian grins that was all they could see in the darkness. The second was to hide their frustration at the foolishness of others, their fellow dragonkin included. Rolling eyes is much easier when no one else can see.
keysToEntries.DragonkinDarkMageRobes:
The dragonkin of Ashenfall all reveled in power, gaining elemental mastery from the strange anima of the land. Although their kind tended to ignore magic, in favour of alchemy and science, the dragonkin of Ashenfall enjoyed their time as sorcerer kings.

They were robes that empowered their magic, using alchemical techniques to create cloth that would resist their elemental powers. Fire would not burn so easily, cold would not damage the threads and lightning could be turned aside with a flick of a cloak. Though these techniques are lost to time, the echoes of their powerful resistance remains.

Although dragonkin tend to favour form over fashion, they stitched these robes with undeniable regal zeal. They were more elaborate than necessary, more stylish than required, feeding into the egos of their wearers.

Rasthin, it is said, was particularly fond of stitching elaborate filigree into her robes. Made from threads woven with precious metals, and gemstones, her robes would sparkle and glitter as she flew across the realm, and when she brought forward her flames, everyone marveled.

Glittering against the sky, she inspired devotion as much as fear in her subjects.
keysToEntries.DragonkinDarkMageLegs:
The dragonkin of Ashenfall all reveled in power, gaining elemental mastery from the strange anima of the land. Although their kind tended to ignore magic, in favour of alchemy and science, the dragonkin of Ashenfall enjoyed their time as sorcerer kings.

They were robes that empowered their magic, using alchemical techniques to create cloth that would resist their elemental powers. Fire would not burn so easily, cold would not damage the threads and lightning could be turned aside with a flick of a cloak. Though these techniques are lost to time, the echoes of their powerful resistance remains.

Lacrussa was the only dragonkin to resist the alure of magic. She insisted that science was the greater power, capable of almost anything with time and study. Lacrussa wore the robes as an a small act of rebellion, claiming that magic would always be overcome in the end.

Unlike her companions, Lacrussa refused to decorate her robes. She took no joy in precious stones, or shining metals. She refused to embroider designs across the cloak. She did, however, use alchemical techniques to enhance the defensive power of the robes. Something the other dragonkin soon came to copy, proving, in her mind, the superiority of science.
keysToEntries.BagOfNoggin:
Noggin was never the best looker in Ashenfall, or so others claimed. He lived his life ashamed of his appearance and wore a sad-looking sack across his face so that none would fall ill from his hideous features.

He found himself an outcast. Mocked and jeered at by the locals of Bramblemead, so he hid himself away in the forests, but kept wearing the sack.

One day, while he was standing in a field scaring birds away, just for the fun, he met a lost villager who had wandered too far. She was frightened, at first, and fearful of the strange figure with a face of itchy cloth, but as they talked and laughed together that fear fell away.

In time, they would grow closer with one another. Developing feelings that Noggin never believed he would feel. On the day they were to be wed, Noggin finally let her see the face beneath the sackcloth, but there was no face to be found.

Instead, when the sack was removed, Noggin’s clothes went limp and fell to the floor in a pile and the woman screamed as the silence engulfed her.

Now Noggin’s face appears on scarecrows and training dummy’s all across Ashenfall, and on cold, dark nights, you could swear they are watching.
keysToEntries.WildArchersLegs:
The Wild Knights were known for their ability to scout the wild areas of Ashenfall. They could walk where no one else dared to tread, and they were masters of the hunt, putting even the garou to shame.

Their archers wore sturdy leathers, using carefully laid folds to shield them against harm. They would break easily, but the archers made it part of their ritual to maintain them regularly. Each day they would wake and tend to the leather, making their armour seem brand new with each morning.

Archers worked alone, it was easier that way. They could stalk their prey, silent and slowly, sometimes taking days between kills as they followed their quarry around the lands. They would hunt monsters and people with equal focus, as dictated by the order’s commands.

To harm the wilds was to make yourself a target for the Wild Knights. To sully the green lands of Guthix, an unforgivable crime. Yet, there were always those who would risk their ire, wannabe despots, and greedy fools yearning to plunder the realm.

They all met the fate they deserved, in the end.
keysToEntries.Sustenance:
Food is a basic need for all living creatures. We consume, or we die. Life on Ashenfall is no different.

Your Sustenance meter tracks how hungry you are. It is filled by eating food and it will deplete over time as you exert yourself trying to survive the wilds of Ashenfall.

A high Sustenance meter causes your health to regenerate more quickly. However, should you run out of Sustenance, your health will start depleting as your body canibalises itself in an attempt to survive.

Keep an eye on how hungry you are. Higher tier foods will provide more Sustenance than lower tier foods, but berries will help you survive in a pinch.
keysToEntries.Thirst:
Water is the most basic necessity for all known life on Gielinor. Dehydration is deadlier than the strongest monster, guaranteeing death in its victims if they suffer it long enough.

Your Hydration meter tracks how well hydrated you are. The higher your meter, the better your rate of stamina regeneration – allowing you to perform more actions. Skill longer. Fight better.

Should your Hydration meter reach zero, you’ll start to suffer constant health loss until you can refill your meter by drinking a source of water.

Dirty water can be found in ponds, lakes and other bodies of water around Ashenfall. Dirty water can be purified by boiling it at a campfire.
keysToEntries.Endurance:
We all need to rest from time to time. Sometimes everything just gets too much, we get tired, stressed, and just generally beaten down by the world, and it’s no different on Ashenfall.

Rest represents your overall state of resilience. Your ability to overcome to mental trials of the wild. It will deplete over time if you’re not resting, as well as every time you suffer lethal damage. Certain environmental effects will also take their toll.

The lower your Rest, the less functional you’ll be. You’ll find it harder to lug everything around. It becomes more draining to swing an axe, or a sword. Even magic will become more difficult as you tired yourself out.

There are four levels of Rest:

Well-rested - this will leave you functioning at your best

Tired, you’ll start to suffer penalties to your Stamina, as well as your Carrying Capacity.

Knackered - everything is really starting to get to you. You’ll suffer penalties to Stamina, Carry Capacity and even find yourself thirstier and hungrier.

Exhausted- you’ll suffer heavy penalties to Stamina and Carry Capacity, as well as finding spells more expensive to cast.

Fortunately you can recover your Rest with a nice long rest in bed.
keysToEntries.Lifeward:
Ashenfall is rich in anima, it flows through every rock, every tree and every living thing on the continent. This means that armour that is forged, on Ashenfall, is inherently more magically attuned than armour made anywhere else. This extra magical energy manifests as Lifeward, a protective barrier generated by the armour that shields the wearer from harm.

Lifeward acts almost like extra health, taking damage in place of their bearer’s vitals. Save for a few conditions, such as poison or bleed damage, the Lifeward protects against any attack.

Once it’s gone, it’s gone. Health regeneration has no effect on it. Instead Lifeward must be recharged with Wardstones.

Wardstones are small devices crafted at a rune altar. They’re made from rune essence, stone and just a drop of the crafter’s blood. This last, macabre, ingredient is what allows Wardstones to forge a connection to their creator, allowing them to recharge their Lifeward at the cost to a small amount of damage to their soul, creating Soulscourge buildup.

Damage to one’s soul heals over time, so it sounds much scarier than it is.

Additionally, some moments provide a temporary increase in soul that extends beyond the person, becoming energy that the ward can absorb. Happy moments in life are the best examples of these, just little moments that make life worth living provide an excess of soul. On Ashenfall, this is most often provided from a good meal, providing just enough excess of soul to give the lifeward a little boost.
keysToEntries.Soulscourge:
A long time ago, a cataclysmic event struck Ashenfall. It split the land and poisoned the air, sending clouds of tainted dust up into the air where it was caught by Guthix's veil. Over the years this dust has seeped into the water of Ashenfall, tainting it with a dark sort of energy. Now, if someone drinks from this tainted water, and their ancestors hadn't spent their life being slowly warped by the strange anima of Ashenfall, they build up Soulscourge, as the anima contained within their soul is damaged. 

A soul is a container of anima within a person, shaped and enriched by their experiences. It is not piety, nor devotion, that grants a strong and robust soul, but adventure. Heroes, wanderers and explorers have strong and durable souls, allowing them to withstand the Soulscourge buildup for longer. 

Should someone gain enough Soulscourge buildup, they will be afflicted by the condition of the same name. Their soul rips, tears and starts to unravel, dealing constant damage until it can be healed. This can be fatal, as one needs an intact soul to survive. 

Fortunately, souls heal surprisingly quickly. Soulscourge buildup will fade over time, as the soul repairs and enriches itself with the adventures of Ashenfall. 

To avoid Soulscourge buildup from drinking water, you need only boil it at a campfire. The taint in the water can be separated from it by boiling it and letting it evaporate off, where it harmlessly disperses into the atmosphere.
keysToEntries.DirtyWater: This water will quench your thirst a little, but it contains a faint residue of a cataclysmic event that afflicted Ashenfall. As a result, there are traces of a tainted substance in the water that will damage the soul of anyone who drinks it, if they’re not a native that’s adapted to it.
keysToEntries.CleanWater: Boiling the water separates the tainted elements in the liquid, allowing you to scrape it off and enjoy a more pleasant beverage.
keysToEntries.RedberryInfusion: The juice of the berries infuses the water with a pleasant flavour. It’s easier and more refreshing to drink, offering greater hydrating properties than normal water.
keysToEntries.DwellberryInfusion: The delicious dwellberry infuses the water with a delightful flavour. It’s easier and far more refreshing than normal water. You’ll find this hydrates you much better than normal water.
keysToEntries.FerociousInfusion: Boiling away the tainted material, in the water, while infusing it with an animal bone, causes the mixture to draw in some of the primal resonance of the bone. Drinking the infusion temporarily allows the drinker to feel some of that animalistic ferocity.
keysToEntries.FleetfootedInfusion: The deer of Ashenfall are quick and nimble, able to dash across entire regions at the drop of a hat. By boiling the tainted water of Ashenfall combined with a deer’s antlers, some of this essence of swiftness is drawn into the clean water, granting anyone who drinks it a measure of that fleet-footed nature.
keysToEntries.EvasiveInfusion: The smaller creatures can be annoyingly good at getting out of harms way. When boiling water infused with a small fang, belonging to one of these critters, this evasive essence can be pulled into the clean water, allowing the drinker to benefit from it.
keysToEntries.DriedRedberries: Easier, than standard redberries, to eat on the go, tastier too.
keysToEntries.DriedDwellberries: Delicious berries. They make a great snack. Very popular among garou druids as a seasoning with their latest quarry.
keysToEntries.RatRoast: First discovered by a Lumbridge chef after a kitchen fire burned his mentor to ashes. Beneath the master chef’s hat was a deliciously roasted rat. Sure, it may not be the greatest delicacy of all time, but it’ll keep hunger at bay.
keysToEntries.Haunch: Flavourful roasted meat. A comforting meal to enjoy in the wilds of Ashenfall.
keysToEntries.GrilledMushrooms:
A great meal for a real fun guy… sorry.

Popular with terrible pun writers and villagers alike, roasted mushrooms were a staple diet across much Ashenfall. Wild Knights would forage for the best mushrooms to enjoy on their long hunts.

For those braving the poison filled Bloodblight Swamps, the fortifying effect of mushrooms made them even more popular.
keysToEntries.FriedOnions: Although the preparation leaves you weeping, and the smell makes it worse, fried onions are a delicious meal to get you through a difficult day.
keysToEntries.FlankSteak: Thick and juicy, the flank steak is more filling than you might first expect. The dense meat means each mouthful makes you fuller for longer.
keysToEntries.RedberryCrunchies: Although a crude facsimile of gnomish cooking, the redberry crunchy is a pleasant snack that will get you through the day.
keysToEntries.DwellberryCrunchies: Dwellberries on Ashenfall are heartier than their mainland counterparts and make for excellent ingredients. The Wild Knights were particularly fond of this combination, though they referred to them as “Dwellberry splats” because Olis, their inventor, wasn’t a particularly good chef.
keysToEntries.FortifyingCrunchies: The mushrooms of Ashenfall have absorbed enough magic over the years to give them special properties. A well-cooked mushroom will absorb certain toxins from whoever ingests them, making them invaluable for scouting into poison-filled areas.
keysToEntries.HearryCrunchies: Loosely based on gnomish cooking, these delicious fritters make for hearty and comforting meals.
keysToEntries.VegetableSoup: A good soup won’t just fill your stomach, it’ll lift your spirits. Warm and comforting, the taste of home. Popular across all of Gielinor by anyone with a pan and some vegetables.
keysToEntries.FortifyingSoup: It is said that Guthix created the first fortifying soup, as a gift for his chief druids. While the first few attempts created terrible food poisoning -- Guthix was a god, not a chef -- the perfected recipe soon became a favourite once he mixed in mushrooms. This was because the mushrooms absorbed much of the toxins, making it much more palatable.
keysToEntries.HeartySoup: As the Bramblemead villagers discovered heartier vegetables, their recipes improved just as much. The simple soup now felt richer, tastier and more fulfilling. A nice, hearty, meal to get them through the tough jobs on the farm.
keysToEntries.FortifyingHeartySoup: The Wild Knights would prepare this soup before heading into the poisonous Bloodblight swamp, or when facing venomous monsters. The mushrooms would absorb some of the toxins they encountered, and the soup would be filling enough to last them through the long journeys.
keysToEntries.FortifyingStew: Meat and mushrooms, an alliance that stretches back across time. Both flavours mixing together to create a unified taste explosion. On Ashenfall, the mushrooms are infused with anima, and draw toxins away from those who ingest them. Popular with anyone looking to avoid poison.
keysToEntries.HeartyStew: A thick and tasty stew, rich in flavour and nutrients. Bramblemead’s head chef, Leander, use to say that eating this stew is like sitting around a warm fire with friends, the aroma of the rich gravy filling your nostrils makes you feel like you’re home. Villagers would gather when he made a big cauldron of the stew, each of them ready for an evening with friends.
keysToEntries.RedberryGlazedRoastMeat: Delicious roasted meat, with a redberry glaze. The fruit flavour combines with the meat in a sublime manner that makes it hard to eat this slowly.
keysToEntries.RedberryGlazedFlank: A thick, delicious and juicy flank steak, glazed with subtle redberry tones. Delicious and nutritious.
keysToEntries.DwellberryGlazedRoastMeat: Dwellberries boiled down into a sticky sauce and liberally trailed across the meat. At first you might expect the combination wouldn’t work, but as you take that first mouthful, you find your tastebuds writhe in delight for this delicious combination.
keysToEntries.DwellberryGlazedFlank: A thick and juicy steak, combined with a rich and sticky dwellberry sauce. Each mouthful is like a taste explosion, and it can be difficult to hold yourself back. Orik the Bear was a Wild Knight who excelled in this dish, and other Wild Knights would seek him out at the gatherings for his culinary expertise.
