keysToEntries.MixedGrill: For the carnivore in you, a plate of meat mixed with meat.
keysToEntries.BerryCompote: Berries and more berries, what could be better? A sweet and sour sensation in each mouthful. A refreshing, yet enjoyable meal. Well, more like a dessert, but no one’s judging you.
keysToEntries.PioneerScarf: An attractive scarf, worn by those who first ventured forth into Ashenfall.
keysToEntries.PioneerCape: An embroidered cape, commemorating the first to explore the untamed wilds of Ashenfall.
keysToEntries.DragonbaneCape:
A seer once dreamed about brave warriors who walked across Ashenfall in another time. An age both past and present, where the rules of the world were different. These great heroes challenged and slew an echo of Velgar, saving a land that never was. 
While the seer’s memory of that dream fades with each passing moment, she was able to embroider the event into a cape, so that all might know of these legends from another time.
keysToEntries.Cathan:
Cathan is the first to tell you that he was raised in privilege. Wealthy family. Loving parents. Everything a growing boy could need. Yet, like for so many of us, it wasn’t enough. He craved excitement, exploration and adventure, yearning more than anything to discover the wonders of the world. 

As a child he slipped away from his family, in search of adventure and monsters to slay. He found more than he bargained for, and the lesson for his hubris cost him a leg. 

In the months that followed, the boy fell into a deep darkness. Life offered little meaning when he could barely walk a few steps. His parents, in response, hired him a tutor, so that he might rekindle that spark of adventure that had once burned so brightly. 

It worked. In Vannaka he found someone that could push him out of his melancholy. Who taught him how to wield a blade and target the weaknesses of his foes. Vannaka brought in others, who helped train him further. In Zanik, young Cathan, learned how to survive in the wilds. In Doric he learned to craft weapons and armour to take down his foes. Finally, the Wise Old Man taught him a little bit of magic, and young Cathan’s curious excitement burned brightly once more. 

When the opportunity came to explore a new land, Cathan was the first to sign up and convinced his mentors to join him. As they sailed to Ashenfall he stood at the bow of the ship smiling at the adventures ahead. 

In the chaos that followed, he’s keenly aware of that age old phrase “be careful what you wish for”.
keysToEntries.FocusedAttackPotion:
This potion streamlines your vision and hones your reflexes. Your synapses surge with clarity and you find it easier to understand the nuance of the blade.

The Wild Knights relied heavily on these potions to train their new fighters during the decline of the Guthixian faith. With fewer numbers, it became more important to teach people quickly.
keysToEntries.FocusedMiningPotion:
This potion makes it easier to swing a pickaxe. Your muscles are more relaxed and able to handle greater strain, making it easier to strike at the ore, learning the weak points.

Druids used to whip up huge batches of this for visiting dwarves, who would mix it with beer for dubious results.
keysToEntries.FocusedWoodcuttingPotion: Woodcutting is less about speed and strength, and more about precision. The focus this potion provides, lets you hone your senses, attacking the same place repeatedly along the tree trunk. The result is more efficiently felled trees and far fewer splinters.
keysToEntries.FocusedArtisanPotion: Looking and smelling suspiciously like coffee, this potion lets crafters sit down and create their favourite things. Tapestries, wood carvings, anything that requires time and focus, can be enhanced with the focus this potion provides.
keysToEntries.FocusedCookingPotion: It’s easy to forget that cooking is more than just throwing ingredients into the pot and applying fire. There are balances of flavours. Subtle blendings of ingredients to create that perfect mix. This potion assists with that by enhancing the smell and tastebuds of the chef, so they can learn just how much of an ingredient needs to be added for optimal results.
keysToEntries.FocusedConstructionPotion:
This potion is sweet, yet strong. The ingredients have stewed within the mixture for some time and allowed it to increase in potency. While it’s perfectly fine to drink it directly from the bottle, you find yourself wishing you had a nice mug to use instead.

Still, this potion makes you acutely (haha, see what I did there?) aware of angles. You can measure a plank of wood with just a few well placed glares. The inner architect in you revels at the new possibilities before you, and you find yourself planning much further ahead.
keysToEntries.FocusedRunecraftingPotion:
Ashenfall is the birthplace of runecrafting. When Guthix settled here, he discovered the strange anima pouring out of the ground, different strands of magic resonating with certain concepts and natural laws.

This potion lets you see the world as he did, just for a while. You can spot the colourful flows of anima as they shimmer in the air and you learn how better to trap them in the rune essence, creating the potential for future magic.
keysToEntries.QuarryMasterPotion:
Mining is a strenuous task. Each swing of that pickaxe makes your muscles ache and your bones quiver. Exhaustion creeps into your soul and takes root, making it take longer and longer to gather that precious ore.

This potion acts as a pick-me-up. An energising drink that makes you a monster of a miner, able to swing your pickaxe as though it barely weighed anything at all.
keysToEntries.LumberjackPotion: This potions helps you sleep all night and work all day. It gives you the feeling of well oiled elbow and shoulder joints, and it numbs the pain in your muscles that’s created from swinging an axe for long stretches at a time.
keysToEntries.LesserAttackPotion:
No one is entirely sure who invented this potion, but those without the knowledge were invariably the losers in warfare. In ancient times armies would be given doses of this potion before they were sent off to battle. It made them deadlier, faster and vastly increased their chances of victory.

The Children of Shadows used to try brew large quantities of this potion every time they went off on a mission, so that they would have the edge were they ever caught. Many a guard could attest to the power of this potion, but fortunately it didn’t make up for a general lack of melee skill.
keysToEntries.LargeWardstone: A more powerful and refined wardstone, this one allows you a much greater return on investment. When activated, it draws energy from the soul more cleanly and efficiently, reducing the impact of Soulscourge on the user.
keysToEntries.MediumWardstone: Some materials are better at resonating with souls than others. This is why a home made of better quality stones feels more comfortable than one made from pebbles. When used in wardstones, they allow for more powerful effects at no greater cost to the soul itself. Drawing out more of the ambient anima within a person, whilst minimizing the damage done.
keysToEntries.Wardstone:
Wardstones are handy tools for anyone working with wards. Armour, when created on an anima-rich continent like Ashenfall, is infused with threads of enchantment that harness natural magic to provide an extra layer of protection for the wearer.

This protection depletes upon use, as the energy within the ward is expended on the effect. But the wardstone can rectify this situation… for a price.

The stone around the wardstone is hollow and more brittle than it seems, and the rune essence is carefully laid within it along with a final ingredient – a drop of the creators own blood. This blood, and the rune essence formation, creates a sort of harmonious connection between the bearer of the wardstone and the stone itself.

When the stone is broken, it activates the rune essence and draws anima in from the nearest pure source – the soul of the user.

Souls are containers of pure anima. They grow over time from experience and adventure, the more the bearer of said soul enriches their life with activity, the stronger the soul. This means that the damage the wardstone creates, whilst not insignificant, is easily repaired through the simple passage of time. Especially on Ashenfall, where merely surviving to the next hour is an adventure.

Wardstones are great in a pinch, but one must be careful not to use too many, less the damage they deal to themselves becomes too great.
keysToEntries.LesserMagicPotion:
Although humans cannot use magic without runes, they can become quite proficient conduits for power. A wizard and a farmer, when possessing the same runes, do not produce the same results, the former far outshines the former. 

This made some farmers pretty upset. For years they had ranted to their cows and sheep about how wizards were just snooty uppity types with shiny rocks. Yet, faced with this revelation, there was no denying that a farmer and a wizard simply weren’t equals in disguise. 

One farmer wanted to level the playing field and brought in a local witch who was had a grudge against the snooty men in blue robes. 

Between the two of them, they found a way to improve a person’s connection to the magic within them via a magical potion. Those who drank it cast spells more efficiently and with greater skill than they ever could before. 

The farmer wanted to use this new advantage to put the wizards in their place, raze down the tower and claim magic a tool of the people. Unfortunately, the witch saw a different future, one that sparkled with glittering gold coins. 

She died happily, wealthier than she had ever dreamed, surrounded by adoring young suiters who wanted a piece of her inheritance. The farmer died after being trampled to death by cows that were tired of his grumbling.
keysToEntries.Whetstone:
The blade is not just a weapon in the wilds of Ashenfall, it’s a tool. Sometimes it’s the difference between seeing tomorrow and having a very bad today. 

Dwarves have always understood the importance of properly maintaining one’s equipment. They turned the care for their weapons into a ritual of almost religious significance. Blades would be cleaned, serviced and sharpened so that they might cut through deadlier dangers and challenge greater threats. 

The whetstone is a simple device, but one that served as a backbone of this ritual. A smith would learn to gently sharpen the blade to the thinnest edge possible, treating it almost like a competition. They would boast and brag with each other about the sharpness of their blades. 

A popular dwarven myth speaks of the Prime Whetstone, capable of sharpening a blade to the point that no armour or metal could withstand it. Sadly, or perhaps thankfully, no evidence exists to provide any truth to the myth.
keysToEntries.MagicFocus:
The wizard Mallory was never as good as he had hoped to be. He was proficient in spellcasting and his rote memory was excellent, but the power of his spells always fell a little short. He advanced through the ranks mostly through technicality, but he never quite earned the respect of his peers. 

That was until the day he created the focus. Mallory had always been good at making things. He enjoyed the simpler magic of crafting, and he found you could weave small threads of magic into the construction of something, breathing just a little bit more life and wonder into it than before. True craftsmanship, he believed, carried a little bit of the creator’s soul in it. 

The focus proved this theory and elevated it further. By creating a focus and subconsciously imbuing it with a bit of his essence, it would create a smoother, more fluid, channel of magic through the body, which created spells of greater potency. 

Tragically, after sharing his knowledge with the other wizards, he was murdered by his mentor. Who then took his knowledge and claimed it as his own. Karma is a stern judge, however, and the offending wizard later found himself being swallowed whole and digested by a dragon. 

Just punishment, I’m sure Mallory would agree.
keysToEntries.PlagueKing:
Weakness: Crush
Resistance: Stab
Immune: Poison

When a fungal rat lives too long, it can consume so much pestilence and decay that it bloats beyond the size of its weaker kin. In this state it absorbs ambient anima, becoming stronger and deadlier, a monarch of its kind.

The plague king is bane to the healthy. It spreads sickness wherever it goes, becoming a terrible disease against the living.

It was the duty of the Wild Knights to slay any fungal rat in sight, to make sure that a plague king never had a chance to develop. With the passing of the Wild Knights of Bramblemead, the world finds itself in terrible danger, should this beast ever learn how to swim.
keysToEntries.RatKing:
Weakness: Stab
Resistance: Crush

The rat king is a giant rat that has consumed the hearts of other rats. A monstrous creature infused with the fear, rage and hatred of its own kind. The magical essence in the hearts infused the rat king, causing it to swell to monstrous size and imbuing it with a terrible hunger it can never sate.

Legend speaks of a rat king so large, that it threatened the entire village of Bramblemead. It was slain by a brief alliance of the Saradominist Paladins and the Guthixian Wild Knights, but not without significant casualties.

The beasts heart was burned over a great pyre in the village, so that no rats might consume any of its remains and threaten a successor rat monarch.
keysToEntries.VaultArchon:
Weakness: Crush
Resistance: None
Immune: Poison, Bleed, Burning

Rasthin was never fond of just leaving things to chance. She always saw a need to create bigger, deadlier, weapons with which to destroy her foes. With the dragons she encouraged Lacrussa’s experiments, searching for ways to make them into a more potent force. With the vault guardians she pushed them further still.

The archon is a marvel of dragonkin science. Infused with enough magical force to level a small village, if left uncontained. The magic that runs through its circuits acts as a connection to all the other guardians, motivating and animating them by the archon’s will.

At the height of their power, these creatures could out-strategise the most cunning generals. Fortunately, time has dulled this wits and their reflexes, reducing them to a strength only deadly to a village, rather than a nation.
keysToEntries.GoblinRunt:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: None

Goblin society is not one of equals. The strong do not guard the weak, and the vulnerable should expect to be exploited at every turn. Those too weak to stand, die. Those not strong enough to earn respect, receive nothing but scorn.

So it is for the runts of goblin society. They survive birth and they live their lives grabbing what sustenance they can from the world around them. They scour the ruins of farms and villages, desperately looking for weak creatures to cook and eat. A diet of scrawny rats, sickened kebbits and on the really desperate days, a vegetable or two.

The runts of Brynmoor expect nothing and receive less. It is the dream of each of them to be able to prove themselves worthy of the respect of their peers. They must win a battle, show they can fight and defend themselves. Might proves right, after all, and they are so very tired of being wrong. So they hurl themselves at any passing adventurer, in the hope of a swift kill that might end their life of quiet shame.
keysToEntries.GoblinBruiser:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: None

Might makes right and these goblins are never wrong.

The leaders of the goblin tribes by strength alone, the bruisers enjoy nothing more than a life of bullying other goblins around. They are assumed to be the smartest, because if you tell them they’re wrong, you’ll get a fist in the face. They’re the most charismatic, because if you don’t like what they’re saying… well… fist and face return once more. In fact most of their skillset consists of fists approaching faces at high velocity.

They are one of the few goblins with brains enough to wedge a large sheet of metal between their vulnerable internal organs and the person poking at them with a sharp object. So in goblin society that makes them an unrivalled genius.
keysToEntries.VaultOverlord:
Weakness: Crush
Resistance: None
Immune: Poison, Bleed, Burning

A pride of Brakka’s ingenuity, the Vault Overlord towers over the other vault guardians as a shiny example of one dragonkin’s ego. Larger and more physically powerful than the other guardians, the Overlord was a siege engine put to work as a bodyguard. 

It was set to guard Brakka’s most prized treasures. Objects he kept in the vaults, so that none but he might gaze upon their beauty. 

In time the other dragonkin learned of the Overlord and created versions of their own, setting them to guard their secrets against any that might dare pilfer them. 

In the time of the dragon uprising, the Overlords could have proved a useful soldier against Kuldra’s brood. Yet the arrogance and shortsightedness, of their dragonkin masters, meant that these walking siege weapons would never see the light of day.
keysToEntries.Movement:
Back in my day movement was much more civilized, everyone worked in neat little squares, moving entire steps at a time. Yet modern adventurers feel a much greater need for precision, yearning to swap between tiny steps and enormous leaps.

Here on Ashenfall you can walk, run, jump and crouch around to your hearts content. Certain spells can also allow you to jump further, or even teleport short distances.

Efficient, yes, but it’s just not as elegant as the grid-like movement of my day.
keysToEntries.HealthAndStamina:
Sadly, most of us aren’t immortal. We are each made from squishy bags of flesh, that don’t do well when stabbed, sliced or otherwise turned into a pincushion. In Dragonwilds, we represent the stability of our squishy fleshy bits by our Hitpoints.

When we’re injured, our Hitpoints go down. If they reach zero, why I’m afraid that’s rather it. Well, until you resurrect of course – handy thing that. You can recover Hitpoints with certain potions, or by making sure you’re well fed.

Stamina, is the measure of how tired we are. The lower your stamina, the less energy you have and you need a little bit of energy to handle most tasks. Attacking, dodging and skilling all use stamina when you engage in them. If your stamina is at zero, you won’t be able to perform the action you are attempting to do.

Fortunately, stamina recovers over time, especially if you keep yourself well hydrated.
keysToEntries.DeathAndGravestones:
Look, there’s no sugar coating this, but one day we’re all going to die. Especially you. Especially here. Ashenfall is a violent and dangerous place, where even the chickens want you dead.

When your Hitpoints reaches zero, that’s it, you’re dead. Fortunately, unless you’re particularly hardcore, you’ll find that death isn’t the end you expect. For some reason, your soul is preserved, and rather than journeying to the world beyond, you’ll wake up again with only your dignity suffering any permanent damage.

If you’ve claimed a bed for yourself, you’ll return to there on your death. Otherwise, you’ll find yourself back at the old Saradomin temple.

Sadly, whatever mysterious force saves you from eternity, appears to have a bad back and whilst they’re happy to lug you all the way to safety, your stuff is another matter. When you die, you’ll keep your runes, quest items and the clothes you have equipped, but everything else will be kept in a gravestone awaiting your return.

If you can make your way back to your gravestone, you’ll be able to reclaim all your lost equipment and resources, and continue your journey.

In hard mode, you can only have one gravestone at a time. So, if you die a second time, before reclaiming your gear, you’ll lose everything your first grave was protecting.
keysToEntries.Stability:
I don’t know about you, but I collapse easily without the proper foundations. Well, buildings are no different. Unsupported, you can only build so high, without your new constructions falling in on themselves.

You can build higher with stronger materials, or by carefully placing support structures, but be aware that, much like me, your buildings can only be supported so far before their inner demons destroy them… err… I mean before gravity wins the battle.
keysToEntries.Cosiness:
Home is where the heart is. Which is a horrifying statement when you think about it.

Still, home has its advantages, and cosiness is one of those. When you build a home and claim a bed, you’ll find that life just feels much easier to manage. Specifically, you’ll find that your Hydration, Endurance and Sustenance bars become much easier to manage.

While you remain within the comfort of your home, you’ll need to eat less, drink less and rest less. Meaning that you can craft to your hearts content, without worrying about little things like starving to death.
keysToEntries.Attack:
Unfortunately, conflict is inevitable on Ashenfall. You will need to hit things with clubs, stab them with daggers and impale your foes with a volley of carefully crafted arrows.

There are three main combat styles, magic, ranged and melee, each one has its strengths and its weaknesses.

Magic attacks are capable of generating powerful side effects, air magic can knock something back, fire magic can burn your enemies to a crisp. However, you will need a steady supply of runes to keep casting spells.

Ranged attacks can snipe enemies from afar, but you’ll need to make sure you don’t run out of arrows.

Melee attacks get up close and personal, but you won’t need to worry about ammunition to lay the smackdown on that uppity little goblin – not Zanik – who’s been giving you lip.

When engaged in battle, keep an eye on your stamina, as each attack will cost you some of it. As you level up your combat skills, you’ll be able to deal longer combo chains to more efficiently use your stamina.
keysToEntries.Defence:
Armour will make life easier, reducing the damage you receive. The better the armour, the greater the damage reduction. Be sure to upgrade your gear at every opportunity. 

You may also want to avoid damage from time to time. A well-timed block will negate all damage by parrying the attack, making it easier to counterattack. While, if like me you prefer to avoid a knife in the stomach, you can always dodge an attack and get out of the way.

Perhaps the best way to protect one’s most precious internal organs, is to get out of the way of an attacker before they can stab you. Dodging is the key to this. Dash, roll and scream incoherently as you whiz away from an enemy’s attack before I can reach you. You can dodge in multiple directions, so make sure to keep an eye on your surroundings so you don't dodge off a cliff or anything. 

I did that once. Not fun.
keysToEntries.DamageTypesWeaknessAndResistance:
I have been punched, stabbed, and shot with arrows, over the course of my many years of adventuring and each injury feels like a unique moment. Damage, as you might image, comes in all shapes and sizes. Different weapons just hit differently.

Some weapons stab. Some slash away flesh. Others break bones with mighty crushing movements. Ranged attacks pierce you with unreasonably sharp arrows, and magic strikes you down with the primordial elements, air, fire, earth and water.

We humans are vulnerable to each of these in equal measure, but this isn’t true for everything. Some creatures have tougher skin, but bones more susceptible to crushing. Others might burn easily, while the chill of water barely touches them.

It’s important to learn the weaknesses and resistances of your enemies, so that you can make sure you’re dealing the sort of damage that really takes them out.
keysToEntries.StatusEffects:
There’s more than just bruising that awaits the unprepared adventurer, Ashenfall’s also home to a number of different conditions we in the biz like to refer to as status effects.

These effects, once inflicted, cause a continuing problem for a while. You could spend too much time in the Bloodblight Swamp and become poisoned, slowly losing health as time drags on.

Certain creatures and weapons may inflict you with nasty cuts that cause bleeding, leading to a large amount of damage if not dealt with quickly.

Each Status Effect is its own special little problem, and it’s a good idea to make sure you pay close attention to any that you run across.
keysToEntries.Durability:
Nothing lasts forever, just ask my knees. Time was they were spry and full of life, now they grate together like an angry pair of hedgehogs getting in a fight. Equipment is similarly affected. Weapons, armour and tools have a limited amount of durability before they stop working. Think of it like hitpoints for inanimate objects.

You can repair an object at a crafting bench, or you can use certain spells to recover durability. Once an item breaks, it won’t be destroyed, but it won’t be useful until you can repair it. So make sure to keep an eye on your gear’s durability.
keysToEntries.Food:
We are all slaves to the beast within the belly. Its gurgling roar urges us ever onward to consume, consume, consume. At least that’s the excuse I use when people ask about my ever-expanding midriff.

Still, we need food to survive, even the most restrictive (sane) diet plans acknowledge that. Ashenfall has a wealth of options, some delicious and others… well… less so. Food will increase your Sustenance meter, which makes you healthier and heartier. Some food will also provide special benefits, such as resistances to poison.

The higher quality the food, the longer its effects last, so make sure you’re always making the best meals you can.
keysToEntries.Infusions:
Water is great, drink lots of it, but sometimes it can get a little… repetitive. Dull even. If you find yourself in that situation then don’t fall to despair, because there’s a solution: Infusions.

Due to some cataclysmic nonsense on the continent, water on Ashenfall is infected with some sort of taint or corruption. Boiling the water seems to remove this imperfection somehow, but it also allows you the opportunity to spice up your drinks.

Adding certain things to water as you boil it, such as berries, bones or even antlers, will provide additional benefits. Fruity infusions are more hydrating, filling your Hydration meter faster. Certain animal parts may also provide other benefits, such as improved battle prowess or more efficient dodging.

Try experimenting with different infusions to create drinks more tailored to your playstyle.
keysToEntries.Perks:
Think of perks a little like status effects that don’t want to kill you. They’re friendly, helpful even. Certain foods, drinks, potions and other things, may give you temporary benefits. These benefits, called perks, might be resistances to certain effects, or perhaps they’ll give you an edge in combat.

There are several perks to discover and mastering them will make your adventures much easier.
keysToEntries.Potions:
Once you’ve crafted yourself a cauldron, you’ll have access to the wonderful world of experimental herblore. Why, I remember the good old days of chucking random ingredients into the pot and hoping they didn’t explode.

Lost a good few friends in those days. Fond memories.

Potions can provide you a multitude of benefits. Some will knit together broken flesh, others might provide you with resistances to the harmful effects of Ashenfall. Still others might grant you temporary perks to make your skilling activities easier.

You’ll need herbs to make potions. They can be found all over the region, but especially in swampier biomes. Combine herbs with anything you can get your hands on to discover the wonderful benefits of herblore.
keysToEntries.TheStorySoFar:
It’s not every day that an entire new continent appears out of nowhere, but Ashenfall really wanted to be different to everywhere else. They say that the clouds just parted and there it was, an entire region, unveiled as if by magic. 

Which, of course it was. 

The lure of exploration sparked a great deal of interest. Wealthy backers reached out to adventurers across the realm and promised them a land of wonder, mystery and undiscovered riches. 

“Riches” appealed immediately to the old hero Dionysius, aka the Wise Old Man, who never felt that he really got the life of luxury he deserved for all his decades of selfless heroism. He met with the backers and they hastily agreed to make him the leader of this expedition. More accurately he told them he would be the leader, and none of them had a strong enough shield to disagree. 

Seeing it as a learning opportunity, the Wise Old Man reached out to his latest student, a young man named Cathan, and encouraged him to join up. After all, what better training could you offer than the wild and dangerous unknown? 

In turn, Cathan encouraged other adventurers, including his other tutors, Zanik – the cave goblin adventurer, and Vannaka – a slayer master with a need to prove himself. At first they turned him down, but stories and legends of these new lands soon reached the two of them and they could not turn their curiosity away. 

Finally, the dwarf Doric, found himself lured into the expedition, after a very foolish night’s drinking. It had seemed like such a good idea when his brain was swimming in mead, but in the morning after, once he’d signed himself up, he found himself overcome with regret. 

Still, along with a small group of brave adventurers, the expedition members set forth, sailing north from the mainland, across the ocean in search of new horizons. 

It didn’t go according to plan. 

As they reached the shores of Ashenfall, and the lush green region of Brynmoor, the sky darkened, and the air filled with the deafening sounds of beating wings. Dragons, a legion of them, descended from the sky and tore the expedition fleet apart. As the fires tore through the ships, the dragons laughed. 

“Queen Kuldra bids you welcome!” they sneered as the expedition turned to ash. 

Only a few members made it to shore, dragging themselves onto the land, exhausted, beaten and bruised. For days they survived by the skin of their teeth, relying on the strange magic that this continent provided. Anima was freely available, bursting out of the very earth itself. Magic, pretty much on tap. 

When they found the remnants of human civilization, it raised questions. Who had lived here? What had happened to them? Though the dark shape of the dragon, Velgar, in the sky, felt like the answer to the last question. 

Within a temple on the top of the hill, overlooking the endless blue seas, they found the remnants of an ancient lodestone and this sparked the opportunity they needed. After repairing the lodestone the Wise Old Man reached out with a magical message to the waiting explorers in the mainland: “Come to Ashenfall, be ready for trouble.”

You didn’t have much going for you on the mainland. A series of unfortunate events had robbed you of home, wealth and status. You had nothing to your name but the clothes on your back and when you heard the message you knew you had nothing to lose. 

This adventure offered a new start, a chance to make something of yourself. So you took the opportunity and answered the call. 

Ashenfall awaits!

keysToEntries.BronzeCrowssbow:
The members of the Wild Knights who trained with melee weapons, generally found themselves the victors in friendly sparring matches against their companions. The reasoning was simple – shields. A shield could turn aside blade, arrow and spell, with equal skill and it left their enemy vulnerable to counterattack. This sense of superiority began to change from friendly competition, into a sense of smugness capable of souring many friendships.

Roliker Hallister was particularly obnoxious. His skill with sword and shield was admittedly impressive. At the age of twenty he could best even some of the commanders of the Wild Knights in single combat. Healthy pride twisted into smug egoism, which then rotted down into unpleasant superiority. He was rude. He was dismissive. He was pretty much an obnoxious nightmare to be around.

Which is why he was very surprised by the bolt in his shoulder, and why no one else seemed too inclined to help him out. Vanida Mox was a skilled archer and inventor. She was quick to learn, quick to study and she had the enviable ability to see when something was wrong and work to find a solution. Popular with the other knights, because she would work with them, share accolades, and stand for what the order truly meant – a barrier between the world and the wild.

As Roliker’s ego grew and grew, she realised that the archers in the order were at a significant disadvantage in close combat. At range, they were obviously superior, but as all the sparring matches were held in the courtyard, there was precious little distance available. So she began working on a device that would deliver the power of an arrow but allowing one hand free to hold a shield.

It was very effective.

She named it a “cross-bow”, because she was very very angry when she unloaded it into the cloud of smugness known as Roliker. Later that name would be attributes to the mechanical structure of the device, but now you know better.
keysToEntries.IronCrossbow:
Iron turned the war against the garou more than some people would like to admit. They would claim superior training, tactics, and skills of their soldiers, but the truth is far simpler. Iron was strong and iron weapons were brutal.

Ranged weapons proved effective against the garou leadership, bound as they were by strict codes of honour. A thane was a deadly opponent up close, but if you could pierce their internal organs before they were within a swing’s reach, then they were no more dangerous than any other beast. It is perhaps no surprise then, that there was a race to create weapons to challenge them.

The people of Brynmoor had learned of a Wild Knight invention, a weapon that would allow one to deliver powerful ranged attacks, whilst still allowing the bearer to wield a shield. They took this design, studied and refined it and then upgraded it to include the power of iron weapons.

Garou numbers dropped rapidly as a result, turning the war heavily against them. In their desperation, the garou turned to their goddess and she, in turn, sought allies with deadlier weapons still.
keysToEntries.BluriteCrossboq:
For centuries alchemists have yearned to create gold. To transmute base metals into something pure and shiny. While gold is the goal, there are other, stranger metals, along the way, and what could be more bizarre than a bar of purest blue.

Blurite is defined by its colour. It’s in the very name. Yet it offers no real magical, or alchemical value beyond other metals. Artistic, perhaps, it was favoured by poets, bards and artisans of more eccentric tastes, but it still offered little in the way of superior weaponry.

That was until Gothren Actillian took a crack at it. He was an blacksmith, obsessed with the less desired metals and materials. He would hunt for hours in the darkest caverns for slivers of blue, ignoring glittering silver and gold. To him the metal was special, radiant and beautiful. The hues generated a joy in him that he could not describe, and he longed to craft it into something befitting its splendour. He was delighted when he found the ore glittering away in a hidden dragonkin vault in Ghornfell's central region. 

He was never any good with a sword, and his magical prowess wouldn’t frighten a kebbit. Yet crossbows, those he could wield. He was a skilled marksman, and could knock an apple from a chinchompa’s head before it exploded.

The crossbow, blue as the ocean, caused much mockery from his companions, yet their mockery soon died as he speared a bolt through the heart of a dragon. Something all of their weapons had failed to do.

He was just disappointed that the dragon’s scales were green and went searching for that ever elusive blue.
keysToEntries.BronzeCrossbowLimbs:
The complicated mechanisms of the crossbow sparked a small technological revolution in Bramblemead. Such devices had once been the domain of the dwarves, who guarded their advanced techniques to maintain their smithing dominion. However, soon human smiths found themselves creating these complex trigger mechanisms.

There were casualties, certainly. Some mechanisms tightened strings too greatly, snapping back with a force that could cause serious harm.

One notable example involved the pet belonging to the village priest, a curious feline with absolutely no sense of self-preservation. The cat entered the workshop, as she so often did, and took it upon herself to knock everything she could to the floor. Unfortunately, in doing so, she triggered the release mechanism being worked on and quickly learned that cats are not meant to fly.
keysToEntries.IronCrossbowLimbs:
Following certain incidents with cats and crossbow limbs, a superstition began to build in Bramblemead about the danger of crossbows and felines. It was considered bad luck to house a cat in the same home as a crossbow, with reasoning ranging from the safety of the cat, to the safety of the human population should the cats rise up and develop opposable thumbs.

Whilst harmless at first, when the need for crossbows grew due to the conflict with the garou, cats found themselves with fewer and fewer homes willing to house them. Over the years this led to domestic cats being a rare sight in Bramblemead.

It also created a deep-seated resentment in the cats, and they plotted their revenge. Most would tell you that cats had nothing to do with the destruction of Bramblemead and the Brynmoor settlements, yet if you ask a cat, they will simply purr with a sinister grin on their face.
keysToEntries.BluriteCrossbowLimbs:
Not all cats are terrified of crossbows, though most of the Bramblemead population quickly learned to fear them. Some cats could see their merit but were painfully aware of their human pet’s limited imagination.

Mittens was the lifelong owner of Gothren Actillian. A blacksmith’s cat, he had learned how to keep the forge fires perfectly hot by playfully jumping on the bellows periodically – something his pet seemed to enjoy. He knew the right length of time a sword should be cooled for and would make sure his human stuck to that timing by offering useful alarms made of glorious clattering things.

But blurite? That was a new one. Mittens could see there was little value to the metal, beyond some weird human aesthetic that he did not understand, yet there was some potential to the construction. With carefully timed meows, and some creative knocking over of reagents, Mittens was able to engineer a crossbow of superior design, worthy of his cherished pet.
keysToEntries.BronzeCrossbowBolts:
Not a lot can be said about small spears of metal to be launched at high speeds into your opponent. They do exactly what you think they’ll do to the target, and it’s rarely pretty.

Guards would buy crossbow bolts in bulk from the village blacksmiths, giving him a steady income. This sadly had the side effect of making the village blacksmith the number one most regularly broken into location.
keysToEntries.IronCrossbowBolts:
One sad part of human nature is that they will constantly strive to craft deadlier and deadlier weapons. Iron crossbow bolts were harder than their bronze counterpart, capable of penetrating the toughest garou armour.

Obrimex, of the garou, once tried to replicate these weapons. He crafted little sharpened tubes of iron that he felt would match their power. Alas, the garou hadn’t yet crafted crossbow technology, and throwing these little metal darts largely proved to be irritating rather than dangerous.
keysToEntries.BluriteCrossbowBolts:
It was Mittens that learned how to turn blurite into crossbow bolts. His pet, the blacksmith Gothren, had continued to rely on iron and bronze to get the job done, but Mittens had realised that there was great potential in this strange blue metal. He would ruin sand moulds, crafted by Gothren to forge blurite bars, making them appear as long thin strips. It took more ruined moulds than the cat had hoped for his owner to realise that blurite bolts would match their namesake crossbow.

Whether they were more effective, is of no value to a cat, appearance is, after all, everything.
keysToEntries.EnchantedBronzeCrossbowBolts:
Magic was never enough. Oh, sure, you could change bones into delicious peaches, you could summon water out of thin air, you could call down fire upon your foes and laugh as their flesh turns to ash (if you’re that way inclined). Yet, even so, it was never enough. There was always more death to deal, more damage to inflict, more pain to spread and the priests of Brynmoor wanted more of it.

They wanted to see the garou crushed. Wanted to see them begging as the last of their pups expired beneath Saradomin’s purifying flame. So, they commissioned the wizards and alchemists to find new and innovative ways to summon suffering.

Crossbow bolts proved to be an interesting revelation. By affixing gemstones to the tips of these weapons, not only could they inflict deadly wounds on their enemies, but they could also inflict debilitating pain so powerful that the opponent would be stunned, making the next attack much easier.

The priests were very happy with this discovery. The so called “heretics” within Brynmoor were less so.
keysToEntries.EnchantedBluriteCrossbowBolts:
No one is entirely sure who thought to enchant blurite crossbow bolts in Brynmoor. The earliest references talk about Ignatius Wormwood, a failed apprentice wizard with too much time on his hands.

The story goes that he was challenged by one of the Knights to a duel, over some spat about a maiden or other. Evidently Ignatius may have been a failure in his robes, but worryingly successful out of them, if you get my gist.

Now, the knight was a large and imposing man -- referred to by his compatriots as the immovable boulder -- and angry as he was, he had no intention of killing Ignatius, so he gave him a simple challenge instead. If Ignatius could knock him down, he’d win the duel, otherwise the Knight would claim victory and see Ignatius punished for his mischiefs.

When Ignatius pulled the crossbow from his robes, the knight was concerned but knew that his armour and shield could stand against them. Yet, when the bolt impacted the knight’s pristine iron armour, he found himself hurled to the floor and the wind knocked out of him.

Gracious in defeat the knight let Ignatius go, only for the wizard to wander into the next person he had wronged.
keysToEntries.EnchantedIronBolts:
Stone remembers, wood forgets, iron holds a grudge. So goes the Imcamdo saying. A reference to how much stronger metal weapons were than wood and stone. Still, with magic, this adage can become painfully true.

The first enchanted iron bolts on Ashenfall were crafted by Lina Hesperian, one of the Amalgamated. She had hair that flowered like a waterfall down her spine. Her eyes were crystalline lakes, sparkling in the light, and her smile was as refreshing as any spring. She enchanted the bolts so that they might carry the gift of water, washing away infection for a clean and gentle kill. She made them for the hunters, so that they might claim their quarries with as little suffering as possible.

She met the end that far too many Amalgamated suffered. Alone, frightened, in the cold and the dark of an alley, her blood seeping from the knife wound in her chest. As she gave her last breath, she cursed the world for it’s cruelty, and the iron listened.

Now, each enchanted iron bolt taps into that grudge, inflicting a reminder of her suffering on any that feel the bolt’s sting.
keysToEntries.BluriteOre:
Sometimes colours just want to manifest themselves. There is the sweetness of orange. The cowardice of yellow, and the peaceful serenity of a summery green. Blue, tired with only being linked to sorrow and sadness, sought a physical existence. One that could be shaped into new and wonderous things, and so blurite was born.

A metal that is so very, very, blue.

It’s in the name. After all, it’s blue, right?
keysToEntries.BluriteBar: Blue concentrated and refined, melted down and reforged into a cuboid shape so that you may carry around purest blue with you wherever you go.
keysToEntries.WeaponPoison:
Used in crafting Poison Arrows or to coat your equipped Melee Weapon with Poison for a short time.

There are times in life where you really want someone to regret ever meeting you. It could be a long-held grudge, it could be on the battlefield, or it could be in a never-ending meeting that could so easily have been an email but has now persisted past your lunchbreak and you had so been looking forward to that sushi… ahem… sorry, where was I? Oh, yes, pain!

Poison is sometimes considered to be a coward’s weapon. Personally, I prefer to think of it as the smart weapon choice, after all, honour is only worth anything if you’re the winner of the battle. A corpse with honour still rots in the dirt. With a blade coated in poison, you can almost guarantee the death of your enemies, all it takes is a single nick and their fate is sealed.

An investment well worth making, if you ask me.
keysToEntries.PoisonedFangArrows:
The Children of Shadows loved using these. The very notion of bone and poison combined as a weapon; it touched some deep dark part of themselves and filled them with a malicious sort of glee.

Alas such weapons proved relatively useless in any secret campaigns against the Saradominists of Bramblemead. Certainly, they could assassinate a baker if they needed to, but against a knight it would just ping harmlessly off their armour.

Still, they made for good talking points at the secretive meetings, making enjoyably sinister table-decorations that paired well with the wine and cheeses.
keysToEntries.PoisonedBronzeArrows:
The Wild Knights were not above poison, any edge in a battle was worth having. Poison meant that you could take down a dangerous beast, even if your initial shot wasn’t a mortal one.

The druids among the order felt little concern over its use either, after all nature is filled with wielders of poison and so the Wild Knight archers were simply emulating nature’s tools.
keysToEntries.PoisonedIronArrows:
Lougrim abhorred the use of poison on arrows, she felt it cheapened the hunt. The mages could wield it in their spells, but a hunter should produce smooth, quick kills, creating as little suffering as possible.

Most garou, in turn, would at first refuse to use poisons on their arrows. They followed her teachings, and they hunted swiftly and cleanly. After Lougrim’s fall it became clear just how foolish it was to seek such a merciful path, their new High Queen had made it clear that they should use all tools at their disposal.
keysToEntries.Kwuarm:
This herb has travelled far to deliver its malicious benefits to the people of Ashenfall. Its name, in Dwarvish, means “warm death” since it causes a terrible burning fever if the sap is consumed.

Crushed down, so that it’s sap and oils can be easily accessed, this herb is a requirement in some of the deadliest and most dangerous poisonous mixtures.
keysToEntries.PoisonIchor:
Dropped by poisonous enemies.

Nature is full of malicious little critters who carry toxins. Snakes, spiders, politicians, each of them has ways to make other creatures suffer.

This ichor is prized by poisoners and alchemists, as it can be used to create deadly concoctions that will give someone, or something, a really bad day.
keysToEntries.WeaponBarbs:
Used to craft Barbed Arrows or to coat your equipped melee weapon with barbs, causing Bleed buildup for a short duration.

Sometimes you just really want to hurt something. It happens. We each have that little voice that tells us to make something bleed, just a little.

Well, blacksmiths thought to themselves “what if we listen to that voice?” and created these little doohickies. Barbs for your weapons, so that they may tear, rend and otherwise force your opponents to bleed with every strike.
keysToEntries.BarbedBronzeArrows:
The barbed bronze arrow was the ammunition of choice for the hunting party of Lord Gawurin, the regent of Ashenfall. He and his hunting party would head out into the woods alongside a small contingent of White Knight bodyguards. The lord would hunt deer, boars and other game there, but he wasn’t the most accurate shot. 

This arrow was designed for bloodletting, meaning he only needed any hit to begin the prey’s downfall. A single strike would start them bleeding, slowing them down and making them easier to hunt. The hunt outcome was all but inevitable. Poor sport, some would whisper, but the lord cared only for the end result. 

It’s perhaps fitting, then, that Lord Gawurin would be hunted with his own arrows on the night of Falling Embers.
keysToEntries.BarbedIronArrow:
The first of these weapons was not commissioned for war. Nor was it commissioned for hunting. Instead, this most deadly arrow was created for nothing more than vengeance.

Against a pigeon.

Lord Gawurin was the ruler of Brynmoor, as appointed by the priests of Saradomin. He wore expensive garments of the finest materials, adorned with glittering jewels and gold chain. So, he was most affronted when a single pigeon determined that such a garment was the perfect restroom choice.

The pigeon's audacity was unforgivable. Day after day, Lord Gawurin's garments were defiled by this feathered fiend. His anger grew, fuelling a desire for revenge that consumed him. He commissioned an arrow, a weapon of brutal design, with a single purpose- to make the pigeon suffer.

With a sinister smile, he summoned the most skilled hunter, a woman who had brought down countless foes, and ordered her to end the pigeon's reign of terror.

She stood at the top of the castle, her bow steady, her aim true. The tension in the air was palpable as she prepared to unleash the arrow of vengeance upon the pigeon. The world seemed to hold its breath in anticipation.

She missed.
keysToEntries.FireOil:
Used to craft Fire arrows.

Some people just like to watch the world burn… and you can too, with these new handy fire oils! That’s right, just lob one of these beauties at your enemies and watch them ignite. Enjoy their screams as their skin boils away and breathe in the delightful aroma of a fresh barbecue!

Fire oil, like snake oil, was once the creation of a travelling merchant to Ashenfall. She had journeyed to Brynmoor alongside some of the Saradominist settlers, and with her she brought pungent unguents, elucidating elixirs, marvellous medicines, and weapons of localised destruction.

When her wares summoned the call for refunds, she would dazzle the onlookers with a sudden fiery display and then disappear into the alleyways until she had been forgotten. She would then return in one of a staggering array of disguises, until the tragic day when her wagon hit a pothole during a quick getaway and her many, many, bottles of fire oils decided to sing the song of their people.

Fortunately, even though she and everything she owned, tragically found itself reduced to smouldering atoms, her recipe lived on and found more conventional uses.
keysToEntries.FireBronzeArrows:
Deadly arrows made deadlier with fire. Each arrow burns with the fury reserved for the most unpleasant parts of Infernus. They sear, they singe, and they melt the flesh, turning their victim into little more than ashes floating away on the wind.

In the days of the great war against the Garou, the priests of Saradomin encouraged the use of these weapons. They saw it not just as a means of eradicating the hated beastmen, but to purify the world from their taint. Lougrim was a false goddess, in their eyes, and her followers had to be cleansed from existence.

Modern scholars would doubtless be confused at their monotheistic mindset, considering that the literal god of war, Bandos, walked beside them into battle. The mental gymnastics employed by the Brynmoor priesthood, could have rivalled any athlete in the Gielinor Games.
keysToEntries.FireIronArrows:
Rasthin was the master of fire. It was the gift she had stolen from the “soul of the world” as she called it. She could use the inner flame to inspire confidence or rouse people into a great fervour. The fire could reveal secrets, forcing truth from those who stood before her. But, more practical than all of that was one simple truth, that fire could burn.

There were many a dragon that tried to battle Rasthin directly, believing their dragonfire could destroy her. Their bones litter Ashenfall. It was only Queen Kuldra herself who could challenge the dragonkin, and theirs was a battle that left scars in the lands for centuries.

These arrows borrow a piece of Rasthin’s power. Able to inspire only by the fear they kindle in the wielder’s enemies. Yet, kindle they do, for those who face these flames will surely feel echoes of the fury wrought by the dragonkin lord of fire.
keysToEntries.Naptha:
Distilled from Swamp Tar and Charcoal.

Once known as “thick water”, naptha is liquid fire in waiting. It takes little to bring this substance to ignition, even too bright a sunny day can bring the raging flame from within.

As such, this material is widely sought by anyone with a strong desire to set things aflame. Pyromaniacs, alchemists and dragon cultists alike yearn for this liquid.
keysToEntries.Shrapnel:
Dropped by Bleed inflicting enemies.

Small collections of sharp little objects. Stone, metal, bone, each of these combines to create a little bit of hate in the palm of your hand. The brutal points of these bits of shrapnel can tear the skin and rend the flesh, making them perfect for crafting weapons to make things bleed.
keysToEntries.BluriteDeposit: A rock containing the most blue ore you've ever seen.
keysToEntries.SteakAndEggs: Some meals require silence. As you chew each mouthful, you reminisce on times past. On loved ones lost. On friends remembered. On moments that bring a smile to your face – simple and uncomplicated, without the weight of the world’s burdens crushing down on you. This is one of those meals.
keysToEntries.SweetVegBall:
Popular among gnomes, this sticky ball of fruits and vegetables is a combination of savoury and sweet that almost feels like it shouldn’t work. Yet it does.

There is a crispness to the outer shell, savoury and salty, which gives way to a delicious fruity sweetness beneath.

Gnomish ambassadors would create large numbers of these sweet treats, piled high on silver platters, to delight visiting dignitaries at lavish receptions. Diplomats would often joke about being spoiled by such treats. Which is ironic really, as these spoil very quickly, so enjoy them now while you can.
keysToEntries.CheekyFryUp:
The grease. The sizzling scents. The feeling of your arteries withering just from a single glance at the dish. Is there anything in life as close to the divine as a nice, cheeky fry up?

Popular among those who have consumed a few too many gnomish cocktails the night before, a cheeky fry up is hailed as a great cure for the pounding headache that follows such indulgences.
keysToEntries.RoastDinner:
Imagine it. You gather around the table, the warm smell of a freshly cooked roast wafts across the room. Your mother places a bowl of roast potatoes, so divinely crisp that you wonder if they’re even real. Your sibling places a tray of freshly cooked vegetables, a kaleidoscope of colourful foods waiting to be consumed. Your father, doggedly sticking to traditional gender roles as if the past is an anchor weighing him down, slices into the roast serving each of you juicy cuts of meat that make your mouth water.

And what are you doing in all of this? Hrmm? Nothing, that’s what! Pull your life together Stanley!

If your name is not Stanley, you can ignore that above message.

Here on Ashenfall you do not have those smiling faces to reflect upon, only the memories elucidated by the roast meat before you. As you tuck in, you’ll recall those moments of happier times, before you got stranded here with nothing.
keysToEntries.SqueakingSausage:
Sausages are proof that ignorance is bliss. When you bite into the juicy banger, you are best served not to think about its contents. Nor about the origins of the skin that contains the meat so well.

Although simple, and lacking in much needed herbs and spices, these sausages manage to curb your hunger well enough to help you avoid thinking too much about what they’re made from.

Ignore the squeak… and the whiskers.
keysToEntries.PungentOmelette:
Get a nose peg for this one. The aroma that wafts from this culinary creation is almost unholy in its sensation. It reaches down from your nostrils into your throat, then it winds its way into your mouth and twists your tastebuds in ways that must surely be illegal.

Still, if you can fight through the stench, then this eggy abomination is surprisingly tasty and helps fortify your body against toxic elements.

The “if” is doing a lot of work in that statement though.
keysToEntries.ArcaneInfusion:
Wizards always push themselves too far. They compete academically with anything that moves, insisting on demonstrating their intellectual superior over everyone around them. Their intelligence is a glittering brooch that must be seen by everyone.

Naturally, this sort of mentality causes many a wizard to burn out. Academia creates a sort of stress that could be bottled and weaponised, only to be banned by conventions around the world for its cruelty. Yet, a wizard does it to themselves, pushing and pushing beyond the limits of the human condition.

Inevitably they turn to external solutions, and this particular mixture has pushed many a mage past academic burnout just long enough to get by. Somehow it makes magic flow easier, spells cast stronger than before, and the wizard is capable of feats they might never have achieved before.

They also tell themselves that it lets them get by on only two hours of sleep a night. It does not and it all catches up with them in the end. Wizard burnout can be disturbingly literal.
keysToEntries.RelentlessInfusion:
Popular among Wild Knight scouts, this mixture focussed the senses and improved reaction times. It was taken by hunters as they explored the dangerous forests of Ashenfall, allowing them respond to threats faster than before. Those extra moments saved countless lives.

In the village of Bramblemead there was a contingent of foolish youngsters who began drinking this mixture in large quantities, due to the energetic thrill it created. It would allow them to engage in strenuous activities for far longer without getting tired.

Its popularly began to grow among older members of the population as well, though no one would be willing to explain why.
keysToEntries.Egg:
If you prescribe to the theory of evolution, then this object wins the debate. After all, what came before the egg wasn’t technically a chicken, but something similar yet different. So, the egg would have come first, to then hatch the first chicken.

Of course, the real question here is whether chickens evolved, or whether they were created by the gods? No god claims to have created the first chicken, nor, indeed, the first egg, but they never deny it when asked.

So, which came first? The god or the egg?
keysToEntries.FriedEgg:
Slimy, greasy and everything you need. There are few things as surprisingly satisfying as a fried egg sandwich. If only you had some bread!

Contrary to popular humour the aviansie have no issue with people eating eggs around them. Indeed many of them have raptor like tastes and will happily consume the eggs of livestock birds. Boiled almhas (a domesticated bat-like creature) eggs were popular snacks on Abbinah, yet those eggs could not be fried as they would combust dramatically when exposed to a naked flame.

Fortunately chicken eggs are far less flammable.
keysToEntries.SteelHelm:
It was late in the development of Fellhollow that the people needed armour. They made do with old hand me down leather and bronze gear, brought with them from Bramblemead, for the most part, because what need was there for armour when you were protected by dragons.

Kuldra would put a stop to that mindset in the end, but it was the Children of Shadows who forged the first steel gear in Fellhollow. One member, in particular, Sadia Vulketra, began to feel that the order lacked the strength it may one day need to defend against the Saradominist forces.

There was no war. No conflict like that in Emberwood, indeed the four major faiths got along rather well with one another. Yet, the history books told Sadia exactly what the priests of Saradomin would do to the worshippers of the god of chaos.

She forged a helm first, with the assistance of one of her many lovers, a blacksmith Amalgamated called Vesk. The first helmet was styled with great curling horns, stained black by the forge’s flames.

Naturally, this style didn’t prove popular with the rest of the village, as they began crafting steel helms of their own, favouring a more simplistic approach, and a feathered plume, to look fancy.
keysToEntries.SteelBody:
It was late in the development of Fellhollow that the people needed armour. They made do with old hand me down leather and bronze gear, brought with them from Bramblemead, for the most part, because what need was there for armour when you were protected by dragons.

Not all members of the Children of Shadows were as paranoid as Sadia, many enjoyed a blending of faiths and would regularly attend the inter-faith meetings held by the elder priests of the different religions. Still, many felt a growing unease building, even in their idyllic village, and so they began to push for the construction of steel armour.

They took the recipes discovered by Vesk and Sadia, and they found cheaper and more efficient blacksmiths to work with. The elaborate designs favoured by Sadia and Vesk, were soon forgotten in favour of much simpler and efficient designs that could be worn by anyone, regardless of faith.

With the abundance of resources in Fellhollow, soon everyone seemed to have a set of steel armour and for many it became an excuse to forge family crests and trim with filigree that set them apart from one another.

They had no idea how necessary these suits of family armour would prove to be.
keysToEntries.SteelLegs:
It was late in the development of Fellhollow that the people needed armour. They made do with old hand me down leather and bronze gear, brought with them from Bramblemead, for the most part, because what need was there for armour when you were protected by dragons.

When Kuldra arrived it was as if the air had grown heavy, pressing down on everyone. A whole village fell silent as the Godeater turned her gaze upon them. As Imaru and Xikotal rose to face her, villagers instinctively ran to don their heirloom steel armour, hoping that they wouldn‘t have to use it, but knowing in their hearts that they would.

Yet, it wasn’t Kuldra that found a use for the armour. After Xikotal’s death, Kuldra left the region, leaving Imaru to her grief. And it would be that grief and the rage that came with it that would ultimately lead to the rising dead and the sad and sorry need to fight against them.
keysToEntries.GreenDragonhideCoif:
Wear the skin of your enemies, so that they know to fear you! This is the ranger way, to dress themselves in the outer layers of weaker dragons so that they larger dragons know not to mess with them.

Such was the logic of Bilar Amaris, a hunter that dwelled withing Emberwood. He was renowned among the locals, skilled with the bow and capable of taking down almost any prey. When the green dragons descended on the region, he decided that he would be the apex predator, hunting them down.

This was partially done in a misguided attempt to win the affections of the “black-haired beauty” that had set up a little potion shop in the village. Like many idiots, he had convinced himself that all a woman could ever want was a strong man that was capable of killing something bigger than he was.

He was, unsurprisingly, deeply mistaken.
keysToEntries.GreenDragonhideBody:
Wear the skin of your enemies, so that they know to fear you! This is the ranger way, to dress themselves in the outer layers of weaker dragons so that they larger dragons know not to mess with them.

Love makes fools of all of us. Infatuation more so, especially one-sided. Bilar was in love, so he claimed. He spoke of her beauty. He spoke of her acumen. He spoke of… well, he spoke a lot about things that you do not repeat in polite society. He wanted her and he wanted the ultimate gift to win her affection: a dragon’s heart.

Yet, dragons are unwilling to part with such a vital organ, and Bilar had to get creative. He found a dead dragon, one that had been slain in battle with a rival, and he decided to skin it. With it’s scaley hide in his possession he spent weeks fashioning powerful armour from the remains, until he had an outfit that resembled the creature he hunted.

So dressed, he set out towards the great stone circle near Emberwood, knowing that a dragon had recently made it its home. He would slay it, claim its heart and present it to the woman he loved and she would surely agree to be his forever.
keysToEntries.GreenDragonhideLegs:
Wear the skin of your enemies, so that they know to fear you! This is the ranger way, to dress themselves in the outer layers of weaker dragons so that they larger dragons know not to mess with them.

The funeral of Bilar Amaris was not a particularly popular one. A few fellow hunters stopped by to pay the briefest of respects, and a drunk great uncle turned up and got the name wrong. His death would have passed into complete obscurity, except for the clothes he was wearing when he died.

Despite the mauled condition of his corpse, the villagers that found him noticed the surprisingly supple and yet sturdy armour. Barely a scratch covered the dragonhide, and they remarked that if only he’d covered the neck a bit better, then he might not have ended up in the state he did.

The villagers brought the armour back and leatherworkers set to work on repeating it and improving it. Soon the best hunters in Emberwood would be wearing green dragonhide armour, and matched with better common sense, it kept them alive much longer.
keysToEntries.SplitbarkHelm:
Not all druids follow the notion that one must live a life of peaceful coexistence. After all, the animal kingdom is full of conflict and conquest, predator and prey, as well as battles for dominance. Some druids feel, therefore, that to truly emulate nature one must do so fully, including tooth and claw. The problem there is that druids are, by nature, generally on the squishier side and so they needed armour that would protect their vital areas better than their normal robes.

On Ashenfall, it was the druid Pikenmix who first tried to break out of the peace-loving mould. They learned magic that would allow them to hurl flame from their fingertips hoping it might be enough. Yet, as the enemies got too close, she soon learned that she needed stronger defence than her meagre hood would allow.

It was when she was lost in thought, that she found the solution – by walking straight into a tree. Her nose broke in several places and her entire body screamed in pain, yet the tree remained unaffected by her violent interaction.

So it was that she crafted herself a helm made from the very same tree. Partially in revenge, but mostly because she knew how strong the bark could be.
keysToEntries.SplitbarkBody:
Not all druids follow the notion that one must live a life of peaceful coexistence. After all, the animal kingdom is full of conflict and conquest, predator and prey, as well as battles for dominance. Some druids feel, therefore, that to truly emulate nature one must do so fully, including tooth and claw. The problem there is that druids are, by nature, generally on the squishier side and so they needed armour that would protect their vital areas better than their normal robes.

Soon, many druids began wearing these robes made from the bark of Ashenfall trees. The first incarnations were deeply unwieldy, taking on an ungainly and unfashionable cylindrical shape – little more than tree trunks with holes for the arms and legs. They were effective insomuch that attacks rarely got through, but they made it somewhat difficult to retaliate.

Later the wizard Lavernius realises that with a few tweaks, the addition of some elegant fine cloth, one could fashion the splitbark armour into a far more glamorous form. She took to decorating her robes with glittering gemstones and silver filigree, making her stand out from the crowd. An excellent distraction in magical duels, but a terrible spotlight for local thieves.
keysToEntries.SplitbarkLegs:
Not all druids follow the notion that one must live a life of peaceful coexistence. After all, the animal kingdom is full of conflict and conquest, predator and prey, as well as battles for dominance. Some druids feel, therefore, that to truly emulate nature one must do so fully, including tooth and claw. The problem there is that druids are, by nature, generally on the squishier side and so they needed armour that would protect their vital areas better than their normal robes.

After having her glittering armour stolen from her, Lavernius decided that she was tired of being “Miss Nice Wizard” and crafted a version of the armour that would blend worryingly well into the local scenery. She crept up upon her robbers as they spent an evening trying to prize gemstones from what remained of her helm, and she unleashed her terrible revenge.

Thousands of chinchompas, each of them suffering from a powerful confusion, were turned upon the miscreants. The resulting explosion was so bright that it turned night into day for a moment, and the flames spreads with worrying rapacity.

Later bandits soon agreed that Lavernius was to be given a very wide berth.
keysToEntries.CompostBucket:
The first bucket on Gielinor was created by Mrs Hyacinth, to help her around the farm. They had buckets back on the human homeworld of Teragard, of course, but she hadn’t brought anything with her from the village Guthix had recruited her from.

The problem was that Mrs Hyacinth had a cow, named the traditional Bessie, that produced an unreasonable amount of manure, and Mrs Hyacinth needed something to help with the clear up.

She used local twigs, at first, weaving together more of a basket than a bucket, and the results were not as watertight as she had hoped. In the end, she meticulously combined strips of wood, tied together with cord, and they were able to withstand even the worst of Bessie’s endeavours.

Now, you too can fill one of these marvelous contraptions with horrifically pungent compost. Spread the smelly stuff across your farming plots and marvel at the health and happiness of your leafy little friends.
keysToEntries.PlantCure:
Fashioned from clean water, weeds and animal bones, this mixture leeches out the vitality from those ingredients and transfers it to the sick and dying plants in your care. Cooked over a range, this concoction was first thought to be the domain of druids and herblore masters, but in truth it was a cook that put the pieces together. 

Old mother Potterington was much beloved by the people of Bramblemead. On weekends, when people allowed themselves brief breaks form their toils, she would whip up a great batch of something delicious and simmering, and neighbouring farmers would drop by for a meal. 

This kindness extended not only to people, but to her garden and farming patches too. She whipped up a broth so infused with nutrients, that Brassica Prime himself is said to have blessed it. When fed to plants, their withering and wilting leaves would stiffen and return to vibrancy, life and vitality theirs once again.

Now you can brew such a concoction yourself, so that your plants might always live their healthiest days.
keysToEntries.WateringCan.Wooden:
Rain is unreliable. It is far too whimsical in when and where it falls. Relying on its good nature is a sure-fire way to court drought, famine and ruin. Because of this, the druids created rainfall you could hold, handheld containers of water that could be summoned when and where it was needed.

Fill one of these up from any water source and you can use it to keep your farming plots nice and moist.
keysToEntries.WateringCan.Bronze: While the druids relied on wooden watering cans, the more pragmatic farmers turned to metal. Bronze was sturdier, lasted longer and proved to ultimately be the better tool. Crops across Ashenfall found themselves constantly existing a state of blissful hydration, never knowing the agony of thirst.
keysToEntries.WateringCan.Steel:
With the discovery of steel, most people found new and wonderful ways to bring death. They crafted sharper swords, stronger shields, axes that could slice through a foe like a hot knife through butter. Yet, some people saw this new material, as a chance to bring life.

Steel watering cans were sturdier and more durable. Able to bring much needed moisture to dehydrated gardens than before.
keysToEntries.WoodenSpade: Sometimes the very earth itself must be punished for its cruelty! These wooden tools, simple and elegant, can break through the planet’s crust, letting you show Gielinor exactly who’s boss.
keysToEntries.BronzeSpade: Wood breaks, shatters and splinters, which can be very frustrating when out in the garden. Metal is more durable and longer lasting, and bronze is a very malleable metal. With one of these spades you can assault the skin of the planet, reshaping Gielinor to suit your own grand designs.
keysToEntries.SteelSpade:
Fallen from too many tall buildings, only to be punished for your misstep by the cruel and malicious ground? Well, now is your chance to get your revenge. The smug soil doesn’t think you can touch it, but you can prove it wrong by stabbing it through it’s smug topsoil smirk with a nice steel spade.

Oh, sure, it’s great for gardening, but really this is a tool for sweet, sweet revenge.
keysToEntries.Secateurs:
The best way to get leaves to stay healthy, is to instil within them a healthy sense of fear. By pruning away the leaves that have allowed themselves to fall to weakness, frailty and disease, you show the other leaves what happens if they don’t behave.

I find that whispering their failures to the plant, as I slice away parts of their extremities, emboldens the plant with a desire to thrive.
keysToEntries.MagicSecateurs:
Ordinary secateurs are good for the visibly problematic parts of the plant, but sometimes you need a little extra touch to help your flowers thrive. First created by the druids, thousands of years ago, these magical devices are imbued with a desire to heal. When you cut away the diseased leaves, the enchantment within seeks out hidden parts of the plant that are beginning to succumb to infection, and eradicate the disease before it begins.

As a result, your plants end up healthier and happier, often leading to better harvests. No wonder the druids of Ashenfall had the most extravagant gardens.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Cabbage:
Hail Brassica Prime, lord of deliciousness and nutrition! Within these tiny seeds rest all the power of creation, ready to burst forth into spherical orbs of crisp deliciousness.

Guthix once encountered Brassica Prime when he wandered the moors of Ashenfall. On a strange outcrop, beneath a waterfall that reflected the silver moonlight, he spied a cabbage so large and so full of nutrition that he was briefly stunned at its magnificence.

When the cabbage spoke, the god of balance found himself quite at a loss. According to the cabbage, he was the lord of all vegetables, and keeper of the ancient secrets of a balanced diet, seeing some small kinship in Guthix’s search for harmony. They spoke long and strange conversations, deep into the night, about the nature of magic, divinity and, of course, food.

Brassica’s stories changed every night. Each moonrise offered a new excuse for his divinity, and new lessons on how he had supposedly shaped creation itself, by mixing together the base vitamins needed for the universe to thrive.

Mad, perhaps, or just composed of an alien way of thinking, Guthix took some small comfort in his strange new friend. As such, he made a point of having his druids sow cabbage seeds in every field, and around all his settlements, so that Brassica might be honoured in some small way.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Potato:
Know this secret, child, the potato is a source to foods both delicious and dangerous. They can be baked, roasted over the fire to create a delicious fluffy centre encased within a crisp and tasty core. Or they can be combined with butter and milk to craft a small mountain of creamy extravagance that can serve both as meal and sculpture tool.

These seeds, small and unremarkable, can be transformed into meals of such exquisite flavour, that they can end wars. Or, as was the case of the feud between the Sherrydoe and Nab families, start one -- that ended up in a house burning to the ground, three failed marriages and a cow being briefly elected mayor. It’s best not to ask.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Flax: Now you can plant these pretty blue flowers wherever you like. Bring much needed vibrancy to your drab and dreary little wooden hovel. Or, of course, you could grow them to harvest them and turn them into thread, but isn’t that a waste when they’re so very pretty?
keysToEntries.Seeds.Marrentil:
No longer do you need to traipse through the swamps in search of these precious little herbs, now you can grow them in your own back garden.

When not drowned out by the foul stench of the swamps, these herbs have a pleasant odour to them that can attract invisible squirrels to your garden. Keep your eyes open for them!
keysToEntries.Seeds.Harralander:
Tired of sinking into swamp-mud in search of these fragrant little herbs? Well now you can grow them yourself.

The fragrance of these herbs is even better without the swamp to dull it, and can be a very pleasant pick-me-up first thing in the day.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Redberry:
Grow your own tasty berries with these lovely little seeds. In ancient Ashenfall, young villagers would gift a potential romantic partner with a handful of berries, and if the plant flourished it was seen as proof that they were meant to be together.

Most considered it a rather cheap and rubbish gift, and so contrary to the folklore, these rarely resulted in meaningful relationships.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Onion:
Sometimes you just want a way to make everyone around you cry. Onions have always served this purpose, and I often bring one with me to slice into at social gatherings when I feel everyone is being just a little too happy.
