With these seeds, you can avoid scavenging for onions across the dangerous plains and instead produce a steady supply with them to torment your friends and family. Or you could eat them, I guess, if you’re weird.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Dwellberry:
Garou consider dwellberries to have spiritual significance. Their priests believe that if they are crushed into paste and used as war-paint, they can ward off evil spirits, misfortune and vermin.

The seeds are often used in this process, creating a powdery texture that can make it easier to spread the paint across the fur.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Toadflax: Although they prefer the toxic atmosphere of the Bloodblight swamps, these seeds can grow just about anywhere with the right care paid to them.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Kwuarm: These plants are greedily hoarded by goblins and garou, their poisonous capabilities highly desired by their scouts. With these seeds, you no longer need to slaughter goblins in droves to acquire these herbs, instead you are now able to grow them yourself in your own back yard.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Snapdragon:
Unfortunately, these seeds do not grow adorable little scaly nightmares to terrorise your enemies.

Fortunately, they do grow into useful little medicinal herbs with various uses in herblore. So, an alright trade off, even if the tiny dragons would be better.
keysToEntries.Seeds.CorseCotton: Popular with necromantic horticulturalists, the corpse cotton plant is as useful as it is mildly disturbing. These seeds will allow you to avoid getting yourself blown up in random locations, instead allowing to access this awful plant in the comfort of your own garden.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Cadavarberries:
Sometimes you just want to fill your garden with deadly berries. This was certainly true of Agnes DeWithlington-Smithe, a self-proclaimed “witch” with zero magical talent. She delighted in filling her garden with strange and dangerous herbs and flowers. The more poisonous, the better.

Locals would claim that she was a retired assassin, trying to hone her craft. In truth she just found something about the plants that she admired, and, ultimately, she thought they were pretty.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Irit: Thorny irit is a pain to harvest. You must seek out the spiky roots, and the battle with them before you can claim the herbs for yourself. With these seeds you can skip some of that faff and instead grow them wherever you like, the very pinnacle of convenience.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Pumpkin:
These gourds are most popular around the autumn, where they are hollowed out by local children and used to ward away spirits – poorly, as it seems they attract more than they repel.

Indeed one strange fellow found himself so delighted by the strange gourds that he took an aspect of them into himself, feeling that they best embodied his favourite time of the year. So the strange individual known as “Pumpkin Pete”, part of the Pete family, came to be.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Tomato: Unlock the plant that causes the most arguments amongst the truly bored, the humble tomato. Is it a fruit? A vegetable? A plague on sandwiches everywhere? Well, now you can start these arguments wherever you like, by planting one of these beauties in a fresh farming patch.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Wheat: If you’re trying to reduce your carbohydrate intake, then you probably want to toss these seeds into the fire. If, however, you’re a fan of bread in all its many and sundry forms, then you can start your life as a wheat farmer with a handful of these seeds. Just plant them in a farming patch and start planning the name of your future bakery.
keysToEntries.Seeds.Watermelon: These tiny seeds can grow into a delightful patch of scrumptious watermelons. Use them to grow plants that can create delicious fruit salads, add garnish to garish cocktails, or you could paint faces on them if you’re very lonely.
keysToEntries.Tomato: Squishy red little things, these fruits are a staple in sauces, salads or hurled at people in the stocks when you want to laugh at their misfortune.
keysToEntries.Wheat: These humble grains are the staple of many a burgeoning society. Breads, cakes, pastries and all manner of delicious foodstuffs can be forged from these marvellous stalks.
keysToEntries.Watermelon:
Sometimes it’s not enough to drink water, you desire the urge to chomp on it instead. These melons are delicious and refreshing in ways that few other fruits seem able to match.

Smashing one with a hammer is not recommended, but still manages to be weirdly satisfying.
keysToEntries.GreenDragonhideLeather:
Is it ingenuity of hubris to refine the mighty scales of a dragon down into this thick and sturdy leather?

To the Wild Knights it was the natural order. Hides became leather, which became a resilient second skin they would wear whilst riding into battle. Favoured by archers and scouts due to the nimble flexibility of the scales, whilst still acting as a resilient bulwark against sword, arrow and claw alike.

To the people of Emberwood village, however, such sights were akin to blasphemy. Whilst the green dragons showed little mercy, the villagers loved and adored their majestic draconic protectors, Imaru and Xikotal, and they thought that wearing the skin of their kind might potentially cause offence.
keysToEntries.GreenDragonHide:
Dragons are never fond of surrendering their natural armour, a natural feature they’ve used to great advantage.

In the earliest days of the Dragonkin experiments, the scales of a dragon were something to be proud of. They would turn away blade and arrow with equal skill, and even magic struggled to pierce their natural defences.

Still, this wasn’t good enough for their makers. Lacrussa, herself, saw the hide of dragons as a promising start, but little more, and she would strive to forge newer and more resilient scales on her creations. The green dragons were, in her opinion, some of the weakest of the dragons, and she only kept them around because her sibling enjoyed the glittering green reflections of the sunlight on their scales.
keysToEntries.HardLeatherScraps:
Fragments of thick and sturdy hides, these scraps are of little use on their own, but combined together they can become a resilient leather with a wide range of uses.

The Children of Shadows would scavenge these scraps across Fellhollow, using the pieces to craft profane totems to their sinister god. Unaware that he would be offended at such a low-effort offering.
keysToEntries.DragonBlood: The blood of a dragon is a strange thing. It is infused with magic in a way that most other creatures can’t match, a side effect of the alchemical processes that created their kind. Dragonkin scientists needed blood that could adapt to a wide range of chimeric additions to the body. As such dragon blood, unlike human blood, doesn’t possess any particular type and each could act as donor to the other.
keysToEntries.DraconicLeather:
Tanned hide infused with a dragon’s vigor, draconic leather was a gamechanging discovery to the people of Fellhollow. It was as flexible as normal leather, but more resilient than even hardened leather, and it could be fashioned into a variety of shapes useful in a variety of armoured styles.

For a short while, the fashionistas of Emberwood village would use this leather to create garments that evoked the majesty of their draconic protectors, without bearing the scales that might cause undue offence.
keysToEntries.DraconicHide:
Do not pity the dragonwolf. Oh, you don’t? That’s fine then. Yes, horrible little blighters aren’t they? Their teeth are too jagged and they’re far too aggressive for my tastes. Still, apparently deadly critters don’t just want to roll over and die so you can harvest their skin, who’d have thought?

The dragonwolf is an experiment that didn’t quite go right. It wasn’t wrong, exactly, it was certainly a step in the direction their creator wanted, but like most Dragonkin experiments, they was never “good enough”. Too warm-blooded. Too fluffy. Too weak.

Fortunately, the crafters in Emberwood were not quite so picky. They found that hide to be incredibly durable and hardy, whilst remaining surprisingly flexible. They would craft wonderous outfits from the tanned leather, trying to emulate their winged overseers.
keysToEntries.CorpseCotton:
The corpse flower is a strange creature. Strange, almost flesh-like sacks, filled with a noxious gas that explodes upon contact with the air. The resulting mass of fibres looks disturbingly like thin strips of a cadaver’s skin, earning it the name “corpse cotton” by the more macabre Emberwood villagers.

In truth it’s little more than thick plant fibres that can be woven together into a smooth, durable and, frankly, fashionable fabric.
keysToEntries.FineThread: Because “corpse thread” is far too sinister a title, this thread is referred to as fine thread. It’s stronger, thinner and more malleable than cotton threads. The villagers of Emberwood enjoyed this thread in their clothing, creating garments that would suffer greater strain than their previous gear. Farmers were particularly fond of it.
keysToEntries.FineCloth: More elegant and refined than cotton cloth, this fabric was very popular mong Emberwood citizenry. They would craft beautiful dresses, fine shirts and elegant robes, many of which included patterns of undulating blue and black in honour of the serpentine dances they would watch in the sky, as Imaru and Xikotal swam the air currents together.
keysToEntries.Ectoplasm:
There are many theories on what ectoplasm is, each as disturbing as the last. Some say that it’s emotion, condensed over years of existence. Others think that it’s some sort of infection of the soul, as only the undead seem to produce it.

In truth, ectoplasm is none of these. It’s the outer shell of a soul, the skin more or less, and acts as a sort of lubrication between the flesh and the spirit. Tiny bits of reality combine with the immaterial otherness of the underworld and congeal into a sort of spiritual mixture that ghosts and other undead find incredible useful.

Necromancers prize the stuff, finding a myriad of uses for it. Most people find it weird and decidedly “icky”.
keysToEntries.NecroticBonemeal:
The walking dead are strange things, animated by hunger, need, rage and a touch of magic. In Ashenfall it is the hunger of Imaru that drives them, as her great maw draws in the souls of both the living and the dead, the afflicted try to cling to life with everything that they have.

They fail, but the drive remains. Fear, desperation, and simple need keeps the body moving, if not alive. This changes the body, that hunger and rot seeping into the very bones and changing them to be something other than they were.

When ground down, these bones become a powder containing hints of that animating magic. A necromantic resonance with a wide range of uses.
keysToEntries.SacredOil:
Although the recipe is significantly different to the oil created in Morytania, this oil serves a similar purpose. The priests of Emberwood created this oil to assist in funerary rites, the strange scent of the oil would help the dead pass on when cremated and, crucially, in the time of the Withering, the bodies would be reduced to ash, unable to get up and cause further trouble.

Originally this oil used pressed olives, and the influence of the gods to create an oil that would burn a body sufficiently to avoid the dead rising. As the Withering increased and civilisation began to fall into ruin, the priests found alternative methods to create a sufficiently strong flame.
keysToEntries.Coal:
Coal is the petrified memory of a long dead ecosystem. Plants and animals, who lived countless aeons ago, crushed down into rocks of rigid carbon and becoming a powerful fuel that changes civilisations.

When you burn the coal, imagine the worlds that came before and the lives forgotten. Let each piece become a funeral pyre for a long-lost age.

Or enjoy shiny new steel armour, your call!
keysToEntries.SteelBar:
If iron had been a marvel, steel was a revelation. Sturdier structures. More durable armour. Weapons sharper and stronger than ever seen before. If the people of Brynmoor had known about steel, then perhaps the garou threat would have been over almost as soon as it began.

For the people of Emberwood, it led to superior farming tools, stronger buildings and better hunting equipment. After all, what need did people have for weapons and armour with a dragon protecting them?
keysToEntries.WitheredHeart:
The heart is a pump, nothing more, designed to push precious blood around the circulatory system, so that our weak and feeble frames can function. Yet, metaphysically, it is the most important organ, for it houses our emotional centre, the very essence of what makes us human.

A withered heart isn’t just a heart that has languished. Nor is it just a heart that is drained of blood. No, a withered heart has had all the humanity leeched from it, emptied of emotion, or connection, of self.

This process is imperfect, of course, and tiny fragments of soul-matter remains, that can be refined and extracted from the heart. Withdrawing the last remnants of what made the owner “them”.
keysToEntries.UndeadBone:
You may think an undead bone is no different from a normal bone, and in most cases that’s true. The animating forces of necromancy normally just puppet the bones, allowing them to be possessed by a spirit or controlled by some external force.

The undead of Fellhollow are different. They animated by the last remnants of their humanity, tiny bits of soul clinging on in tattered forms. They move because they need to and they shudder because they fear what happens when they stop.

As such, the bones of the Fellhollow dead have strange properties that a skilled craftsman can refine and find uses for.

Morbid, yes, but inventive.
keysToEntries.HollowBark:
It is perhaps strange that bark could be referred to as hollow. It’s generally extracted in strips, or patches, but nothing that could act as a container. Indeed there would need to be an entire tree without a centre to make it hollow, and that’s exactly the case here.

When the withering began it was souls that suffered first, torn away and dragged into the underworld to be consumed by the cursed dragon. Yet, as time drew on, Imaru’s hunger did not diminish, only grow, and so the world itself began to suffer.

Things taste worse in Fellhollow. Dreams are vapid and empty, if you have them at all. A campire feels colder, even if it warms you just as well as before. Because the substance, the soul of that thing if you will, is being consumed.

Hollow bark comes from a tree that has been hollowed out from within. Its insides less than they should be, weaker and wrong. As awful as this is, it makes the bark develop in strange ways desirable by skilled mages with a poor sense of fashion.
keysToEntries.WillowLogs: Willow wood is a wonderful material. Flexible and yet strong and durable, it makes for an excellent resource in a variety of projects. Rare to find, it exists predominantly in small groves connected to a pool of water. Legend has it that the pools of water are formed from the tears of weeping willows, whose spirits lament the loss of a long-lost god.
keysToEntries.PurifyingWater: This water is drawn from a rejuvenation pool and contains within it the spark to fight off the Wither-rot. Although Death keeps these pools active in modern times, a long time ago these pools would have been blessed by Imaru and her magic. She created the water as a way to stave off illness and corruption, in the population she protected.
keysToEntries.WitheringWater:
Drawn from pools of wither-rot water, lakes and streams poisoned by the waters of the river Noumenon – the river of the dead.

These waters have become corrupted by Imaru’s hunger and each drop longs to devour one’s soul.
keysToEntries.Irit:
It is unsurprising that irit grows in Fellhollow, considering it has such a close association with the dead. Whereas normal irit grows around graveyards and near the temples of death deities, the irit in Fellhollow grows everywhere.

Yet, unlike its leafier version, the irit here is twisted and sharp. It is formed of thorny roots that writhe out of the ground and must be cut down to access the herb beneath. After all, the dead in Fellhollow refuse to stay buried without a fight, why wouldn’t the irit react the same?
keysToEntries.Cadavaberries:
Don’t eat them. No, seriously, don’t. At least not raw. Cadavaberries are poisonous and deadly, often used in sinister ceremonies of ritual sacrifice. The cadavaberries on Ashenfall are equally toxic, but can be coaxed into good behaviour with the judicious addition of fire.

The toxins can be burned away, providing a surprisingly pleasant tasting berry beneath. But, one must be careful, for preparing them wrong is the last mistake someone is likely to make.
keysToEntries.Pumpkin:
They seem strange without faces, don’t they?

Heralds of the spookiest of seasons, the humble pumpkin exists in two worlds, the world of the living and the world of the dead. When carved it acts as a ward against dangerous spirits and when mixed with spices and added to warm drinks, it acts as a lure for ex-girlfriends, distracting them as it gives you time to escape.
keysToEntries.UndeadMeat:
Despite the appearance of their former owners, and the deeply unpleasant smell emanating from it, undead meat is not rotten. It, like the nature of its former owners, is preserved in a state between life and death, as if it’s on the cusp of souring, but never quite getting there.

This unique nature gives it some very interesting properties, but sadly taste isn’t one of them.
keysToEntries.Bread:
Simple wheat, water, yeast and salt, combined together and incinerated within an oven. A staple across many cultures and species across Gielinor and beyond. The Icyene of Hallow enjoy a flatbread that they can wrap food in, for a meal on the wind. The aviansie of Abbinah, when the limited wheat is available, prefer a thick and sturdy bread, so that each bite lasts longer. Elves enjoy a sourdough, priding themselves on how long they can keep the starter alive.

For the people of Ashenfall, much like their cousins in the mainland, a simple loaf is more than enough.
keysToEntries.RoastPumpkin:
Whilst some prefer to hollow out a pumpkin, carving features into it that should scare away the creatures of the night, more practical people eat them.

A roasted pumpkin is a hearty affair, full of texture and a mellow taste that goes especially well in desserts.
keysToEntries.GrilledTomatoes: Somehow still juicy, even after a judicious application of fire.
keysToEntries.BagOfFlour: Fun fact, these tiny granules of wheat are surprisingly combustible. So be careful with fire magic around them.
keysToEntries.MincedUndeadMeat:
Don’t think about it. Just burn it until it’s unrecognisable and try not to think about the implications of consuming the flesh of rotting zombie animals.

I’m sure it’s fine.

Do I want a bite? No, no, I’m good… err… trying to watch my figure. You go right ahead.
keysToEntries.PumpkinSpiceInfusion:
Across dimensional rifts this drink calls out to a part of us we try and hide. That piece of us that wants a touch of whimsy, when the world turns darker than we feel comfortable with. As the approaching shadow of winter looms, its cold chill reaching into our souls, drinks like these offer a little warmth against the oncoming frost.

Still, its allure can be overwhelming. There are some who succumb too heavily to its charms, becoming thralls to the flavour. Their entire personality clasps onto this mixture with frightening zeal.

It is tasty… but at what price?
keysToEntries.CadavaberryInfusion:
At first glance you might just assume this is poison, for the cadavaberry is a dangerous fruit. Yet, with careful preparation, and an appropriate amount of boiling, the berry can become a sharp, yet not fatal addition to any dessert.

Mixed with water, it becomes an empowering infusion, that puts a sprint in your step. Partially because your body is reminded at just how close it came to death in drinking it, so makes the most of the life it has left.
keysToEntries.FruitPie:
Grannies everywhere are masters of this dish. Delicious fruits, a touch of sugar, some delightful pastry and that final ingredient – a dark blessing to ancient gods.

Or love, that works too.

With the pact complete, these pies allow you those fond memories, of comfort, hope and family. Something much needed out in the wilds of Ashenfall.
keysToEntries.DubiousMeatPie:
The smell from this pie is strange. Undertones of gamey meats, the tantalising touch of subtle herbs and spices. The pastry is crip, inviting…and yet, beneath the crust, hidden behind the pleasantries, is the scent of something otherworldly.

Death has touched this pie.
keysToEntries.PumpkinSoup: A taste of autumn reduced to slurry and warmed over the fire. In Fellhollow, pumpkin soup was a popular dish when the temperature started to drop. Locals would gather together and share enormous cauldrons full of soup, as a way to build bonds with their community. It is said that once the villagers created an enormous cauldron, so that Imaru and Xikotal could join in the tradition, though for the great dragons it was barely an appetiser.
keysToEntries.BeefAndTomatoStew: Tomatoes go well with just about everything. They enhance the flavours of anything they’re paired with and a nice beef stew is no exception. The combined flavours are comforting as well as filling, and almost make you forget that you’re a long way from home, on a continent filled with murderous dragons.
keysToEntries.VeganFryUp: Crisp and delicious vegetables, neatly fried mushrooms, there are few breakfasts quite as delicious as a fry up, even for those who abjure bacon.
keysToEntries.SteelDagger:
The children of darkness were very fond of steel daggers. They were powerful, dangerous and very expensive at the time, making them the perfect ritual tool to demonstrate their adoration of the god of chaos.

Mostly the offspring of wealthy families, the rituals of the Children were performed out of boredom more than religious fervour. They would gather at secret meetings and proudly display their elaborately carved daggers, trying to prove to each other who was the most devoted to the lord of darkness by way of expensive kitchenware.

If you asked any of them to use such a blade in battle, they would be more inclined to slit their own throats in fear, rather than mutilate their opponents.
keysToEntries.SteelScimitar:
It is said that the origins of the scimitar come from Al Kharid, where skilled swordsmen would wield them in a dazzling manner akin to a dance. Travelling dignitaries would be wowed by the spectacle, applauding great choreographed sword clashes that dramatized legendary battles of history’s past.

They were brought to Ashenfall by a noble family during the god wars, who had taken up the scimitar as their weapon of choice, in service, of course, of the mighty Saradomin. This family was proud and pious, which made them all the more concerned when their daughter was one of the first to show signs of Amalgamation.

Love, for once, triumphed over prejudice, and the family scorned their wealth and prestige in order to protect their child and they joined many others who fled the growing unrest in the village of Bramblemead and travelled north to Fellhollow. There, they taught the secrets of the scimitar to the local blacksmiths, and the weapon found itself a great deal of popularity in the times to come.
keysToEntries.SteelSword:
Atilius Rasmodel was a proud and powerful knight. Her skill with the sword was without compare and her bravery seemingly infinite against the dangers in Fellhollow. She was chosen by Imaru as one of her guards, not that a dragon needed such a thing, and she was made responsible for the defence of the dragons when they fell into the weeks long torpors they were known to suffer.

She called herself the Dragon Knight, even though such a title was unofficial and self-proclaimed, it soon stuck among the village people and they would bow and nod in appreciation of her skill as they passed her.

When Kuldra descended on the region, demanding humanity’s destruction, Atilius climbed on Xikotal’s back in hope to drive her steel blade through the Godeater’s heart. She died beside the dragon she had sworn to protect, free of all remorse, in the line of duty.
keysToEntries.SteelMace:
The priests of Emberwood village were not as militant as their fellows back in Brynmoor. They saw little need for weapons, when a dragon watched over their flock. Still, they carried steel maces by their side as a symbol of office, each one ornately engraved with the symbol of their chosen deity.

Although the Saradominist priests had first insisted that different faiths could only turn on one another, they found an odd sort of kinship in their fellows and would regularly enjoy debates of the merits and flaws of each other’s faiths.
keysToEntries.SteelGreatsword:
It was the followers of Armadyl who enjoyed the greatsword so much, in defiance of the bows that were more the more traditional choice. They said that it reminded them of the legend of the godsword, and would lament not knowing the secrets of forging one, as it would be quite useful against the godeater, Kuldra, and her call for humanity’s death.

Sadly for the villagers, the art of godsword creation was never discovered, so they turned to steel instead. Whilst significantly less flashy, and almost certainly not going to terrify any deities, a steel sword will do significant damage to anything not divinely attuned.
keysToEntries.SteelGreataxe:
Power corrupts, and what’s more powerful than an enormous axe comprised of a metal stronger than any seen before? Such was the logic of Olga Skulltaker (not her real name), the self-styled bandit queen of Fellhollow.

As a child, Olga had always enjoyed listening to her grandfather’s stories from the mainland. Tales of mighty warriors defeating terrifying foes. Purehearted heroes challenging evil knights to great battles across pointlessly high bridges and the like. Her parents always laughed and called her “their little hero” but the truth was she was far more interested in the villain.

When she was old enough to wield a weapon, she trained with the biggest axes she could carry, and her sparring partners feared facing off against her because she always took things too far. Once even costing another student their fingers.

As an adult, she found a place for herself among the Children of Shadows. She had hoped they would be dangerous, wicked people, like herself, but instead they spend their time reciting terrible poetry and talking about evil, without ever doing it.

So she took her axe, changed her name, and found the highest bridge she could, and attacked anyone that tried to cross it.
keysToEntries.SteelWarhammer:
Lionel wasn’t good at much in life, but he could swing a hammer and he could swing it hard. As one of the Amalgamated, born with hair of metal and skin of stone, Lionel worried that he would be as hated as the Amalgamated in Brynmoor had been, but Fellhollow proved a kinder place.

Instead of being feared and shunned, his unnatural strength became a source of much pride in his family and friends. Locals would challenge him to heft heavier and heavier things, cheering and lavishing him with praise as he did so.

Life had been good. He was happy. So, of course, it all had to end.

When the dead began to rise, arrows and blades seemed less effective than before, but crushing force still got the job done. His family and friends escaped to the Silverthorn Keep and Lionel waited at the guardhouse, standing as a wall against the tide of the undead. Countless zombies and skeletons fell to his hammer, reduced to little more than powder in a few careful swings. He could have stemmed the tide if that were all, but when the Zogre descended upon him, even Lionel’s Warhammer eventually broke against the strain.
keysToEntries.SteelShield:
There are few things as good at shielding you from harm, as a great slab of metal between you and your foe. The steel shield is more durable than its iron kin, capable of resisting greater and more deadly blows.

It was popular among Fellhollow locals, who would play a game that indicated an intelligence shortage among the population. The game consisted of one player being equipped with a crossbow and other players would hide around the area carrying steel shields. The crossbow wielder would then hunt the other players and try and fire a bolt into their shields, marking them as out of the game.

Unsurprisingly the injuries caused by this game were large and common place. Even the death of some of the players wouldn’t cause the activity to be banned. “It’s tradition”, “it’s just good fun”, “hardly anyone gets hurt”, these were the arguments made even as parents grieved the loss of their child from the game.
keysToEntries.SplitbarkStaff:
Magic always inspires people. Something about rewriting the underpinning rules of your reality, with little more than force of will and a handful of stones, makes people reach for lofty goals. In Fellhollow it was the wizard Falistrius that wanted to make a name for themselves.

They wanted to be a magical scholar. A researching who might uncover the secrets of the universe itself. They sought spells of greater and greater power, dabbling in mysteries both profane and forbidden, and came up empty handed.

After constantly searching for power, it was an unexpected duel that would grant her the fame she sought, at a price she would have preferred to avoid. A travelling elven sorcerer arrived in the village of Emberwood one night and the local drunk foolishly picked a fight with him. Unfortunately for Falistrius that drunk was her wife.

The elf challenged the drunk to a duel, for satisfaction over a drink spilled, and by local custom Falistrius was forced to answer. They met in the forests of Fellhollow, and unleashed their magic.

The elf’s spells were like nothing she had ever seen before. Cold and dark, sanguine and terrible, they tore into her magical defences like they were nothing and writhed inside her soul like a foul nightmare. Against the strain, her staff shattered, leaving her vulnerable and in desperation she grasped a fallen hollow tree branch and urged her magic through it.

It wasn’t enough to save her. But it was enough to earn the respect and satisfaction of the sorcerer, who left the region with his pale-skinned cohorts. Ever since then, young Fellhollow mages would craft staves from the hollow bark from the forest, in memory of Falistrius.
keysToEntries.WillowShortbow:
As the dead rose in greater and greater numbers, the villagers needed stronger weapons to defeat them. Their ash and oak longbows failed to unleash sufficient force needed to stem the relentless tide of the undead. They needed a stronger material, that was also flexible enough to deliver arrows of great enough force.

Willow was their solution. It is said that the first willow shortbow was gifted to a villager by a spirit that dwelled within the grove. Legends say that the spirit had an unnatural beauty, and would lure foolish villagers into the waters where they would be drowned. Yet, somehow, one villager earned not the spirits ire, but their admiration.

The spirit gave up their immortal life, to court the villager. They brought gifts of flowers, fish and finally they gifted a shortbow made from the willow trees in the spirits grove. The validity of this tale is hotly debated, with most assuming it is little more than myth, yet willow groves are still considered spiritual places, where one must be careful who they talk to.
keysToEntries.WillowLongbow:
Ulv had many skills, but hunting was his strongest. He was inherently quiet, still as the night, and he could hold his position, in the trees or bushes, for hours at a time. Ravanna was never good at this. When the two of them hunted together, Ravanna would sneeze, cough or scream at the sight of an unexpected insect, and the two of them would watch their quarry disappear off into the night.

Although this would occasionally test their friendship, Ulv never really held it against Ravanna. Instead he continued attempting to teach the necromancer the ways of the forest, and the techniques the Wild Knights would use to track down game. It never took. Try as she might, Ravanna was never going to be silent as a shadow.

In apology, she looked for a way to create a weapon that would reduce the impact of her noisy nature. Longbows fired further and faster, meaning that less stealth was needed. She made her way into Fellhollow in search of a material strong enough, and returned with willow branches that she crafted into a longbow as a gift for her friend.

Their hunts improved dramatically afterward. That was, until they became the hunted.
keysToEntries.SteelArrows:
Traditional wisdom says that to kill a zombie, you must destroy the brain. This is disappointingly untrue in Fellhollow, the animating force of Imaru’s hunger needs no thought to drive it forward.

No, a far better plan is to destroy the bones and muscles with sharp metal-tipped spikes launched at the creatures with as much force as you can manage. Steel arrows perform this task very nicely.
keysToEntries.SteelBolts:
Sharpened bolts of steel projected at great speed is a formula for your target to have a very bad day.

Although it was cheaper and quicker to forgo the coal and craft iron bolts, the relentless nature of the undead hordes required a more practical approach. Steel was stronger than iron, and was more likely to put the ravenous undead back in the dirt where they belonged, making it very popular in the last few years of Fellhollow’s resistance.
keysToEntries.PoisonedSteelArrows:
What’s better than sharpened pieces of wood with super strong metal made into even sharper points, firing at your enemies with unreasonable speed? Why, the very same thing, but coated with a deadly toxic poison!

I’d say your enemies complain about you doing this, but they’re too dead to fill in the forms. So you’re good.
keysToEntries.SteelBarbedArrows: Sometimes it’s not enough to just kill your enemy. There are times when you really want them to suffer as well. Steel arrows are nasty enough, but covering them in deadly barbs that will rend the flesh and empty the liquid lifeforce of a person onto the forest floor? That’s just overkill.
keysToEntries.SteelFireArrows: Some people just want to watch the world burn! Some people want to watch the world burn, but have thought through the logistics of fire and so decide to set the world aflame from a distance. These arrows are popular among the latter category.
keysToEntries.EnchantedSteelBolts:
Sharpened rods of metal, enchanted with magic. A surefire way to guarantee that your enemies have a particularly bad time.

In Fellhollow these bolts were vital against the undead, as they provided that additional firepower needed to take down the relentless tide of shambling corpses.
keysToEntries.Zogre:
It was Bandos who brought the ogres with him, in his crusade to destroy Lougrim and wipe out the garou. The ogres were powerful and deadly brutes, that smashed through the garou defences as though they were paper.

When Bandos left and the war stagnated, the ogres discovered peace for the first time in their lives. It was, refreshing. When some of the Bramblemead villagers left to travel north, the ogres followed them. When they arrived in Emberwood, the ogres offered to lay down their weapons and pick up farming tools instead.

For a while it was peaceful, even pleasant. The humans and ogres worked side by side and built a camaraderie that could have brought the region into a new age of prosperity.

Alas, that was not to be. Now these zogres wander the region with murderous intent. Hulking undead beasts capable of tearing a person a part with their bare hands. They see no reason. Seek no understanding. They exist only to destroy all life in their path.
keysToEntries.RotswornZombie:
What is worse than a shambling corpse that years to feast on all your precious fleshy parts? A shambling corpse that’s infected with a terrible rot that will tear your soul apart if you let it.

These zombies were once the most faithful followers of Imaru, her chosen servants. Each of them was branded with a special mark that would allow her to gift them a small semblance of her power. As humans they were healthier, lived longer and they thrived under her care.

Yet when Imaru’s hunger spread from the underworld, it was these unfortunates that fell first to this terrible affliction. Now they act as extensions to her hunger, sharing the rot with any unfortunate enough to cross their path.
keysToEntries.Zombie:
The dead should not move like this, yet these creatures don’t seem to have gotten that message. Undead are normally animated by magic, though some are motivated by stranger things.

These undead are animated by hunger, and the desperate final moments of what is left of their soul. Regrets, need and pain drive muscle, sinew and bone into motion, creating this most pitiable of creatures.
keysToEntries.RotswornMarauder:
The Scalesworn were heroes. Only the strongest, most skilled and powerful marauders would be chosen by Xikotal to act as guardians of the dragons. They would guard the great beasts as they slept, standing strong against any force that might try to do them harm.

They were blessed with the mark of the dragon, granting them some of the power of their draconic masters.

These marks would be their undoing, when Imaru refused to stay dead. They became the first of the infected, inflicted with a terrible withering curse that would drag the souls of any they touched into Imaru’s hungry maw.
keysToEntries.RotswornNecromancer:
Hatred guides these creatures, a vitriolic disgust at a world too cruel to function. They were once mages who had delved into the secrets of necromancy, desperate for power they were too foolish to wield.

The power within them has twisted and rotted, infected by Imaru’s vengeful rage. Their souls have been long hollowed out and now they are driven by a need to infect and corrupt, raising the dead in further service of vengeance.
keysToEntries.RotswornWarrior:
Foul creatures, warped by Imaru’s terrible hunger. They were once convicts and criminals, now cursed to serve as Imaru’s claws.

In the ages past those who had committed the gravest sins were given to Xikotal for conversion. He would breathe into them a new way of thinking, draconic magic creating a fog in their minds that erased their wicked past and offering them a newer future.

Some would be farmers. Others might live their lives in service of the village, even taking roles as priests or healers. The strongest would be given new identities, soldiers to stand as the dragon’s forces.

They were closest to Imaru when she died and as she drew in the souls around her they were the first to be afflicted. Withered and rotten, these tragic creatures remember nothing but a desire to infect and destroy.
keysToEntries.SkeletalArcher:
By the time you’ve heard their creaking bones, it’s already too late. Despite their lack of sinew, sensory organs, or anything that should make them this dead, the skeletal archer is surprisingly deft.

In life they were scouts, hunters or Wild Knight archers, in death they’re simply something best avoided.
keysToEntries.SkeletalHoplite:
In life the hoplites named themselves in honour of the legendary forces of Saradomin, back on Hallow. They took their identity from the sacred texts, creating a disciplined and loyal legion of skilled warriors.

Now, that discipline has all but faded. They forget the shield walls, or the turtle formation. They forget the bonds they once shared with their friends. Now they only remember that a spear must be thrust forward, preferably into someone else.
keysToEntries.SkeletalMarauder:
There were once warriors in Fellhollow that wielded great weapons with tremendous force. They were boastful, proud and dangerous people – yet they were beloved.

A tradition among these great hulking figures, was to seek out a far smaller person and act as their shield. They became the guardian angels of the bullied and the protectors of the abused. Wielding their great blades to fight against cruelty or unkindness, making the world happier by force.

Now that instinct is gone.

These skeletal creatures remember only that a sword must be swung, and that a blade needs to cut into flesh. They remember only that bone must be cleaved and that the living must be turned into the dead, swelling their armies with each swing of the blade.
keysToEntries.SkeletalWarrior:
Perhaps these were once guards, or soldiers. Wild Knights protecting the balance of the land. It is impossible to say who these creatures once were, because now all they are is merciless dead.

The tragic truth is that many of these people fought as hard as they could against the tide of undead. They stood at the gates to the village holding back the ravening horde. They walled themselves up in the keep, protecting their allies. They stood at the gate into Fellhollow and said “you will go no further!”. Yet, now, they are the monsters they fought so hard against.
keysToEntries.SkeletalNecromancer:
Power is a corrupting force, it can turn reasonable people into monsters. Have you ever noticed how a seemingly reasonable person can go mad with power in the silliest of roles? That’s what happened to the necromancers of Fellhollow.

At first they were mages, of varying skill. Some were scholars of the arcane. Others priests in training. But each of them was obsessed with the mystery of the dead. They would learn secrets in strange places. In strange whispers on the wind, in ancient tomes stolen from the mainland. Some even learned of the necromantic arts in their dreams.

They had sought to command the dead, but now they have become them. Their powers turned only to spreading their affliction, the last vestiges of their souls trying to claim some semblance of meaning when there is none left for them to take.
keysToEntries.UndeadRat: And you thought the giant rats were bad enough!
keysToEntries.RotriddenRat: These nasty little creatures embody all the viciousness of their living kin, but with the added hatred of an undead dragon. Their bites will wither your soul, threatening to drag you into Imaru's gaze.
keysToEntries.UndeadCow: Rotted and twisted, these cows have a terrible beef with the living!
keysToEntries.RotriddenCow: Once gentle creatures, the rotridden cows were the few cows that hadn't been filled with an ancestral rage against humanity, for the slaughter of their kin back on the mainland. Unfortunately, this gentle nature made them carriers for the cursed hunger of Imaru and now these cows seek to damage the very souls of their victims, rotting them from within.
keysToEntries.Dragonwolf:
The unholy fusion of dragon and beast, creations of twisted dragonkin science. These creatures seem somehow resistant to the cursed nature of the region, but they refuse to sit still long enough for anyone to ask them why.

Not that they would answer, they have a wolf’s mind, not a dragons.
keysToEntries.Imaru:
Once, Imaru was a gentle creature. She and her mate, the black dragon Xikotal, would dance through the air together, lost in their love. Although the dragon’s rage still burned inside her, the cooling power of the water she was infused with, calmed her and brought the reason and clarity that she enjoyed.

Dragonkin experiments had robbed her of the one thing she craved, to be a mother. So when the humans came to the region, she convinced Xikotal that they should take them under their wing, becoming protectors and, perhaps, even parents to the fragile things.

Together they built a society with peace and cooperation at its core. The villagers thrived, and Imaru and her mate thrived with them. She used the magic within her to bless her new pets, making sure that she could protect them wherever they were.

How cruel a thing is fate? Once protector, Imaru became the threat. After being slain by Kuldra, Imaru became a blighted thing. She refused to pass into the afterlife and wedged herself within the river of souls, feasting on the dead of Fellhollow in search of power. Once the guardian of her beloved adopted children, Imaru was lost to everything but the rage and the grief. She would have torn apart the world for one more shot at Kuldra.

Fortunately, that won’t be an option.
keysToEntries.WitherReductionPotion:
As the hunger of Imaru spread across Fellhollow, it tore the souls of its victims from the bodies they had been previously so attached to. It was impossible to work out who would suffer the affliction next, and that created great unrest among the people, since their friends and family could become mindless undead at any moment.

The priests of Zamorak, surprisingly, were the first to discover a temporary solution, creating a potion infused with the rebellious nature of Zamorak. As Imaru attempted to draw the souls from the bodies of people, they would drink this and then she found this meal much harder to digest. The souls fought back against her hunger, locking themselves to their flesh with chains Zamorak would not have approved of.

The effects weren’t a permanent end to the problem, but they delayed the condition long enough for people to spot the signs and remove the problem from the Emberwood border. Infected villagers would be cast out of the protective barrier where they would face their fate alone.
keysToEntries.WitherIncreasePotion:
Love is a strange emotion, powerful enough to start wars and destroy nations. It overrides reason, instilling all it afflicts with a beautiful sort of madness that many never choose to cure.

In Fellhollow it was the reason for many a life cut short. One man, Haager, was in love, utterly and completely with a farmhand called Claus. The two of them would meet in the pumpkin patches, laughing with one another about the village gossip. On moonlit nights they would dance in the silvery rays, forgetting the world around them and focused only on one another.

So Haagar’s mind broke alongside his heart, when Claus returned to those fields as a wither-rotted zombie.

He had been trained as a alchemist, and had spent some time studying the secrets of magic. Potions could perform wonderous feats, so he set himself to the task of reuniting himself with Claus.

He created a potion that would encourage within him the same affliction as had befallen the love of his life, and he smiled as his soul withered as a result.

It is said, that under a full moon in Fellhollow, two zombies can be seen dancing hand in hand in the ruins that were once farmland.
keysToEntries.ThornyIritRoot: Irit is normally much more manageable and leafy, but here in Fellhollow it was twisted into a hateful thorn-covered vine. Mind your fingers when harvesting it.
keysToEntries.WillowTree: Legends say that willow trees house the weeping spirits of those who died in the woods. Anywhere but Fellhollow this would be nonsense, but in a land so heavily infused with the dead, you can’t rule it out.
keysToEntries.CoalRock: A rock, with coal in it. The implications of so much coal would cause any Gielinorian historian a headache, reminding them that there is so much about the world that they may never know.
keysToEntries.CadavaberryBush: Deadly, poisonous berries. Delicious deadly berries.
keysToEntries.CorpseCottonPlant: Why would nature create such a thing? Seriously, why? What cruel god thought that this was a reasonable plant to add to the world?
keysToEntries.InspiringAmuletOfAttack.SoldiersAmulet:
Magic lives in the dirt, the ore and the rocks. It crystallises inside gemstones, matrices of magical energy just waiting to be unlocked. In Brynmoor the priests of Saradomin kept the secrets of magical amulets within their purview. They would craft jewellery that would enhance the fighting instinct of their soldiers, and hand then out like candy.

As the faith in Saradomin rose, the faith in Guthix waned. Partially because of the more active and present proselytising of the Saraodminist priests, who where more vocal than the quiet druids, but also because the paladins and bronze knights seemed significantly stronger than the Guthix-aligned guard. These amulets being their secret weapon.
keysToEntries.InspiringAmuletOfRanged.ArchersAmulet:
The Wild Knights used many tools and tricks in their hunts. Looking for any edge they might have against the untamed wilds of Ashenfall. One jeweller, a water-Amalgamated by the name of Lochlan, created an amulet imbued with the communal spirit of the hunters. He would leave the gemstone, that would serve as its base, in the gathering hall and soon it became a tradition for each hunter to touch the gemstone if their hunt was successful.

Over the years this gemstone took on their pride, prowess and skill and that became a magical enchantment that lay within the heart of the stone. Crafting an amulet from the crystal, Lochlan found a way of sharing that enchantment with everyone who wore it.

Now, millennia later, the echoes of that enchantment linger within the crystals, just waiting for someone to remember them.
keysToEntries.InspiringAmuletOfMagic.MagesRing:
It is perhaps not surprising that wizard’s made use of magical jewellery as early as possible. Indeed the first few pieces worn on Ashenfall were little more than decoration, and gaudy wizards would attempt to outdo one another with shinier and shinier pieces.

As part of their ever increasing “prank war” wizards began to create jewellery that was enchanted with effects designed to frustrate, or otherwise hinder, the wearer, believing that enchantments in jewellery were little more than silly trinkets. One mage created what she assumed was an auditory illusion, within an amulet, the intention being that when the wearer would try and cast a spell, the jewellery would play an annoying earworm tune, distracting the poor caster.

This did not happen. Something about the threads of magic, the structure of the crystal and the concealing spell that was intended to hide the prank, created something unique. Instead of making it harder to cast spell, the wearers found it easier. Understanding came faster, spells and incantations clearer than before.

What was intended to distract instead focused the wearer, allowing them to concentrate in ways they had never been able to before.
keysToEntries.InspiringRingOfMining.ProspectorsRing:
The dwarves know that the earth has a memory, gemstones especially so. What they don’t tell you, is that the earth holds a grudge. Each time a pick pierces the stone or smashes a piece of ore from its home, the earth remembers.

A skilled enchanter can use that grudge, that earthen memory, and turn it back against the grudge-filled earth. Traditionally this is done via jewellery, with dwarven goldsmiths making most of their living from miners purchasing these trinkets.
keysToEntries.InspiringRingOfWoodcutting.LumberjacksRi:
If trees could move, they would kill us all. That’s the terrible truth that no lumberjack speaks of, instead they silently protect the rest of us from arboreal wrath. When a lumberjack was trained by the druids in Brynmoor, and taught the secrets of the murderous woods, they would be gifted a ring to help them in their battle against the vile trees.

These trinkets have helped lumberjacks defend us all from the cruelty of the woods, in a war that most don’t even believe is happening.
keysToEntries.InspiringRingOfRunecrafting.Runecrafters:
Ashenfall was the first place that Guthix created runes, but he never passed those secrets on to his followers. It wasn’t until the legendary human, V, discovered the secret, that it was taught to the general population.

Still, something about Ashenfall makes the process easier. No altars are needed, nor talisman’s required, yet runes can be crafted with an abundance that would make the mainland guild’s jealous.

This ring taps into that ease, infusing the soul with some of that ancient knowledge of the island. Be careful sleeping with this on, as you may dream the memories of runecrafters past.

Or of a dog made of cheese. No one knows why.
keysToEntries.InspiringRingOfArtisan.CraftersRing:
No one quite knows why this ring works. It’s as if ancestral grandmothers hover behind the wearer, whispering to them the ancient secrets of their crafting techniques. Old styles, long since lost to tragedy or history, can be restored in a flash of inspiration when someone wears this amulet.

For most, though, they simply find themselves in a trance like state – able to ignore the world around them and focus on their craft. Learning more and more about it with each moment that passes.
keysToEntries.InspiringRingOfConstruction.BuildersRing:
This ring is infused with tiny elements of the dragonkin Brakka, a powerful creature and an architect of some skill. His hubris remains, even after his death at Kuldra’s hands, and motes of his knowledge can be preserved within crystalline structures.

Although you cannot glean the secrets of the dragonkin from these motes of memory, his instinct and innate talent for construction and be harnessed to create a ring that makes it easier to understand the art of architecture.
keysToEntries.InspiringRingOfCooking.ChefsRing:
Cooking is a form of magic that few people truly master. Meals can return lost memories, shatter dour moods and rekindle friendships long since abandoned.

This ring was created by accident, when a parent was trying to teach a child how to cook. They were holding the unfinished piece, absent-mindedly, as they explained to their progeny about all the steps needed to understand a recipe.

This knowledge, infused with the emotion of a parents instruction, enchanted the ring so that all future wearers might benefit from their knowledge.
keysToEntries.RingOfRecoil:
Vengeance isn’t a dish best served cold, but one served quickly. Whilst taking years to enact the cruellest retaliation might work in the civilised world, in the wilds of Ashenfall a quick strike is far more valuable.

This ring was first created by one of the Amalgamated, worn as both deterrence and for some small satisfaction when they were targeted by vigilante mobs. It didn’t prevent the bruises, or fix the broken bones, but their assailants attacks would end much more quickly when they discovered they weren’t as untouchable as they thought.
keysToEntries.RingOfLife:
Harold has a job for you and won’t let something as trivial as your expired mortality get in the way. This piece of enchanted jewellery is primed to go off when you’re about to land on Death’s list, whisking you away to safety before the scythe needs to be swung.

There are many sorcerers that have studied these rings for years, going slowly mad as they try to uncover the secrets of immortality. If they could just learn the methods by which this ring operates, then they could extend their lives indefinitely.

Only one wizard ever actually learned this secret, but he forgot the method by which this ring works, for it teleports you at the moment of your death. As the ring does not stop the aging process, this unfortunate mage now spends his eternity in perpetual teleportation – hurled through the abyss, never to take form again.
keysToEntries.AmuletOfStrength:
Might makes right, is the cry of every wannabe despot. Bandos in particular was very fond of this statement. This amulet upholds that notion and does its best to help make sure you are the most correct person around.

It imbues your muscles with body-aligned anima, the magic strengthening the sinew and fortifying your bones. It adds greater force to your swing that will crush your enemies before you.

The goblin general, Snotknucke, wore this amulet and attempted to hunt down Velgar. He believed that, with its magic, he could fell the beast in a single swing of his club. Snotknuckle was stronger, deadlier and more powerful than ever, but this did very little against the rock that Velgar dropped on his head.
keysToEntries.AmuletOfDefence:
Not everyone can wear armour when faced with the hordes of shambling undead. Children and the frail, become targets with little to protect them from gnashing teeth and jaws. In these scenarios magic becomes incredibly important, and these amulets delayed the deaths of countless lives.

Not everyone was happy with such utilitarian approaches to survival, however. As the days drew on and resources grew scarce, empathy began to wane in the population of Emberwood. Amulets of defence were handed out to those determined to have the most need, but the criteria for that became a hot subject of contention.

It would be nice to imagine a world where everyone pulled together to defend against the undead, but human nature sadly shattered that dream. Still, while there would always be those who would attempt to steal the amulets from the needy, there were plenty more who would stand against them.

These amulets now carry an echo of those protectors, shielding its wearers from harm.
keysToEntries.AmuletOfAccuracy:
The Wild Knights knew the dangers of missing their target. If an arrow did not slay its target quickly, then too soon would the quarry either escape, or, worse, gather friends. Wolf packs could easily tear a small group of Knights to pieces if not dealt with fast.

The Amulet of Accuracy made sure that hunts went smoothly. When a shot landed, it landed hard. Alpha wolves would be slain in a single well-placed shot, and sturdier threats might go down in a small flurry of arrows.

When Silverthorn Keep stood against the rising horde of zombies, these amulets were worn by the keep defenders who unleashed a torrent of arrows into the hungry dead.
keysToEntries.AmuletOfMagic:
Emberwood had its fair share of spellcasters, though lacking the funding of the tower to the south. With more limited resources the mages of Emberwood found other methods to strengthen their art, and the Amulet of Magic was popular among all of them.

Druids worse these amulets while performing rituals around the standing stones, hoping that it might strengthen their bond with nature. The self-proclaimed warlocks, of the Children of Shadows, would sit in darkened basements wearing these glittering amulets as they intoned dark rites that had no effect. The Wild Knight mages would use them more pragmatically, wielding magic against all the threats that the villagers were unaware of.
keysToEntries.SalveAmulet:
The name is a bit of a misnomer, since this amulet has very little to do with the river Salve. Instead its name refers to the crystal that traditionally sits at the heart. This crystal was first brought to the region by Saradominist priests, some time in the third age. They had harvested it from a cave near the river Salve and found ways to cultivate on Ashenfall’s soil, by utilising the natural magic infusing everything.

Sanctified by faith and magic, this amulet was deadly to the undead, and the dragonkin Lacrussa was fascinated with the properties. She stole the recipe for its creation and hid it within her vault, long before the withering curse spread over Fellhollow. Instead she had delved too deeply in her own experiments and discovered something buried within the earth that would twist and reanimate the dead.

This amulet allowed her to hold the forces back and keep them from overrunning her lab. That was, until, she met her end at Kuldra’s fangs.
keysToEntries.WolfbaneDagger: Vannaka knows this dagger only too well, for it was used against him every time he tried to prove his mettle against the other slayer masters. Although he would never willingly transform to prove his might, the unsettling sight of the dagger broke his morale enough to make him falter, and that was enough of an edge for his defeat.
keysToEntries.ShadowSword:
It might be better to refer to this sword as a smoke sword, given its wispy nature, but there is no denying that this is an ephemeral blade aligned with the world of the dead.

It feels cold to the touch and sometimes, when you least expect it, you are almost certain you can hear whispering coming from the blade.
keysToEntries.DorgeshuunCrossbow:
The cave goblins are a practical sort, using materials more readily available underground than any that might require potential damage to their subterranean dwellings, such as mining. This crossbow is made from bones, traditionally of creatures found beneath the surface.

Zanik once used this crossbow to defeat a deadly spectre in the underworld. A being infused with some dread malign power, that threatened the denizens of Um. Normal weapons had no effect on the creature, yet for some reason the bone bolts fired by this crossbow were enough to unravel its material form, banishing it back to the darkness.
keysToEntries.BoneBolts: Special bolts whittled from bones, whilst only of use for a very specific crossbow, these bolts pack a surprising amount of punch.
keysToEntries.UndeadRangersBow:
There was a villager in Emberwood called Holtz, who was a master with the bow. He could shoot rats from across a field and never scored less than a bullseye whenever he aimed for an archery target. Yet he had no desire for warfare, and no matter the words of encouragement from the captain, he would never join the guard.

For him, archery was a passion, an art, never to be used against another person. A stance quickly forgotten by his shambling corpse.
keysToEntries.NecromancerStaff:
Necromancy isn’t inherently evil, despite what some of the scriptures might say. Whilst some cruelly force the dead into service, others make bargains and pacts with spirits, offering them a chance to influence the mortal world again, rather than simply watch events unfold.

In Fellhollow a small group of mages encountered a smoking fissure in the earth. From it they heard strange whispering voices in the darkness, promising them power and knowledge. Greedily they listened, making pacts with the whispering in exchange for the ability to bind the dead to their service.

In life they performed their arts in secret, raising corpses none would miss, or binding ghosts no one even knew were haunting them. Yet in death, that’s where their true power lay.

With their souls withered away and consumed by the dread Imaru, the necromancers were no longer limited by the last vestiges of morality, and with the new powers at their command, turned a struggling defence against the undead, into a horror-filled nightmare where each fallen ally would rise as a foe.
keysToEntries.ImarusTalon:
The essence of an undead dragon, distilled down into a blade. What could go wrong?

This weapon is cold to the touch in ways far beyond chilling. Holding it feels as if you’re out in the cold, with no shelter to protect you. Exposure in the palm of your hand.

Worse than the cold, is the hunger. This scimitar wants to cut, to stab, to slice and to maim. It wants to see your enemies bleed so that it can feast on their suffering. Imaru may be dead, but her hunger is eternal and you feel it infecting you with each swing of the blade.

Still, the power of this blade makes it almost worth it. What’s a little hunger when faced with the destruction of your foes? Lament later, surely, and revel now in the carnage. Perhaps there’s a little dragon in you too?
keysToEntries.WhiteFullHelm:
Sir Percival Lateef was a proud man, assured of his own prowess, character and moral standing. On the mainland, he would train the other knights in swordsmanship, as well as host several long and laborious lectures on his supposed life as an explorer. He had travelled across the lands, to the frozen polar regions, and the jungles of Karamja, but no one ever seemed particularly interested in his stories.

Tired of being ignored and teased for his exploits, he took a boat and sailed it north of the mainland into regions unknown. His boat was destroyed. His crew left for dead, and he found himself on a unknown beach of strange, unnatural hues. The first human on Ashenfall for centuries, he was finally the explorer he dreamed himself to be.
keysToEntries.WhitePlatebody:
Sir Percival Lateef only had a short moment of wonder, before the horror struck. The beach he had arrived on was surrounded by cliffs, meaning his only options were to climb or to swim, and since he was wearing the heavy armour of the White Knights, he decided that sinking wasn’t an option.

It took him many hours and several near falls, but he eventually made his way up and down the cliffside, only to encounter a region filled with the ravenous undead.

As a White Knight he trained to fight such evils and he fended them off as best he could, yet there were too many for him to face alone. He was forced to retreat, to a shrine, atop a rocky island, displaying the symbol of Saradomin, and there he prayed.
keysToEntries.WhitePlatelegs:
Trapped by a shrine that stood atop a rocky island, surrounded by the hungry dead, Sir Lateef prayed to Saradomin with all his strength. He begged for salvation, for a miracle that might free him, but silence was his only answer.

He barricaded himself atop the monument, the dead scrabbling at his defences, their unholy growling keeping him from even a moment’s sleep.

As time drew on, and hunger and thirst took him, he turned to the symbol of Saradomin in defeated rage. Where was his god? Why was he abandoned? Had he not spent his life in service of the lord of light?

Enraged, he took his sword and cleaved Saradomin’s symbol from its plinth, causing it to crash to the floor. Filled with guilt, he hid the fallen piece from his sight. His last act was one of blasphemy, but perhaps an act justly performed.
keysToEntries.RangerHat:
During the final years of Bramblemead corruption had run rampant. Nobles took more than their share and the priests who watched over them turned a blind eye in exchange for their own share of glittering coin. Those who did not possess noble blood, found that their lives became harder, their earnings became smaller and joy became a much more fleeting feeling than it had done before.

One woman decided to change that. She would attack trader caravans marked with goods for the castle, taking from their purses of coin. Tax collectors would also feel the sting of her arrows, surrendering their wealth to the woman clad in green. She was given the name Nightingale, because of the whistling noise her arrows made as they flew through the air, an almost musical tone that sent fear through her targets.

Villagers would then wake in the morning following an attack, to find purses of coin in flowerboxes, window shelves, or resting neatly under the fattest, laziest, cats.
keysToEntries.RangerTunic:
The Nightingale was the bane of the nobility, and they became obsessed with her destruction. Not just her capture, they needed her obliterated – gone from the memories of the population forever. But this was a tricky task indeed, because the wealth the Nightingale harvested, was given back to the people of Bramblemead, rather than lining her own coffers, making her a local hero.

So they hired Moragrin the hunter to track her down. Moragrin was a cruel and merciless assassin, trained in multiple weapon techniques. She delighted in little more than seeing the look in her victims eyes the moment they transition from living to dead.

For days she followed stories of Nightingale, wandering through the village, adopting multiple disguises that she might uncover Nightingale’s real name. In all that time she learned only one thing, that the villagers loved Nightingale and would never knowingly give her up.
keysToEntries.RangerTights:
It was on a cold winter night, that Nightingale and Moragrin finally met. The assassin had given up on interrogating the villagers and worked instead to lay a trap for the highwaywoman. A caravan, loaded with gold, made its way from village to castle under the cover of night, and Nightingale arrived to claim her bounty.

It was a short, but brutal battle. Arrows whistled through the air, making music like an orchestra. Daggers glinted in the moonlight, in a dance of death that would have shredded the life of any normal person. But Nightingale and Moragrin were evenly matched, until an errant magpie brought it all to an end.

Confused by the battle, the magpie had woken from its sleep in a mad panic and dived down at the fighting pair. The distraction was just enough for Moragrin’s blade to pierce the Nightingale’s heart and as she pulled her mask from her victim, Moragrin’s world shattered as she saw the moment the life left the eyes of her younger sister.
keysToEntries.NecromancersCrown:
Those who wield the power over life and death have a tendency to crave control. They need to manage things in their life on a pathological level, and this need often grows into a desire to manage the lives of everyone else.

The most well known necromancer in Fellhollow was a man called Calius Waithe. He was one of the Children of Shadows until he decided that their policies of peaceful evil wasn’t what he was looking for. He was more an overtly evil sort of guy.

He decided, on one fateful autumnal eve, to raise an army of the undead and march them on Emberwood. He imagined the village cowering before him as he named himself their new king.
keysToEntries.NecromancerRobeTop:
Callius Waithe created an army of the undead and marched them on Emberwood. Or, rather, he called his shambling little horde an army, even though it only consisted of five shambling skeletons in various degrees of disrepair.

The village guard took aim at the skeletons, but their arrows lacked the strength to shatter bone, and Callius marched them through the gates with barely any opposition. Still, once he had made it through the gate he was not met with the terror he had hoped for. While shambling skeletons were certainly a fright that caused much shock, their limited numbers helped to reduce the terror.

A small child even giggled and tried to offer one skeleton an apple. An act that filled Callius with unreasonable rage.
keysToEntries.NecromancerRobeBottom:
Faced with the humiliation of villagers not fleeing in terror, the necromancer Callius Waithe was forced to use more harrowing tactics. He grabbed a jar of oil from a nearby blacksmith and threw it over his skeletal minions, and then he set them aflame. For what could be more terrifying than the shambling dead? Why, the shambling burning dead, of course!

This did cause the panic he wanted. Villagers fled the flames quickly, running to the wells in case of the inevitably flaming spread. Spread it did, for while the skeletons themselves did not burn, the oil covering them erupted into excited tongues of flame. Callius laughed at his evil genius, until the flames spread beyond the means of his control.

It is said that on autumn evenings, when the moon is full, the ghost of Callius can be seen screaming in the village, running through the streets trying to put out spectral flames.
keysToEntries.FallenHopliteApsis:
The hoplites of Emberwood named themselves after Saradomin’s forces in the holy scripture. The legendary hoplites were warriors on the world of Hallow, who were trained from farmers and artisans, rather than military families or nobility, and the Emberwood hoplites came from similar stock.

They were best known for their fighting style using spears and shields, with the shield being the defining feature. These shields were designed to be worn for longer periods of time, resting against the shoulder for more comfortable carrying.

The hoplites may have been raised from peasant stock, but their camaraderie made them a force to be reckoned with on the battlefield.

As the withering took them, the hoplites retained the rote memory of their training and remained a deadly threat to all who faced them.
keysToEntries.ImaruDragonfireShield: A shield with the visage of Imaru the devourer welded to the front. Holding the shield feels cold to the touch and instils within you a hunger for battle. Your blood pumps faster with a terrible need to rend your enemy limb from limb.
keysToEntries.FallenHopliteHelm:
Those who joining the Fellhollow military, who were not from the more noble bloodlines, would join the force known as the hoplites. This force of warrior farmers named themselves after the hoplite forces of Hallow, legendary forces spoken of in some of the Saradominist text.

They were disciplined in a way that earned them great respect. Each of them acting as the shield for the person next to them and a spear for the enemy in front. They wore armour reflecting some of the style of Hallow, with plateskirts rather than sturdy grieves, allowing for more freedom of movement, a trait they took great advantage of.
keysToEntries.FallenHopliteChest:
Those who joining the Fellhollow military, who were not from the more noble bloodlines, would join the force known as the hoplites. This force of warrior farmers named themselves after the hoplite forces of Hallow, legendary forces spoken of in some of the Saradominist text.

These hoplites would meet at the end of the week, to train and practice battle manoeuvres. They became walls of impenetrable shields, or terrifying spiked shells, moving with the sort of unity that only comes over years and years of training.

Their secret, so they claimed, was the weekly barbeque. Once training was resolved, if they had performed well enough, the retired members of the hoplites would cook them delicious meats and roasted vegetables, served with an overly generous helping of alcoholic beverages.
keysToEntries.FallenHopliteTassets:
Those who joining the Fellhollow military, who were not from the more noble bloodlines, would join the force known as the hoplites. This force of warrior farmers named themselves after the hoplite forces of Hallow, legendary forces spoken of in some of the Saradominist text.

When the withering began it was the hoplites that were most affected. Their unity and loyalty became a weakness, as members within their ranks fell to the affliction and then turned on the others. This shattered their synchronicity and spread paranoia and distrust among the ranks.

As their members fell, they would rise as the undead and immediately turn on their brethren, ripping the group apart from the inside.

At least in death they stand together again.
keysToEntries.FellhollowCape:
Fellhollow could have been a new utopia, were it not for the cruelty of the godeater. Imaru and Xikotal adored their human neighbours and dreamed of a society where both dragon and human could thrive together in peace.

Now the land, like that dream, has broken. A hateful hunger from beyond the grave has consumed all the life, love and joy in the land. The dead roam desperately seeking to tear the tiniest threads of life from any living thing that crosses their path, and the afterlife itself, here, is poisoned by Imaru’s grief.

Yet, once, long ago, this land was beautiful. A myriad of colours danced in the breeze, the anima twist turning the region into a kaleidoscopic wonder. The now ruined streets were once filled with laughter, joy and song. And the dragons danced across the skies in loved-laced poetry.

This cape is a burieal shroud for a world that could have been.
keysToEntries.CapeOfTheUndead: This cape is all that remains of the once proud archers of Fellhollow. Hunters and scouts who would stalk the forests and woodland hunting game to feed the other villagers. As the time dragged on their flesh and clothes have rotted away, leaving only this cape as a reminder of their existence.
keysToEntries.UndeadRangerHood:
The last hunter in Fellhollow hid in the trees for as long as he could. Beneath him the dead shambled and stumbled and clawed at the bark, yet he remained in the trees safely out of reach.

He fed himself on nuts and berries, stealing form the treasure troves of squirrels and birds, occasionally risking a trip to the ground to hunt a passing animal. He gave up on cooking the meat he hunted, as he could not stay on the ground long enough before the dead rose up to face him, so he tore into the carcasses of deer, sheep and rats, trying his best not to vomit.

He barely noticed when his appetite changed, since he was so used raw flesh and the sickly cold taste of blood pooling in his mouth. So, when the withering began to take him, he hardly noticed.

Sometimes, if you’re quick enough, you may catch a glimpse of a skeleton in the tree branches. Even in death he fears the monsters on the ground.
keysToEntries.BlueCape: An elegant cloth cape in a delightful azure hue.
keysToEntries.RedCape: A fashionable scarlet accessory for the most daring fashionistas.
keysToEntries.YellowCape: Dress like a king with this fabulous yellow cape.
keysToEntries.PurpleCape: Inspired by royalty, this cape is worn by the most elegant adventurers.
keysToEntries.BlackCape: Dress like a creature of the night with this sinister cloak.
keysToEntries.OrangeCape: For those with a love of the autumn, this cape is a must have.
keysToEntries.PinkCape: A cape worn by those with a love of pony clubbing. Which is not as sinister as it sounds…honest.
keysToEntries.GreenCape: Be the envy of druids everywhere with this fabulous accessory.
keysToEntries.WhiteCape: Cleanliness is next to godliness, so some of the white knights claimed. Live your bleached dreams with this immaculate accessory.
keysToEntries.PrimordialHeart:
These strange hearts contain the lingering essence of a nature spirit. A gentle heartbeat, waiting to be reborn to a world free of Imaru’s corruption.

Ashenfall’s nature spirits are creatures of the land, possessing myriad forms and shapes as dictated by the wild anima flowing around them. In Fellhollow their favoured form is that of the terrorbird, a beast favoured by gnome riders on the mainland.

As Imaru’s hunger infected the land, it began to leech away the vitality that kept these creatures healthy and strong. Their forests waned, from great acres of willow trees, down to a handful of groves, the last places their power would reach.

As the land dies, so too do the spirits that protect it. As creatures of the endless cycle, they must be hunted, die and be reborn to return to strength. Yet, there are no hunters in Fellhollow any more. No great beasts roaming the land to slay these gentle spirits.

So, you must be that hunter. You must kill the spirit, so that it may be reborn when Fellhollow thrives once more. How ever many years from now that may be.
keysToEntries.ImarusHead:
A rotting and twisted dragon head, momentary twitches still flicker through it, the last remnants of necromancy at play.

The tragedy of Imaru is that she was once a sweet and gentle creature. Cruel dragonkin experiments had robbed her of the only thing she had ever wanted, to be a mother. So when the humans came to her land, she saw in them the children she never had. Xikotal, her mate, encouraged this nurturing behaviour, knowing the joy it would bring his beloved.

She could have been happy, and the people of Fellhollow could have been happy with her.
