keysToEntries.Curly: Curly resents being called curly. Her hair merely bends back towards her body because she is SO HELLISHLY ALLURING.
keysToEntries.Boggs: Boggs would like to sit on your head and pull your hair so that you make HELL-RATATOUILLE.
keysToEntries.Reek: Reek doesn't reek. He just has an aroma THAT IS UNAPPRECIATED IN HIS OWN TIME.
keysToEntries.Nosey: Nosey watched you while you were sleeping. She thought you were TERRIBLE AT IT.
keysToEntries.Pip: Pip is allergic to apples, which makes her name DEVIOUSLY IRONIC.
keysToEntries.Patch: Patch likes evil stew but would prefer if you ordered it for him as he is UNAPOLOGETICALLY INTROVERTED.
keysToEntries.Raven: The raven wakes early and perches on the battlements of Dowdun Reach. There it can watch for adventurers. It longs for them to suffer a grisly end, not because a raven feels malice, but because a grisly corpse is easier to feast on. The food has been taken out of its wrapper.
keysToEntries.CarnivorousChinchompa: Nature can often miss a punchline. Carnivorous chinchompas do not, in fact, like to chomp on chins. They aim a smidge lower and prefer jugulars.
keysToEntries.CursedEssence:
Cursed essence is a magical agent often used in tanning. It is also something of a curiosity, one that a simple tanner can't readily explain. It goes further than simply binding itself to material; it latches onto it, almost parasitically. 

Demonologists have a theory that, although cursed essence drops from avernic demons of Dowdun Reach, it is actually cthonic in nature. It would explain the tendril-like tenacity.
keysToEntries.MithrilDeposit:
Doric is peeved that mithril is predominantly in Zamorakian territory. Dwarves feel a kinship to the ore: it means a little more to them than the other metals. In his view, Zamorakians don't deserve it.

He won't admit it if you confront him, but it's a big reason that you can find him in Dowdun Reach. One eye on the mithril. If he had it his way, he'd be laying minecart tracks all the way from Dowdun Reach to Temple Woods. Then he'd have to fight the urge to make mithril platebodies until the end of his days.
keysToEntries.LimestoneRock:
If you like the look of this rock, imagine what it could like in your house. Carve off a few chunks with your pickaxe and save up for a fetching limestone interior. The white will complement your eyes.

You might be surprised to learn that Vannaka refuses to use limestone in any capacity. Someone told him that you can contract lime's disease from it, a rash that pains the joints and nerves. "Can't be spoiling these reflexes", he'd say, swinging an axe.

Don't try to correct the man - it's not worth it.
keysToEntries.MapleLogs:
Maple is a versatile tree. It's a formidable material, making some of the finest fishing rods, staves and bows. Its sap is also a glorious thing, capable of being made into nature's nectar: maple syrup.

Its leaf is also smooth and large enough that it can be folded over numerous times, which makes it useful if you're caught short in the forest. Which, we should add, is not, and will not be, a consideration for you. It is extremely unlikely that a 'being caught short', hunger-like bar will be added for you to maintain. Realism can be pushed too far.
keysToEntries.MithrilOre:
Mithril means much to the dwarves. It was first discovered by a dwarf called Tolkun in the First Age, and its seams have been uncovered in much of Gielinor. The dwarves call it 'The Dwarven Welcome' thanks to its shine. Find the ore when mining and it lights up to greet you: a dwarven welcome. 

A 'Dwarven Welcome' is also what dwarves call a headbutt while wearing a mithril helmet.
keysToEntries.Limestone:
Turn your limestone this way and that and you might come across a fossil. Lesser blue dragons have been in the region for some time, and there were experimentations that led to them. Being experimentations, they weren't cognitively blessed, so it's entirely possible to find the talon of a dragon that got trapped in a rockfall.

You could also find the odd chicken wing from the Second Age. The Children of Shadows loved a shake and bake.
keysToEntries.BlueDragonhide:
Blacksmiths hate this one simple trick. They spend their lives mastering interlocking chains and plates, only for dragons to be born with them. A third of the blacksmith's punters are now gone, hunting for dragons rather than dropping some coin at their forges. It's enough to make a blacksmith sob into their anvil. 

Blue dragons hate that one simple trick too, of course. Their venn diagram overlaps with the blacksmith's: they'd rather people got their armours from somewhere other than dragons.
keysToEntries.MysticFibre:
The origin of mystic fibres, thread and cloth are wreathed in mystery. There's little to no mention in any written histories, which - on its own - seems unlikely. It is not a subtle material. There should be log after log of merchants trading it, or actively avoiding trading it. 

It leads me to believe that the mystic material is an elaborate troll. It's a plant - and I don't mean that in the floral definition. I think someone has introduced it as a joke, and I have a list of potential culprits. Brassica Prime: you are number one on that list.
keysToEntries.DreamShard: A little like a conch, you can put this infernal shard to your ear. But instead of hearing the sea, you might hear the mumblings of an ancient mind. It's no less soothing, though. If you ever have trouble sleeping, we recommend the calming white-noise of a fractured malevolent soul.
keysToEntries.VileAshes:
Demons do not leave bones behind on death. That would require a skeleton, a prospect that would make a demon queasy. Instead, demons are magical entities, composed of essence and energies. On death, they disperse, leaving behind an ashy composite.

Don't be mistaken in thinking that it's similar to the ash left after a fire. Dab your tongue in vile ash and you'll soon understand where the 'vile' comes from.
keysToEntries.BlackMetalBar:
Congratulations on killing enough Black Knights to form a bar from their resulting scraps. Don't think about the children you have orphaned, the dreams that you have impaled on the sharp end of a greatsword. Don't think about the concentrated misery you hold in your hands. 

It is rather shiny, though, is it not? Black Metal, particularly if you have the draconic sort (more chromatic, less matte), is mesmerising.
keysToEntries.BlueDragonBlood:
What, you were expecting blue dragon blood to be blue? You wouldn't expect human blood to be flesh-coloured, would you? Why does that though disgust me so much?

Blue dragon blood is an apothecary's dream. It's volatile, and therefore heavily reactive, but it has a kind of built-in warning system. It will change colour and emit a sulphurous stench for a few seconds before reacting, giving ample time to evacuate.
keysToEntries.MapleSap:
It's like the maple tree wants to be cut down. Not only does it produce hardy, versatile wood for tools and weaponry, it bleeds sap that is only a single step away from being maple syrup. If you want proof of the gods' existence in RuneScape, you need only look at maple trees. 

The maple tree gets its revenge in subtle ways. Its sap is inordinately sticky and too much syrup will give you a paunch. They're small victories, but the maple tree takes them.
keysToEntries.BlueDragonScale:
There is a curious trend on Gielinor, whereby the most fragile fighters - archers and magic users - are the easiest to spot. From blue dragonhide to mystic robes, the fashion is to wear garish colours as if the rules of the animal kingdom apply. But no, archers and magic users aren't poisonous, venomous or hoping to be mistaken for a bee.

We wonder why that lack of subtlety might be. In the case of blue dragons and their scales, perhaps it's a cruel joke played by the dragonkin. They have seen that humans keep killing their creations, wearing their hides as trophies. They've got their revenge by way of a generous use of the rainbow.
keysToEntries.MysticThread:
There are many steps to making mystic robes - fibres to thread, thread to cloth, cloth to robes - but you would be wise not to skip any of them. Habberdash the Impatient, a magician who lived in Temple Woods, attempted to make his robes solely from thread. He would wrap himself, round and round, with spool after spool of mystic thread, and then attempt battle.

You can imagine how it went. He got snagged on a goblin's helmet and quickly unwound, mid-battle. We're not sure who would have been more shocked: Habberdash, who promptly got stabbed in the gut, or the goblin, who was confronted with a wizard in his undergarments.
keysToEntries.MysticCloth:
It is almost imperceptible, but mystic cloth hums. It's the magical resonance: the elemental threads pushing against each other as their charge shifts. That oscillation also means that there is a lightly massaging effect on the wearer. It's entirely possible to feel calm from the vibrations as you stride into battle.

You might also be mistaken for thinking that mystic cloth is blue. On a microscopic level, it's actually a multitude of colours, combining to form the illusion of blue. As a magician once told me, it's the same effect as a blue sky: it is the result of all the colours in the world combining to make wondrous azure.
keysToEntries.MithrilBar:
In the Third Age on Ashenfall, mithril was incredibly desirable. It was only available in small quantities then, as its scarcity - perhaps inevitably - made it the fashion of the time. High-priests would incorporate it into their rosaries. Community leaders would wear it as brooches. It was rare enough that people would comment when they saw it. "Ooh, Rosie's doing well for herself". That sort of thing. 

The idea of a mithril bar would have been mind-boggling to anyone in the Third Age. No matter their alignment, they'd have stabbed you in the belly and run away cackling with it. But here you are with an entire ingot. Take a moment and consider yourself a Third Age king.
keysToEntries.BlueDragonhideLeather:
A discerning archer would do well to craft blue dragonhide leather. The colour is fetching, the defence against magic is second-to-none, and the material has an elasticity that makes it perfect for the nimble stuff that archers tend to do.

If it has a downside, it's a little tight. There's no hiding in blue dragonhide chaps, for example: all the lumps and bumps are out there.
keysToEntries.BlackMetalScraps:
Fun fact: black metal is actually steel that has gone through a form of vulcanisation. Demonic or draconic sulphur are used in the process, to slightly different effect. Demonic vulcanisation gives the metal a matte finish, while draconic is more chromatic. 

What you have here is some scraps of black metal. Could be demonic, could be draconic. They're likely off-cuts from the smithing or vulcanisation process, or it's entirely possible that they were bashed off a Black Knight's armour when you boffed them. Regardless, waste-not, want-not. Now you have the material required for some new gear.
keysToEntries.CursedLeather:
You may wonder where the 'cursed' moniker comes from. There is no need to worry: you're not cursed by wearing it, and you're not going to be visited by the three dragons of past, present and future in the night for making it. It has a wildly different etymology. 

The 'cursed' is a tannery thing, you see. Cursed leather is notoriously hard to work and shape. Only the foremost tanners can manipulate it. But even they have a hard time, often exclaiming "cursed leather!" at the top of their lungs.
keysToEntries.DemonicOil:
Combine corrupt bonemeal and vile ashes and the result is demonic oil. We should be specific about what kind of oil this is: please do no pour it into a frying pan or use it on a squeaky hinge. This is potent oil, as the 'demonic' prefix will attest. It is used for crafting, and solely for crafting.

Now you've got us wondering what would happen if you did actually add it to a frying pan or a squeaky hinge. Obviously, please don't do it. But if you do happen to do it, through an accident or momentary lack of wisdom, could you let us know what happens?
keysToEntries.GroundLimestone:
Clearly, all limestone is ground limestone, as it comes from the ground. In many places it IS the ground. But this is specifically limestone that has been ground down into a dust. 

Ground limestone's primary use is in glassblowing. Should you have the crafting capability and a healthy, strong puff, you can use the ground limestone to make molten glass and then glass-produce.
keysToEntries.GlassVessel:
The glass vessel. The emblem of potential. It could find itself filled with anything. Water, demonic oil, maple syrup. It could smash before it ever sees use. It could languish in someone's inventory, forgotten for months. In many ways, we are all glass vessels. 

Check back in your journal for other delightful moments of poignancy and philosophy. You may find your life unutterably changed by the wisdom there.
keysToEntries.MapleSyrup:
We are yet to find something that maple syrup does not elevate. Pancakes and bacon are the obvious ones, but try it out on things that are more left-field. Rat meat? Elevated. Terrorbird meat? Elevated. Undead eel? Eelevated. 

So, thank you, humble maple tree. You are a true wonder of the culinary and apothecary world. It won't stop us woodcutting you, but know that each cut comes with a maple syrup-sized dollop of guilt.
keysToEntries.BlueDragonDust:
Blue dragon dust is one of those things where you wonder who invented it, and what curious sequence of events led to it happening. Some unknown person had the wherewithal to strip a dragon of dragon scales, crush those scales in a pestle and mortar, then apply the resulting dust to a potion. Not only that, they downed said potion and stood in the path of a dragon. Does that not seem inexorably mad to you? 

But thank you, inexorably mad person, because now we have this potent ingredient which can protect a person from dragonfire. You have been forgotten in time, but your blue dusty legacy lives on.
keysToEntries.ZamorakWarriorPack: A bag of personal effects carried by the warrior class of Dowdun Reach. Rummage around inside: you've earned it.
keysToEntries.ZamorakMagePack: The knapsack of a mage within Dowdun Reach. It contains all the materials that the mage would have needed for a day's work. Now they are yours.
keysToEntries.LesserAshes: Amid the sulphurous pile are some items. Don't be scared: reach a hand in and claim what's yours.
keysToEntries.SmallAshes: The magnificent bounty of a demonic kill. Want to know what an infernal creature carries around with them on their day-to-day? Look no further!
keysToEntries.MapleTree: A tree full of bounties, whether it's maple logs for crafting needs or maple syrup for cooking. The maple tree knows how much you need its wares and it's keeping a ledger. You are in debt to the maple tree, and the maple will come to collect.
keysToEntries.CoarseNet:
Pity the poor shrimp or lobster, approaching the final moments of its life, only to find themselves in a coarse net. The snobbish among them might look down their antennae and think 'couldn't it have been a wool net, perhaps, or linen?'. 

We're inclined to agree with them. They have minutes before they get cooked in a pot, yet they don't receive the simple courtesy of a comfy net. When you're on your deathbed, we'll wrap you up in a coarse net and see how you like it.
keysToEntries.WoolNet:
Wool is absolutely rubbish in water. Throw a sheep in a lake if you don't believe us. The water soaks into the wool and turns the poor animal into a sad, soggy anchor.

That rubbishness is - conversely - rather useful when it comes to nets. The wool clods up, leaving barely any holes for a shrimp to make a getaway. It's also heavy as sin. The marine life is thoroughly pinned. 

Now, getting the water out of the net: that's another matter entirely. You might do well to leave it out to dry in the Umbral Sands.
keysToEntries.LinenNet:
Oooh, look at you, making a fine linen net. Lah-di-dah. 

I bet you're the kind to make a linen net and never use it. You just drape it on your side-table at dinner parties, so people can compliment it and ask where you fish. "Oh you know, here and there", you say, idly gesturing to a mounted bass on your wall (bought from a shop). But we see you, fisher-dandy. We see you very well.
keysToEntries.MysticNet:
Brassica on a Bullock, that net is garish. You're not going to lose that, are you? I bet that your eyes lock onto it every time you open your inventory. First thing you see. Boom - MYSTIC NET.

It has the same effect on marine life, of course. They're merrily swimming about, doing whatever lobsters and shrimp do, and then boom - MYSTIC NET. Not much they can do, is there? They're transfixed, drawn in by the brightest of blue colours, like moths to a mirrorball. 

Which explains why the mystic net is rather formidable as a net. Not the most fashionable, mind.
keysToEntries.AshRod:
Ah, the ash rod. It's been here from the First Age, and will be here for many Ages to come. Ever-reliable, and gets the job done. Line up all the tools in the world and the ash rod will likely be named the most productive and certainly the most loved. 

All hail rod ash!
keysToEntries.OakRod:
Oak rod is much like its anagram: it's an A-OK rod. Not brilliant, not terrible.

It's a rod that you will you look back on fondly when you wield the mighty maple rod. "Ah, I remember when I used to use an oak rod", you will say, as you bite the head off a live catfish and stand astride a pile of infernal eels. 

For now, appreciate your time with it. You will miss it once you have left it behind.
keysToEntries.WillowRod:
The weeping willow is a sallow tree, known for its love of water. Find a brook or ford and a willow will be there, lost in its reflection. 

That familiarity with water makes willow a strong wood for fishing rods. There's the merest wisp of magic running through it, an affinity for water. The fish recognise that affinity and relax. They circle until they're caught, which is pretty handy for an angler.
keysToEntries.MapleRod:
This is it. You've made it now. The pinnacle of Dowdun Reach fishing: ownership of a maple rod. Don't let it go to your head. The hook will get stuck in your nostril. 

The humble angler doesn't draw attention to the maple rod. They don't wax it or buy it a nice kebbit-fur sheath. They don't name it something like 'Deborah', or kiss it before they go to bed.

No, the humble angler strides into the middle of other anglers and says "levels?". They listen to the fishing levels of others and nods with appreciation. 

Be the humble angler.
keysToEntries.MithrilPickaxe:
A sign of mining distinction, the mithril pickaxe will get you noticed if you hang out with the right mining crowds. Doric in particularly will give you an appreciative wink. He might even toss you a rock cake or two. 

The mithril pickaxe is cold to the touch, incredibly reflective, and - most importantly - strong as an ox. You will no doubt have already realised that it makes short work of any deposit you find.
keysToEntries.MithrilLoggingAxe:
It hurts Doric a little that you've used his prized mithril to make something that chops wood, but don't worry - he can be something of a snob. Dionysius often reminds him that he uses wood in his minecarts, pickaxe handles and toothpicks. 

Take some time to enjoy your increased capacity for woodcutting. There is a simple joy in watching your own increased efficiency.
keysToEntries.MithrilDagger:
Doric likes to remind Vannaka that you wouldn't find a dwarf making daggers.

As Doric says, "it's a very human thing, a dagger. A dwarf never hides their weapon: we're polite enough to show what you're up against. An' you won't find us attacking from the back or sides. No sir, we bonk you from the front."
keysToEntries.MithrilSword:
Mithril may not be the strongest metal on Gielinor, but it's perhaps the most beguiling. It's the metal that benefits most from looking at it up close. Each sword has unique imperfections.

It's a quality that makes it the most popular choice for ceremonial swords. Go back in time and watch a knighting ceremony in Dowdun Reach, or a bestowal of honours in Bramblemead. You will see your share of mithril swords.
keysToEntries.MithrilGreatsword:
"You see a mithril greatsword, I see a mithril dagger," Vannaka would say. He would then pick two up from Doric's forge, one in each hand, and try not to register effort. It was a routine, a little play, between Vannaka and Doric. 

This time, however, the joke was on Vannaka. Doric had made his mam's rock cakes, and folded some mithril round them - one for each hilt. 

Vannaka wobbled. "Cripes," he said.
keysToEntries.MithrilMace:
The standard term is mace, but Vannaka prefers bludgeon. "It's one of those sound words, like stomp and grunt, right? When I hit someone with it, it bludges. Bludge, bludge, bludge."

"You're thinking of onomatopoeia," Dionysius said, helpfully. 

"Nah, but good try. I'm thinking of breakfast," said Vannaka.
keysToEntries.MithrilGreataxe:
In the early years of Dowdun Reach, the concept of a mithril greataxe would have been unthinkable. A big, showy weapon that used large quantities of mithril would have been seen as a betrayal. The Children of Shadows, holding a monopoly on mithril, wanted to present it as a rare metal. At best, the richest on Ashenfall might have a brooch or tiara that sparkled with mithril. 

Nowadays, Doric can have two greataxes whipped up for you before dinner. Much has changed.
keysToEntries.MithrilScimitar:
The scimitar form is not commonplace on Ashenfall. Unlike the mainland (where you can't walk five metres in the Kharidian Desert without getting slashed by one), the scimitar is a rarity. Only an offshoot of the Garou, found in some of the harshest deserts of Ashenfall, are known to wield it. 

That is likely to change, of course, as more adventurers make landfall. It makes you wonder what the island will look like in five, ten years' time as the cultures intertwine.
keysToEntries.MithrilWarhammer:
The mithril warhammer was once the weapon most associated with Lord Rasmodel of Dowdun Reach. He was rarely seen without it. In the mornings, when he was most in pain, it would be a prop, something to lean on while the night's watch recounted the events of the early hours. In the afternoons, it was a beautiful warning, a threat of what might happen if you disobeyed him.

Lord Rasmodel wasn't one for naming his weapons. He found the custom a little juvenile. But when his thoughts were elsewhere, you might see him pat the head of the warhammer. There was kinship there, even if he wouldn't admit it.
keysToEntries.MithrilShield:
Doric insists that you should be able to see your face in a mithril shield. That's the mark of quality: if you can check for rock cakes in your teeth before battle, then you've made a good one. 

There's at least a little science behind that. The more tempered mithril is, the more it reflects. Doric isn't being a narcissist here: he knows what he is doing.
keysToEntries.MapleStaff:
The Children of Shadows were never a grateful sort, but they would acknowledge that the groves of maple in Dowdun Reach were a boon. Almost everything wooden in the keep - from the library bookcases to the bannisters - was made from maple. 

Alric relished walking among the maple trees. For so much of his life, he had been encased in stone and surrounded by essence: working long hours in the keep. But the maples were for thinking. 

In his twilight years, Alric carried a maple staff with him: the Subjugation Staff. It was a crutch for an old man, yes, but it was also a way to bring the grove with him. When he couldn't muster the energy to visit his favoured trees, he carried a remnant with him.
keysToEntries.MapleShortbow:
Putrilius, the cthonian demon, will tell you that any living thing can be possessed. If needs must, a cthonian can be a cockroach or spider, for a time. 

In one desperate moment, after the Dreaming Stone was shattered, Putrilius was forced to possess a maple tree. It wasn't an experience she enjoyed. Most curiously, she found themselves aligning to the maple's inner cadence. Everything slowed. Putrilius barely registered a thought in two years.

What woke Putrilius was a logging axe. Their maple body was toppled and then fletched into an armature. For a moment, as Putrilius's life faded from the wood, she was a shortbow. 

It was the closest she had come to dying. With a cognitive spark, she moved from the bow to a passing hound before all her lifeforce ebbed away.
keysToEntries.MapleLongbow:
A supple wood, is the maple. When cut into long, reed-like lengths, it can be formed into a worthy longbow. The process also releases syrup - a treat for a day's fletching. Not enough to make a maple toastie, mind, but enough to leave a sweet taste in the mouth.

Bramblemead was often the venue for archery competitions, and the maple longbows would be out in force. Etched into the trophy, you would find many Rasmodels. They were a noble bloodline on Ashenfall, and would often add to their reputation by earning combat trophies.
keysToEntries.MithrilCrossbow:
A mithril crossbow is the standard issue for any modern Black Knight. If you showed capability, then you might be promoted to Black Knight Sniper, and that came with the Black Sniper Crossbow. But for the average Black Knight, the crossbow was a long-kept friend. 

The crossbow's main purpose was to stop demons in their tracks. The Zamorakian magi claimed to keep their demon hordes under control, but demons tested that claim.

The crossbow had other uses. Remove the bolt, rest the crossbow on the floor stock-upwards, and you had a stool. Sit on the floor and it was a headrest. 

You could also waste time shooting ravens. Shooting the red, glowing ravens was an immediate dismissal, however. Alric demanded they were protected, for reasons that he never expanded on.
keysToEntries.MithrilCrossbowLimbs:
What do you call a crossbow without limbs? A REALLY cross bow. 

Oh shush, you. That joke plays well at fletching conventions.
keysToEntries.EnchantedMithrilCrossbowBolts: Goodness, you must really want to kill someone. As if punching through their armour with a crossbow bolt wasn't enough, you want to magically scupper them too. Remind me not to get on your bad side.
keysToEntries.BlackSword:
Where the mithril crossbow is the standard issue of the ranged Black Knight, the black sword is standard for footsoldiers. 

'Black', you may be aware, is not a material. It is instead a descriptor for steel that has gone through a process similar to vulcanisation. There was a time when the vulcanisation was performed by the dragon Xikotal, as a gift to the isolationist knights.

With his death, the process moved towards the demonic. The resulting quality is lower, which has made the original, dragonfire 'black' metal sought after in Dowdun Reach.
keysToEntries.BlackSniperCrossbow:
Climb the turrets of Dowdun Reach in its prime, and you would have found the Black Knight Snipers. These were the elite marksmen of the Black Knights, tasked with looking outward when the rest of the order would look inward. They would scan the skyline for threat - for the beating wings of a dragon. There was no greater trophy for the Black Snipers.

One one occasion, a bolt was loosed at Imaru. She had arrived at the keep for diplomatic purposes, and was rewarded with a bolt to the neck. You could see the conflict within her, as her urges were swallowed. For a second, a disdainful eye moved to the Black Sniper. But she reset and continued to talk to Lord Rasmodel and Alric like nothing happened.

Restraint was certainly beaten into the Black Snipers that night.
keysToEntries.SubjugationStaff:
Anyone who worked with Alric knew about the Subjugation Staff. It would thud, thud, thud on the cobblestone floor as he approached. It was a welcome warning that Alric was arriving, but it came with notes of dread. 

The power of the Subjugation Staff came from what was rolled up and inserted into its middle. It was a demon pact, but one that had been defaulted on. A demon, not a human, had failed to keep their side of the bargain, and that gave the contract a radiating power. Alric captured that power within the staff. While he held it, it was as if all of demonkind owed him something.
keysToEntries.UnholySymbol: For those who wish to keep Zamorak close to their heart, we give you the unholy symbol. To complete the look, we recommend a black top, eyeliner, sticking your tongue out and making Zamorak horns with your hands.
keysToEntries.BrokenTitansSword: Take Lord Rasmodel's broken sword. It's a suitable remnant of him: robust and true to his beliefs, but shattered into pieces. You may even find means of remaking it.
keysToEntries.TitansWrath:
Lord Rasmodel has been put to rest at last.

He rarely knew quiet. His family pushed and cajoled him to further the Rasmodel name. Then he was beset by nightmares, as two malevolent beings grappled over his mind. When the Dreaming Stone shattered, they all took him. He was their spoils. 

We can only hope that, when death came, there was quietus. He felt it for a moment as the Dreaming Stone fell, and - perhaps - he feels it on the journey over the Noumenon. 

The part of him that was a titan - that part remains. You hold it in your hands. Unleash its wrath.
keysToEntries.ZamorakSword:
There are myths and anecdotes about Zamorak appearing to the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach. Few truly believe them. Zamorak is not one for hiding his presence: he's more theatrical than that.

But while the Children of Shadows do not believe that Zamorak has been to Dowdun, they still cling to a hope that he will come. That hope is the only explanation for why the Zamorak Sword exists.

The Zamorak Sword, made of finest dragon-vulcanised black metal, was reserved for Zamorak. It was never used, not once. When the chaos god arrived in the keep, he was to be handed the sword. It was made for him, a token of his glory.

It is yours now. How it is used is entirely down to you. Perhaps you will reserve it for Zamorak too, leaving it in a storage chest until the time of his return. Or perhaps you'll immediately sully it with kebbit blood. The choice is yours.
keysToEntries.ZamorakStaff:
There are myths and anecdotes about Zamorak appearing to the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach. Few truly believe them. Zamorak is not one for hiding his presence: he's more theatrical than that.

It may be hard to believe, but some have tried to 'summon' Zamorak to Dowdun Reach. Call it the hubris of a demon summoner: a student feels they have mastered the art of summoning demons, so believes the next rung on the ladder is god. Never mind that Zamorak has no ounce of demon within him.

Zamorak can be accused of many things, but he does have a sense of humour. These attempts have been greeted with a gift. He has been known to kick a bloodveld through the portal, or another creature he has to hand. It gives the demon summoner a little spot-test.
keysToEntries.MithrilArrows: Mithril is an attractive metal, shimmering and chromatic. If you ever get peppered with mithril arrows, you can look down and admire the constellation on your chest.
keysToEntries.MithrilCrossbowBolts: Demons are hardy creatures. The Black Knights had a habit of calling them 'Rackers', as they'd often require a whole bolt rack to bring down.
keysToEntries.BlackGreataxe: Black metal was most commonly used for armours, crossbows and swords. On occasion, it was used for a greataxe. The reason is not a pleasant one. They were almost exhaustively the tool of an executioner, when the crime merited a public execution. The black metal was a message: you have betrayed the Children of Shadows, and your death will come from the metal of our own.
keysToEntries.ShrimpBait: Fishing bait made from a shrimp. Not terribly exciting, in the grand scheme of things, but still very useful to catching more fish.
keysToEntries.FeatherBait: Some fish are drawn to feathers, something about the colours and the way the water dances around them, it’s almost hypnotic to them.
keysToEntries.AnchovyBait: To attract fish with a taste for saltier prey, there’s little better than a nice anchovy strung up and offered as a sacrifice to those that dwell beneath the waves.
keysToEntries.FeatheredLure: Sometimes a feather alone isn’t enough, there must be flavour as well. Delicious, sticky, berry juice is pressed into this lure, so that the taste of it might spread through the water and attract the attention of fish with fruitier palettes.
keysToEntries.LobsterBait: Some fish can only be attracted with a very expensive meal. Kind of like trying to date someone far out of your league. What better way to woo those with more refined palettes than a delicious lobster dinner?
keysToEntries.SpookyBait: The ghostly remnants of ectoplasm can be strangely appealing to certain fish. Perhaps it is the siren call of the rivers of the underworld, calling all life to it’s ultimate destination. Perhaps it’s some sort of ancestral grief urging the fish to reconnect with those they have lost. Perhaps it’s simply that they find the thing pretty. Who can really say?
keysToEntries.KrillBait: The vile ashes imbedded within these strange fishing lures, whisper to the most sinful part of the fish, drawing them to their demise. At least that’s what the Zamorakian mages claimed. In truth, they were simply tastier and more desirable to the fish, who have little concept of morality.
keysToEntries.AnimatedFeather:
When sparkly feathers coated in delicious juice are not enough, then create one that dances. The animated feather is infused with magic and when it reaches the water it writhes and wriggles in a hypnotic dance that fish feel mystically drawn to.

If you hang one from a window, you may also find other weak-minded creatures dancing to its mysterious movements. Like Terry. Terry is an idiot.
keysToEntries.Shrimp:
Almost all fishing careers begin with these little critters. Their numbers are practically infinite, so the universe responds by gifting them with the survival instinct of an inebriated moth approaching the most magnificent flame.

Many a new fisherman has found their diet consisting almost exclusively of these crunchy little things.
keysToEntries.Sardine:
There is a popular game among children, where they hide themselves around the house and then other children join them once their hiding place has been discovered. It was a game clearly invented by very tired parents, who wanted to make sure their kids were well behaved and contained in a well-known location. After all, where else can you fit seventeen children if not for the wardrobe?

Sadly for Gielinor’s fisherfolk, the creature with the namesake of said children’s game, do not possess the same desire to make their captor’s life easier. Fortunately, however, they are numerous enough that one need not worry themselves too much about the difficulty of catching them.
keysToEntries.Trout:
There is little exciting one can say about trout, they have few advocates and fewer fans. Yet, they do taste delicious with a little lemon and a few herbs, so perhaps we should thank them for not being awful.

One trout was awful. A villain. A monster who rose from the depths to bring terror to us all.

Nah, just kidding – they’re trout… I got nothing.
keysToEntries.Anchovies: Surprisingly good on pizza, these salty little fishes are a popular delicacy across Gielinor.
keysToEntries.Herring:
It is said that if you can cut down the tallest tree in the forest, with one of these, then you can gain power and riches beyond your wildest imagination. Still, others claim that only scarlet versions of this fish can cause such a feat, and so the debate has raged for centuries.

Truth be told, these fish are not particularly prized by lumberjacks anywhere.
keysToEntries.Salmon: The life of a salmon is one of great journey and strife. They swim back over great distances, leaping upstream in search of a cementing their legacy. It is tragic then, perhaps, that such journeys become the happy lunchtimes of bears and other animals.
keysToEntries.Lobster:
Do not be fooled by the traders on the street who preach of rare black lobsters worth unparalleled wealth. These are lies. For the rare black lobster is, in truth, a source of great darkness and evil.

Forged from black stone found beneath the waters of the Cursed Archipelago, the rare black lobster whispers terrible, terrible secrets to its bearer. It urges them towards darker and darker acts, until nothing of the host is left except an overwhelming urge toward evil.

Seek thee not the rare black lobster, for it will bring nought but despair!
keysToEntries.UndeadEel: There is something deeply disturbing about a fish that remains to die. More disturbing still are the eels that slither and writhe and wriggle beneath the waters, their hearts no longer beating and their eyes filled with the milky-white film of rot and decay.
keysToEntries.UndeadBass: Even in death there are some fisherfolk who spend their quiet moments hooking these creatures. They wait, rictus still, in patience and draw these wretched things from beneath the waves only to find their prey as dead and decayed as they are, and cast them back into the waters.
keysToEntries.GiantKrill:
Krill are tiny, harmless, little crustaceans, whose lives amount to little more than mass consumption by larger beasts. Yet, here on Ashenfall, the krill refuse to be the victim. Consuming anima they grow to unreasonable size, hoping to fight off any wood-be predators.

They are rarely successful.
keysToEntries.InfernalEel:
In truth this is not a demonic fish, but rather the result of strange magic and infernal energies. The eel is naturally a magically attuned fish, some generate small thunderstorms within their flesh, others blend with shadows becoming almost entirely invisible. These eels, infused as they were with the abundant anima of Ashenfall, became changed by the energies of Downdun Reach’s demonic rites.

Fortunately, the transformation, while a little disconcerting, only really serves to give them a slightly spicy aftertaste.
keysToEntries.ArmouredCatfish:
Sometimes evolution gets inspired by the wrong things. The catfish of Ashenfall have long been the victim of any creature that chooses to make them lunch. They are caught, cooked and consumed more often than is reasonable, and their suffering seemed too great as a result. So it was that a god took pity on them and gave them a defence.

It is uncertain which god, for none have claimed responsibility, but the divine whimsy could be the result of any of them. All we know is that the fish now have a chance against their assailants, an armoured hide to ward off some of the worst of their misery.

Sadly, they are no match for you.
keysToEntries.RawShrimp: There are many ways to cook shrimp. Countless recipes that promise wonderful taste sensations. Right now, though, I would suggest you don’t really want to eat them. They’re all wriggly.
keysToEntries.RawSardine: Sardines travel in large shoals; their lives spend as part of a grand community. There’s something a little tragic seeing one here on its own. Still, with a little heat, perhaps a sprinkle of salt and other seasonings, and you’ve got yourself a nice snack.
keysToEntries.RawTrout: Bonier than some of the other popular fishes, the trout is nevertheless one of the most regularly consumed freshwater fishes. Its flavour is heavily modified by its diet…which, on Ashenfall, is more than a little worrying if I’m honest.
keysToEntries.RawAnchovies: You could eat these by the handful if you didn’t mind the crunch. Traditionally cured in brine and turned into a deliciously salty snack. Goes very well on pizzas.
keysToEntries.RawHerring: A bit too floppy to be useful for lumberjacks.
keysToEntries.RawSalmon: The Wushanko islands are masters of turning this into a delicious and satisfying meal. Alas, you never did take the time to study their culinary ways and must apply heat to this meal, lest your eject your internalised failures upon Ashenfall’s soil.
keysToEntries.RawLobster: Although some people insist that to get the best flavour a lobster must be alive while it is boiled, you, fortunately for the lobster, have already put it out of its misery. Now you can look forward to boiling it guilt-free for a much gentler flavour.
keysToEntries.RawUndeadEel:
Sometimes you have to ask yourself why you cook everything you find. Is this really going to make a succulent meal? Even dead it twitches occasionally, animated by some remnant of necromantic energy. Its flesh is partially decayed and the smell… oh the smell…

I recommend cooking it very, very thoroughly.
keysToEntries.RawUndeadbass:
In life, these are gamy fish, sleek of form and delicious with a bit of lemon. In unlife… well… they’re a little less appetising and lemons seem surprisingly sparse on Ashenfall.

Still, needs must.
keysToEntries.RawGiantKrill:
To its krill kin, this creature is a beast of epic proportions. The sort of monster of legend that would tower over mountains and shatter towers with a single swipe of its enormous claws.

Here, though, it’s a bit like a big shrimp. Much less intimidating.
keysToEntries.RawInfernalEel: Possessed of a strangely smoky flavour, with a surprisingly spicy kick. Best cooked very carefully, preferably in the presence of a priest.
keysToEntries.RawArmouredCatfish: Looking at this, you’re going to struggle to prepare it properly. Make sure you have a very, very sharp knife.
keysToEntries.UlvsLongbow:
Ulv was always the silent hunter, in more ways than one. While it was true that no word ever left his lips, his footsteps, breath and, most importantly, his arrows, were all equally devoid of sound. The Wild Knights would jokingly call him the Silent Killer – though over time the crueler members of the order called him the Silent But Deadly, knowing he lacked the capability to argue back. The boy took these insults with quiet grace, never biting back, never reacting. He was used to those little cruelties, having faced them all his life. 

He had carved the bow himself and once walked Ravanna through the process. The two of them were as true a friendship as could ever be hoped for. No lingering romantic tensions. No transaction or debt. No honour chained them together. They were friends simply because they were. Because two lonely people found each other and found a comfort in each others eccentricities. The wolf and the bat, platonic soulmates to the end. 

For one of his birthdays, Ravanna enchanted the bow with a simple spell. She took animal bones, skulls and hides and wove necromantic magic through them, wrapping them around the limbs and grip of the bow, imbuing them with just enough of the underworld to strip them of their sound and a little of their substance. Like ghosts, the arrows would sail silently through the air, dropping targets before they ever knew what hit them. 

It would have been the perfect birthday gift. If only Ulv had lived long enough to receive it. 

The garou can be brutal in their assaults, considering notions like mercy to be the domain of the weak-willed, or the cowardly. Ulv was simply in the wrong place, at the wrong time, and was ambushed by a patrol of young garou, looking for the bloodmark that would grant them adulthood. There were six of them in total, but Ulv reduced them down to four, before the claws entered his throat and silenced him forever. 

When Ravanna found his body, she went mad. An anger unlike any other, a rage more powerful than any storm, waged a war inside her between the light and the dark. The darkness won. Using the souls of slain garou, Ravanna's necromantic curse tore through the Highlands, twisting the clouds into screaming things, filled with a fraction of the rage she felt. As the ghosts of the dead garou crashed about within them, the clouds unleashed lightning and thunder upon the populace below, destroying countless settlements in an instant and countless more over the centuries that followed.
keysToEntries.AbraxusRing:
Little is known about the great necromancer, Abraxus, a fact he finds deeply frustrating. In his youth he sought secrets forbidden to others and he wandered the Whispering Swamps of Brynmoor in search of secrets that others dared not know. The swamp was ancient and haunted, so the legends went, and there was a magic to it that was different to any other part of Ashenfall. He explored vaults, caves and battled goblins in the mists, but never found the power that his youth demanded. Until one day, he found the smoke that promised him the power he so desperately craved. 

From there his history is clouded, obscured just as the mist obscured the swamps. He founded a small order of necromancers in Fellhollow and took advantage of the chaos when the Withering began. He sought to bind the souls of the dead into his service, gaining power just as Imaru gained hers from the consumption of the fallen. 

But Abraxus was no dragon, and so frustratingly mortal. A single hoplite spear is all it took to end his life, he would have been embarrassed if he felt it had mattered. As a spirit he lost a lot of things we take for granted, sensation, corporeality, and many of the emotions that everyone else enjoys. Yet, in exchange, he was given the most precious gift of all – time. Time to contemplate. Time to scheme. Time to think about what really matters, in the grand scheme of it all, and he has found his answer. 

His ring, imbued with his dark power, offers no value to him any more. So he bequeaths it to you. In time, perhaps, it may help you become a mage strong enough to earn his respect. Well, a small fraction of it, at least.
keysToEntries.FireMithrilArrows:
The Fogger's Gala was one of the few dates that a Black Knight could look forward to. An annual tradition, it would take place on the mistiest day of the year. As soon as low clouds started rolling in, word spread among the knights that it was Gala day.

It was an informal event on the battlements of the keep. There were quivers that represented each of the night's events, and the knights would name their arrows and place them into the quivers of those they wanted to participate in. Arrows were drawn randomly to determine the order. 

Sergeant Blister was on hand with betting. He brought his blackboard, which he seemed to have on-hand when needed, and a week's wages were placed on friends and adversaries. 

The events all revolved around bows and crossbows. The fog made it almost impossible to see anything, so the Gala was a test of both memory and accuracy. One event required the archer to hit hay bales with fire arrows. The hay bales were invisible, so they only had location names to go by - the hut by the waterfall, Skrexis's bed - which meant firing Hail Mary's in the vague direction of their quarry, hoping that it would ignite in the night.

Other events included Pin the Arrow on the Goblin (as you can probably imagine, a noisy goblin ran about in the fog), Farthest Arrow and Alric's Curtains.
keysToEntries.PoisonedMithrilArrows:
The Rasmodels were notorious for their use of poison. When they lived to the south, in Temple Woods and Bramblemead, it was often said that you should cover your cup, mouth and ears around a Rasmodel. One of the family was finally caught red-handed (or green-handed, considering the poison used), and the rumour moved to certainty. They were banished to Dowdun. 

The reputation followed them, and mostly became a gift rather than curse. No-one in Dowdun Reach wanted to cross the Rasmodels for fear of waking up in spasms. 

We say that it was 'mostly' a gift, because of an event between a young Lord Rasmodel and his cousin. Lord Rasmodel claimed that he could shoot an apple from the top of his cousin's head, and promptly did so. He handed one of the resulting apple-halves to his cousin who ate it, and promptly keeled over and died.

It turned out that the arrow used to the cleave the apple was poisoned. Lord Rasmodel claimed that he had been using his father's hunting arrows, and one of them must have been tainted. It seemed entirely plausible.

But there was still doubt. And that doubt grew into a reputation: a reputation that outmatched his own family's. Even at the age of eight, the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach gave Lord Rasmodel a wide berth.
keysToEntries.BarbedMithrilArrows:
Demons dealt with wounds and ailments on their own. There was no such thing as a demon surgeon or sick bay. If wounds were fatal then the demon would die; if wounds were survivable, then survive they did. 

This was an observation of Summoner Flament, who passed it on to Alric, who idly mentioned it to Lord Rasmodel in one of their weekly meetings. As with most information, Lord Rasmodel examined it for opportunities.

If demons sought no help from their wounds - no remedies, no medical assistance - then his knights would carry weapons that took advantage of that fact. Arrows were poisoned, since no demon carried antipoison. And arrows were barbed, as demons would pull them roughly from their hides, rather than remove them carefully. 

It was a small advantage, but Lord Rasmodel took it. As he would often say, "stack opportunities, one on top of the other, and soon you will have an impregnable tower".
keysToEntries.SalvagePile: Detritus is a wonderful word. 'Detritus'. It even sounds dirty and worthless. But rummage around in it if you will: there may be something worth salvaging.
keysToEntries.DraconicLeatherScraps: Little scraps of hide, snout, claw and fur. Most people would toss it away in disgust, but you - you stick it in your pockets for a rainy day.
keysToEntries.BlackCrossbowLimbs: Did you know that someone who fires a crossbow is often considered an 'arbalister' or 'arbalist'? Don't say that these journals don't teach you something.
keysToEntries.MapleInfusion: The distilled strength of a maple tree, pumped straight to your dietary tract. You might fell a rising fury whenever you stare at a logging axe, however.
keysToEntries.GlassPane: When you take me out of a window, you leave a grieving wife. When you stick me in a door, you will save somebody’s life. What am I?
keysToEntries.GlassDecoration:
Let's take a moment to recognise your glass-blowing skills. 

...

Congratulations on your craftmanship. You have the puff of an angel.
keysToEntries.BlackKnightElite:
Being an Elite Black Knight wasn't just an acknowledgment of fortitude. Lord Rasmodel used them for tasks where conscience was a burden. 

They would be called upon for public executions, where a message needed to be sent. They were always on-call for demon outbreaks. Latterly, they would deal with extreme epidemics of the madness. It was all too common for the voice-infected to move together in a chorus, a large group whose aim was to kill indiscriminately. Elites would quickly put a stop to them.
keysToEntries.LesserDemonShardbound:
When the Dreaming Stone shattered, the aftershocks weren't felt just by humans. Demons were affected too. If anything, their reactions were more unpredictable. 

For some, it was like a connection snapping - a hard reset. It was as if a controlling force had been removed from them; a marionette falling to the ground. 

For others, it was a fury. So much ash was tossed to the wind. A subset of that group gathered the shards for themselves, and they fought others for their stockpiles. They were the shardbound, and no amount of shards would settle them.
keysToEntries.StandardAntipoisonPotion: Just because you're protected by antipoison, it doesn't mean that you should start glugging poison or poking yourself with poison-tipped arrows. Your body still needs to get rid of that poison, and it's not pleasant on the exit.
keysToEntries.StandardFarmingPotion:
A common misconception is that a farming potion is poured onto your crops, rather than into your mouth. While it might sound sensible - it's the crops that are growing, not you - it fundamentally misunderstands what the farming potion does.

The farming potion increases the experience you receive when you farm. It achieves this by making you sensitive to the wants and needs of your plants. You become intensely aware of them. You will know when even a single aphid lands on a leaf; when the last drop has been siphoned from the soil; when a potato wants to hear you sing. You are attuned to your crops, to the point that you might even find kinship with them. If you begin sleeping in your cabbage patch, you should consider lowering your consumption.
keysToEntries.StandardFishingPotion:
You often hear stories about great anglers who have that one marine animal they've never caught. A whale perhaps, or a darting salmon. Well, I had a friend once who had an enmity with a prawn. It was a big prawn, mind you, and every time he caught it, it would leap out of his net and get away. He accused that pawn of gloating: he believed it waited until he hauled in the net before scarpering.

It got to the point where my friend traded his week's wages for a fishing potion. Not a lesser or weak fishing potion: a third-tier, freshly made fishing potion. He was going to catch that prawn, even if he had to starve for days. 

But the potion didn't make him any better at catching the prawn. Instead, he became attuned to the prawn. He was so aware that he knew what it was thinking. Now, every time the prawn escapes, my friend can hear it cackle and let out a little "doofus!".
keysToEntries.ShroudOfNoggin:
Poor old Noggin found no peace in death, because even the gods forgot him. When he reached the River Noumenon, there was no guide to usher him through to the Afterlife. No radiant light to beckon him forward into the loving arms of those he'd lost. instead, there was silence and it went on and on. 

Always quiet and forgettable, the universe simply failed to recall him. His name was recorded in no books of the dead. The various creatures that prey on lost souls, never once thought he seemed like a pleasant meal. Instead they did nothing, except to leave him to his wandering, forgotten and alone. 

Then four people went digging where they shouldn't, and that made Noggin mad. Forgettable as he was, he had always made a point of preserving the status quo and to see these four ruffians blindly digging into the soil that had never done anyone a disservice, why that was unforgivable – he couldn't let it stand. So he crept into their camp, and found the treasure they coveted, and secreted himself within the gold and silver, waiting to be found. Found he was, for when they opened the chest they saw no treasure, no jewels, no trinkets of worth – because Noggin had eaten all of them, swallowed the pieces down into the void – they found only a ghostly sackcloth head glaring back at them, with nothing but hate in its eyes. 

This is that piece of sackcloth, and it's not happy to see you.
keysToEntries.Lurch: Lurch tends to sleep most of the day. It's her metabolism that does the hard work, producing wind that will empty an entire barracks. Considering she will accidentally ignite that flatulence, she's often left to sleep on her own.
keysToEntries.Tiny: As with 90% of pets called Tiny, the 'Tiny' is ironic. She just thinks she's tiny, jumping onto Rexiton's lap when he hasn't even got the flame-retardant sheet ready.
keysToEntries.Rufus: Rufus is deaf, but that doesn't stop him from being the alpha of the group. If anything, it puts him in an admirable position, as he's less bothered by Gnasher's zooms, Lurch's wind, and Tiny's barking.
keysToEntries.LostSoul: A wandering spirit, desperately clawing at any hint of life. Pity them and move on, you cannot help them.
keysToEntries.GhostTerrorBird:
Nature spirits are part of a natural cycle, they are both the hunter and the hunted and death is as important a part of their essence as living is. In death, they rise again, like plants growing from the nutrients of fallen beasts, only to be hunted and slain again and again. 
On paper it’s a tragic existence, but this cycle of life and death is vital for the wellbeing of any ecosystem.
keysToEntries.LesserGreenDragon: Lesser green dragons are weaker than the generals, but that doesn’t make them easy to defeat. Their cruelty is almost as great as Velgar’s, but they fortunately lack the ambition and tactical thinking that would elevate their threat to greater heights.
keysToEntries.SoulstoneSentinel: Once guardians of souls, they have become twisted by Imaru’s fury into something else. They wait behind spectral doors, ready to add more soulstones to their collection, by murdering any living being that intrudes upon their realm.
keysToEntries.UnusualTrout:
The trout is unusual in a few different ways. It's still breathing even out of water, which you could probably classify as more than just 'unusual'. But we like to stay away from hyperbole, so we'll stick with 'unusual' for now.

It also seems to be eyeballing you. Little sideways glances that could be interpreted as judgements. And when you do fail at something - burn a fish, perhaps - it tuts, loudly, like a librarian.

keysToEntries.ui.eos.loginDescription:
This title uses Epic Account Services Basic Profile to connect players across platforms.

By pressing "Continue with Epic Games", you accept Epic Account Services Terms & Conditions. 
The title will then verify whether your platform account is already linked with an Epic Account Services account. If not, the title will create an empty Epic account on your behalf and link it to your Platform account.

You can read more info on Epic Account Services and its Terms & Conditions by pressing "More Info".

Basic Profile access enables the application to authenticate the user with Epic Online Services, access information like the user's screen name, and make in-application purchases. 
However, the application will never have access to the user's password or payment information.

Finally, you could refuse and enjoy the game solo - it's still an amazing experience! And if you change your mind, you can come back to this screen by going to Settings > Legal > Epic Account Services.
keysToEntries.ui.eos.loginDescriptionShort: Epic Online Services link required to play online.

keysToEntries.ui.mainmenu.tooltip.steam: Visit our Dedicated Server's Steam page. From there, you can freely download the dedicated server binaries to host those yourself, at home or in the cloud. For the more technically minded players.
keysToEntries.ui.mainmenu.tooltip.rental: Host your game with Shockbyte, our preferred hosting partner. In just a few click, you can easily and securely rent a server to play with all your friends.

keysToEntries.ComingSoonMessageBody: You will soon be able to browse and play on numerous community driven servers
keysToEntries.ui.mainmenus.landing.newsfeed.Body:
We're delighted to have you on this journey with us. Pardon our dust: the project is in early access, so you may encounter bugs or issues during your time in-game. Balancing and content is not yet final.

We want to hear from you! We’ve got a range of social channels, including our Discord where we’ll be using community feedback to inform the future roadmap of the game. We’re always on the lookout for feedback, so stay tuned for more updates! Until then, have fun!

– The RuneScape: Dragonwilds Team

keysToEntries.ui.mainmenus.difficultytype.normal.toolt:
Lack of Hydration, Sustenance & Rest never reduce Health below 1%.
All Skill XP is retained on death.
Inventory items are stored in a Gravestone on death.
keysToEntries.ui.mainmenus.difficultytype.hard.tooltip:
Hydration, Sustenance & Rest decrease faster and can reduce Health to 0%.
Enemy attacks deal more damage.
Enemies have more Health.
Weaknesses & Resistances affects are increased.
Skill XP beyond your current level is lost on death.
Only one gravestone may be active at a time. If you die with a gravestone active the oldest gravestone and all its contents will be lost.
<Warning>Warning!</> Hardcore players require a hardcore server.
Recommended for experienced players.

keysToEntries.ui.mainmenus.modes.Creative.Description: No building costs. All buildings unlocked. No aggressive enemies. No survival upkeep.
keysToEntries.ui.mainmenus.modes.Title: Select a Mode
keysToEntries.ui.mainmenus.modes.Subtext: Survive and Thrive on the mysterious continent of Ashenfall. Defeat the Dragon Queen Kuldra and her Generals. Become Legend.

keysToEntries.ui.playerconsent.paragraph1: You have the option to say yes or no to your data being used for either or both (a) player activity and game diagnostics and/or (b) internet-based advertising. If you say yes, we will automatically collect data through a plug-in developed by the Jagex Group. The collected data is securely stored and analysed on AWS servers in Europe. Your device will be equally secure and operate normally regardless of your decision.
keysToEntries.ui.playerconsent.paragraph2:
1.	The data we collect

We would like to collect the following types of data (‘Player Activity Data’):
keysToEntries.ui.playerconsent.paragraph5: We would like to use this data for the following purposes (‘Purposes’):
keysToEntries.ui.playerconsent.paragraph10: Please choose what information you are comfortable sharing with us. If you do not opt in using the boxes below, that means we will not collect and use the Player Activity Data for these Purposes.

keysToEntries.ui.playerconsent.paragraph12: I consent to the collection and use of Data for Internet-based Advertising.
keysToEntries.ui.playerconsent.paragraph13: I consent to collection and use of Data for both Purposes.
keysToEntries.ui.playerconsent.paragraph14: If you do give us your consent here, you can withdraw your consent at any time by changing your privacy options and preferences in the legal section of the settings menu. The processing of your personal data (including any third parties who we may share the Player Analytics Data with) will be carried out in accordance with our Privacy Policy – copy of which is available on request and also available here:

keysToEntries.CharacterCreate.Hardcore.Title: Hardcore Mode
keysToEntries.CharacterCreate.Hardcore.Description:
Beat Velgar on Hardcore without dying to claim an exclusive reward.

If any player dies, the reward becomes unobtainable for everyone. You must start a new Hardcore server to try again.

Hardcore mode can only be played with a Hardcore character.
